(ii) AS 19, Leases Paragraphs 22 (c) (e) and (f); 25 (a), (b) and (e); 37 (a) and (f); and 46 (b) and (d) relating to disclosures are not applicable to non corporate entities falling in Level II . Paragraphs 22 (c),(e) and (f); 25 (a), (b) and (e); 37 (a), (f) and (g); and 46 (b), (d) and (e) relating to dis- closures are not applicable to Level III entities. (iii) AS 20, Earnings per share Diluted earnings per share (both including and excluding extraordinary items) is not required to be dis- closed by non-corporate entities falling in Level II and Level III and information required by paragraph 48(ii) of AS 20 is not required to be disclosed by Level III entities if this standard is applicable to these entities. (iv) AS 28, Impairment of assets Non-corporate entities falling in Level II and Level III are allowed to measure the ‘value in use’ on the basis of reasonable estimate thereof instead of computing the value in use by present value technique. Conse- quently, if a non-corporate entity falling in Level II or Level III chooses to measure the ‘value in use’ by not using the present value technique, the relevant provisions of AS 28, such as discount rate etc., would not be applicable to such an entity. Further, such an entity need not disclose the information required by paragraph 121(g) of the Standard. (v) AS 29, Provisions, contingent liabilities and contingent assets Paragraphs 66 and 67 relating to disclosures are not applicable to noncorporate entities falling in Level II and Level III. (vi) AS 25, Interim Financial Reporting does not require a non-corporate entity to present interim financial report. It is applicable only if a noncorporate entity is required or elects to prepare and present an interim fi- nancial report. Only certain Level I non-corporate entities are required by the concerned regulators to pres- ent interim financial results, e.g., quarterly financial results required by the SEBI. Therefore, the recognition and measurement requirements contained in this Standard are applicable to those Level I non-corporate entities for preparation of interim financial results. 154 ENHANCEMENT OF AUDIT CAPACITY FOR CCS | AUDIT MANUAL FOR PACS APPENDICES APPENDIX 5 NABARD CIRCULAR ON ASSET CLASSIFICATION

1/12/14 Audit Manual for PACS file:///C:/Users/GEETHA/Desktop/NABARD CAS/Audit Manual for PACS.htm 113/118 Prudential Norms on Asset Classification, Provisioning and Income Recognition for PACS 155 Ref. No. NB.DoS.HO.Pol/ 1512 /P-162/2009-10 Circular No. 112 /Dos- 22 /2009 23 July 2009 The Registrar of Cooperative Societies (25 implementing States) Dear Sir Prudential Norms on Asset Classification, Provisioning and Income Recognition for Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) - Guidelines 1. As you are aware, under the Government of India package for revival of the Short Term Cooperative Credit Structure (STCCS), the Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) have been recapitalised to achieve a minimum capital adequacy ratio of 7%. The recapitalization assistance was assessed applying pru- dential norms for asset classification, provisioning requirement, income recognition and capital adequacy. 2. Hitherto, prudential norms on asset classification, provisioning and income recognition were not made applicable to PACS for finalisation of their annual accounts. However, as a part of the ongoing reforms process as envisaged in the memorandum of understanding executed by the State Government with Gov- ernment of India and NABARD, prudential norms need to be introduced in the PACS. Towards this end, NABARD in consultation with the RBI has prepared the guidelines (copy enclosed) on prudential norms keeping in view the preparedness of PACS,training inputs received and most importantly, to make the fi- nancial statements of PACS present a true and fair picture of their working. The guidelines may be issued to PACS for adoption in the State from 01 April 2009. 3. A separate communication will follow on capital adequacy norms for PACS. A copy of the letter issued to PACS on application of guidelines may be endorsed to our concerned RO. Yours faithfully Sd/- (G. C. Panigrahi) Chief General Manager

1/12/14 Audit Manual for PACS file:///C:/Users/GEETHA/Desktop/NABARD CAS/Audit Manual for PACS.htm 114/118 156 ENHANCEMENT OF AUDIT CAPACITY FOR CCS | AUDIT MANUAL FOR PACS APPENDICES Prudential Norms on Asset Classification, Provisioning and Income Recognition for Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) - Guidelines (1) LOANS AND ADVANCES Loans and advances may be classified as NPA (non-performing asset) where: a. The installment of principal or interest thereon remains overdue for two crop seasons (short duration crops) and one crop season (long duration crops) in the case of agricultural advances, both short term and long term. The purposes for which this norm is applicable is given in the Annexure. b. Interest and/ or installment of principal remain overdue for a period of more than 90 days in respect of non-agricultural loans and loans for allied activities. c. The account remains out of order for a period of more than 90 days, in respect of an overdraft/ cash credit. d. In all other cases, 90 days overdue criteria to be applicable. An account may be treated as ‘out of order’, if the outstanding balance remains continuously in excess of the sanctioned limit/drawing power. In cases, where the outstanding balance in the principal operating ac- count is less than the sanctioned limit/drawing power, but there are no credits continuously for 90 days or credits are not enough to cover the interest debited during the same period, these accounts may be treated as ‘out of order’. In respect of a borrower having more than one facility, all the facilities granted by PACS to the borrower has to be treated as NPA and not the particular facility or part thereof which has become NPA. Wherever due to natural calamities conversion of the short-term production loan into a term loan or re- schedulement of the repayment period is allowed, the converted/rescheduled loan as well as fresh loan granted has to be treated as current dues and need not be classified as NPA. In the case of all other rescheduled loans, the accounts which have been subjected to restructuring etc., whether in respect of principal installment or interest amount, will be eligible to be upgraded to the stan- dard category only after 1 year of satisfactory performance in regard to repayment of installment/ interest. Advances against term deposits, NSCs, IVPs, KVPs and life insurance policies need not be treated as NPAs, but advances against gold and other ornaments to be treated as NPAs based on the period of overdue and purpose for which loan was disbursed i.e. whether for agriculture or non-agriculture. In the case of staff loans, such loans is to be classified as NPA only when there is a default in repayment of installment of principal or payment of interest on the respective due dates. The loans and advances may be classified into 4 categories and provisions may be made there against as indicated below:- Category Particulars Provision Standard Loans which have not become NPA- agricultural & nonagricultural No provision Sub-standard Overdues for a period not exceeding 3 years 5 Doubtful Overdues above 3 years, and upto 4 years- D1 10 Overdues over 4 years but not exceeding 6 years- D2 15 Overdues exceeding 6 years- D3 50 Unsecured advances 100 Loss Identified by the PACS/auditor 100 157 Overdue loans where decrees or execution petitions have been time-barred or documents are lost or no other legal proof is available, where the members and their sureties are declared insolvent or have died leav- ing no tangible assets, where the members have left the area of operation of the society leaving no property and their sureties have also no means to pay the dues, where the loan is fictitious or when gross misutilisa- tion is noticed and amounts which cannot be recovered due to non-availability of securities or where the realisable value of the security is less than 10% of the outstanding in the borrowal account may be treated as loss assets. In the case of loans outstanding under the back-end subsidy schemes, provision may be made only on net of subsidy basis. It may be noted that the entire outstanding in the loan account be treated as NPA, not the defaulted install- ment, in case of term loans. In

members have left the area of operation of the society leaving no property and their sureties have also no means to pay the dues, where the loan is fictitious or when gross misutilisa- tion is noticed and amounts which cannot be recovered due to non-availability of securities or where the realisable value of the security is less than 10% of the outstanding in the borrowal account may be treated as loss assets. In the case of loans outstanding under the back-end subsidy schemes, provision may be made only on net of subsidy basis. It may be noted that the entire outstanding in the loan account be treated as NPA, not the defaulted install- ment, in case of term loans. In the case of assets other than loans and advances, the provisioning norm may be as under:- (2) CASH ON HAND :- The shortage in cash, if any, observed on verification either by the society officials or by the auditor or by any regulatory/ supervisory authority may be provided for. If the society is holding any soiled notes or mutilated notes as on 31 March of the year, provisions may be made for the value of such notes held.

1/12/14 Audit Manual for PACS file:///C:/Users/GEETHA/Desktop/NABARD CAS/Audit Manual for PACS.htm 115/118 (3) BALANCES WITH BANKS :- The General Ledger/ Subsidiary Ledger may be reconciled on a periodical basis with the pass book or certificate/ statement of account issued by the bank. Provision may be made in respect of the entries not reconciled and outstanding for more than one year. (4) INVESTMENTS :- The investments of a PACS is normally in fixed deposits with higher financing agency and other banks and shares in the Central Cooperative Bank and other cooperative institutions. There may be some PACS which are having investments in government and other approved securities or fixed deposits with Post Offices and/or other institutions. In the case of fixed deposit with DCCB and other banks or with Post Office/ Govt Treasury, no provision is required to be made if the society is having the fixed deposit receipts or other documents to show having such deposits. In the case of fixed deposits with other institu- tions, no provision may be made if the interest is received regularly or the institution is functioning prop- erly. If not, the realisability of the amount may be assessed and provision may be made for the unrealisable portion. The value of fixed deposits with institutions which are under liquidation/ winding up procedure may be treated as loss assets and full provision may be made. The value of fixed deposits which are not col- lected/ received within 6 months from the date of maturity can generally be treated as doubtful of recovery and provision may be made to the full extent. In case of shares in cooperatives or other institutions, other than shares of the CCB to which the PACS is affiliated if the income/dividend is being received regularly, no provision needs to be made. In case, not received regularly, i.e. during the previous 3 years, provision may be made against the investment as the difference between the book value and the breakup value. The breakup value may be calculated as the net worth of the institution divided by the total number of shares of the institution multiplied by the number of shares held by the society in the institution. If the financial statements of the cooperative institutions in which shares are held, are not available for the previous 3 years, provision may be made for the full amount. If the institution in which the PACS has invested money is defunct or under liquidation, the entire investment in such institution may be treated as loss assets and full provision may be made. The government and other trustee securities, if held, may be valued at book value. When securities are purchased at a price higher than the face value, the difference between the purchase value and the face value may be depreciated in equal proportion every year over the remaining maturity period of the security. 158 ENHANCEMENT OF AUDIT CAPACITY FOR CCS | AUDIT MANUAL FOR PACS APPENDICES (5) FIXED ASSETS :- Yearly depreciation may be provided to each of the fixed assets till the value of the Fixed Asset reaches Re.1/-. The rate of depreciation may be decided by the society, based on the normal life of the asset, if not prescribed by the State Cooperative Societies’ Act/ Rules or By-law of the society. In the case of land, no depreciation required, if freehold land and in case of leasehold land, amount in proportion to expiry of the lease period may be provided. (6) SUNDRY DEBTORS AND OTHER RECEIVABLES/ SECURITY DEPOSITS WITH STATE GOVT. DEPART- MENTS :- In the case of amount receivable from government and government departments, if the amount is not received within 1 year from the date of claiming, it can naturally be assumed that there are some problems in realisation of the amount. Therefore, for such amount 10% provision may be made if not received within 1 year, 50% provision if not received within 2 years and 100% provision for more than 3 years. In case of security deposits with utility agencies, no provision need be made. In respect of frauds and embezzlements etc., full provision may be made from the date of detection of frauds. In the case of all other amounts

OTHER RECEIVABLES/ SECURITY DEPOSITS WITH STATE GOVT. DEPART- MENTS :- In the case of amount receivable from government and government departments, if the amount is not received within 1 year from the date of claiming, it can naturally be assumed that there are some problems in realisation of the amount. Therefore, for such amount 10% provision may be made if not received within 1 year, 50% provision if not received within 2 years and 100% provision for more than 3 years. In case of security deposits with utility agencies, no provision need be made. In respect of frauds and embezzlements etc., full provision may be made from the date of detection of frauds. In the case of all other amounts to be received /recovered, full provision may be made if not realised within 3 years. (7) NON-CREDIT BUSINESS:- PACS undertake business like sale of fertiliser, PDS items, clothes, etc. As on 31 March every year a physical verification of each of the items may be made and if any shortage of stock, degraded stock and unsaleable stock is found provision may be made. For shortage of stock, full provision may be made. Any item which remains unsold after the expiry period indicated, may be treated as unsal- able and full provision made there against. The items having no expiry period may be treated as unsalable if remaining unsold for more than 3 years and full provision made. In the case of certain items like cloth, the value depreciates by the passage of time and therefore such items may be valued based on age. The cloths in stock upto 1 year may be valued at purchase price, between one year and 2 years may be valued at 80%, between two years and 3 years at 50% and above 3 years at nil and provision for diminution in value may be made accordingly. (8) INCOME RECOGNITION: Most of the State Cooperative Societies’ Acts/ Rules/ Audit Manuals provide for taking unrealised income to P&L A/c and making corresponding matching provision for overdue interest. Keeping this in view, the following recommendations are made: a. Interest accrued/ receivable on loans & advances:- The amount of interest accrued but not actually realised including the overdue interest as on 31 March each year may be taken to P&L A/c. Provision may be made for the interest overdue. In the case of NPA loans, if in the previ- ous years the interest/ part of the interest not actually collected was taken to P&L A/c. without creation of provision, the interest so taken to P&L A/c. may be provided for. b. Interest on investments :- The interest on fixed deposits, government securities etc., which has accrued but not received may be taken to P&L A/C and provision may be made for the overdue interest. In the case of investments like shares etc., the income/dividend amount re- ceivable may not be known. In such cases, the income/ dividend as and when received need to be reckoned. If interest/ income was taken to P&L A/C in the past and if the investment is treated as doubtful/ unrealisable, provision may be made for the income taken to P & L A/C in the previous years. c. Fee, commission, etc :- The other income like fees, commission etc., in respect of the loans etc., may be treated as income only when the account is standard. Further, in the case of fees,

1/12/14 Audit Manual for PACS file:///C:/Users/GEETHA/Desktop/NABARD CAS/Audit Manual for PACS.htm 116/118 commission etc., taken to P&L A/C in the previous years without actual receipt, on non-re- ceipt of the same/the loan becoming NPA, adequate provision may be made for such income. 159 (9) TIME FRAME FOR IMPLEMENTING THE PRUDENTIAL NORMS; The PACS are located at village level spread across the length and breadth of the country. The staff and Board members of PACS need extensive training and time to assimilate the new concepts involved in the prudential norms. Hence, the prudential norms suggested may be made applicable from 1 April 2009. ANNEXURE DIRECT FINANCE TO FARMERS FOR AGRICULTURAL PURPOSES a. 1 వ్యా కర్త సందేశం : చట్టపర్మైన చర్ాల వి షయంలో అటు కార్యా లయాలలోనూ , ఇటు క్షేత్రస్త తయిలోనూ , ఆవశా కతమేర్కు దస్తాలు , నివేది కలు ఏ వి ధంగా ఉండాలో తె లుసుకోవ్యలి. విచార్ ణ , తనిఖీ ల కు ఏ వి ధంగా దస్తాలు తయారు చేసి , ఉతతరుులు ఇవ్యులి అనేది తెలియాలి . విచార్ ణ, తనిఖీ , ప్రత్యా క ఆడిట్ నివేది కలు ఏ వి ధంగా తయారు చేయాలో తెలిసి ఉండాలి. చాలా మటుకు సర్యార్జి , అర్జిట్రేషణ్ లా ంటి వ్యటి వి షయంలో దస్త్రాకర్ణ , విధా న ని రూపణ ను ఏ వి ధంగా చేయాలో తె లుసుకోవ్యలి. చేసి ంది నేర్ మని మనకు తెలిసినా , నేర్ ని రూపణ చట్టబదధంగా ఉననపుడే శిక్ష కు అనుకూలత సిది ధ సుతంది. అటుు కాని యె డల సదరు నేర్ సుతలు శిక్ష నుండి బయట్ పడటానికి అవకాశం ఏర్పడుతంది. నాా యపర్మై న విధివిధానా లను పాటి ంచుట్లో అసంపూర్జత ని ండడం చే త , కార్యా చర్ణ లోని స్త ంకేతిక హే తవులతో కక్షిదారులు నాాయా లయాలలో సహకార్ శా ఖ తీ సుకంటునన చర్ాల పై ప్రతి కూలంగా ఉతతరుులు పందుతనానరు. లేదా స్ట ట ఉతతరుుల దా ుర్య శిక్ష ను ఖర్యరు చేస్ట చర్ాలను జా పాం చే సుతనానరు. లేదా పునర్జుచార్ణ పే రుతో కా లదోషానికి తెర్ తీ సుతనానరు. చేసి నపని మళ్ళీ మళ్ళీ చే యవలసి వస్తంది. వీట్ని నటినీ కంతమేర్ ని లువర్జంచడానికి , సహకార్ శా ఖలో పని చేస్ట అదృషటం నా కు లభంచి , తదాుర్య ఒకింత విధి నిర్ ుహణ అనుభవ్యనిన ప్రోదిచే సుకననందున, ఈ వి షయాంశ సంగ్రహానిన అందించడం నా తర్పున ఒక చి రు సంకలపం. DISCLAIMER 1. The contents of this booklet have been drawn for practical usage to encompass the material actions required therefor in respective items of action, so as to comply the legal prerequisites. 2. The field level and office level repercussions taken into account to formulate the practical way of methods suggested herein. This booklet does not contain every practical aspect since it is impossible to narrate all, due to inequality of issues to be dealt with. Major steps only highlighted so as to ultimately decrease the legal implications. 3. In the event of inconsistency in between the ways and means capsulated in this booklet or law for the time being in force, latter prevails over former. 4. This Hand Booklet is suggestive in content and not mandatory in any manner. It is requested to ultimately follow the provisions of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964; Allied laws in force.

2 MODULAR STRUCTURE OF THE HAND BOOKLET 1. The “Practical Hand Booklet” is designed with specific modules of which each is intended for a statutory function or part of it. 2. The discharge of statutory functions could encompass the entire departmental web. That means, certain aspects of a function shall be performed at office level, while remaining are to be performed at filed level. Both the levels of action are indispensable and essential all together. 3. On the other hand, the functionaries of the department usually shuttle in between office administration and filed administration. As such, it is highly essential for all the functionaries of the department to equip their skills in both the arenas. 4. Fulfilling these objectives is keystone basis behind the development of this hand booklet of training. 5. In addition, this booklet is aimed to strengthen the departmental functioning, especially to withstand the legal scrutiny of departmental functions by the Hon’ble Courts. 6. The departmental functionaries are investing enormous hard work and dedicative efforts for the betterment of Cooperatives. However, the delinquents are utilizing the shortfalls in the legal awareness of the department functionaries, raising technical points, getting desired reliefs from the Hon’ble Courts, which are altogether fatal to the interest of the department as well as the Cooperatives. 7. The protection of interests of the Cooperatives and the Department is the ultimate focus behind developing this Hand Booklet.

3 8. The actionable steps given in Law, Manuals have not been reiterated herein, since the same exist mutatis mutandis, and all the functionaries are required to ipso facto comply the same while discharging various functions. The areas where these existing instruments not touched, especially the practical approach is the driving factor behind this training booklet. 9. The “Practical Hand Booklet” is divided into the following training modules. MODULE-1: INQUIRY. MODULE-2: INSPECTION. MODULE-3: SPECIAL AUDIT REPORT. MODULE-4: GOVERNING LEGAL PROVISIONS. MODULE-5: SURCHARGE. MODULE-6: ARBITRATION. APPEAL: In order to achieve qualitative merits, one should necessarily identify, point out and resolve the quantitative demerits exist in any system. Then only systemic correction is possible. For this, our department functionaries are not an exception. As such, certain demerits existing in the departmental functionaries have been stated to the extent needful so as to suggest corrective measures. Such an effort is intended not to criticize anybody, but only aimed to plug the strategic leakage points in the departmental functional system. Though it is hard nut to crack, but an indispensable essentiality while in the refinement and reinforcement of the departmental functioning. The Hon’ble Courts have been pointing out avowedly about the existing lacunae involved in the undertaken statutory functions of the department. Now, it is high time to address the professional incapacities, so as to strengthen the departmental functioning.

4 MODULE-1: INQUIRY In order to determine the prerequisites required for ordering an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, it is needful to know about law applicable and shall take appropriate steps to fulfill the said legal provisions. [I]. ACTIONS AT THE LEVEL OF OFFICE UPTO ORDERING AN INQUIRY: (1).LAW : In accordance with Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, “the Registrar can hold an inquiry or direct some person authorised by him by an order in this behalf to hold an inquiry, in any of the following events: 1. Of his own motion ( suo moto ). 2. On the application of a society to which the society concerned is affiliated. 3. On the application of not less than one third of the members of the Committee. 4. On the application of not less than one fifth of the total number of members of the society. In general, the inquiry or inspection is being ordered suo moto in majority cases. The other circumstances are rarest, but those cases are specific and easy to mention in the order. It is needful to state that the ‘Proceedings of ordering an inquiry’ shall contain necessarily certain essentials. In the absence of such essentials, the delinquents are trying to figure out the technical defects therein and invalidating the order of inquiry during the court proceedings. Hence, the order must depict the legal prerequisites provided therefor.

5 (2).UNCONSIDERABLE BASIS FOR ORDERING AN INQUIRY : 1. In certain cases, the inquiries have been ordered on the references of MLA letters, Minister endorsements, PGRS petitions, petitions from local leaders or petitions from others. The referential details of these sources have also been mentioning directly in the inquiry orders, which is found to be inappropriate in the eye of Hon’ble Courts. 2. The petitions intact, PGRS petitions, Letters from MLAs, MPs, Minister endorsements shall not be made basis directly. The delinquents are utilizing such circumstances as their defence in the court of law. 3. Strictly speaking, they are raising the objection that the Registrar has ordered inquiry not on his own motion, but by basing on the recommendation of public representatives or petitioners, hence the very basis of ordering inquiry is invalid and thereby the inquiry report itself was unlawful and there shall be no scope for action whatsoever basing on such inquiry report. 4. Hence, all these documents shall be treated as just “INFORMATION” to commence some and certain official action contemplated in the matter. (3).CONSIDERABLE BASIS FOR ORDERING AN INQUIRY : The erstwhile Hon’ble High Court of Andhra Pradesh has held in L.N.Peta Primary Agricultural Cooperative Society vs. The Government of Andhra Pradesh, rep. by its Principal Secretary, Cooperative Societies Department, Hyderabad and others , (2015) 1 ALD 253 that “ even for an authority to exercise suo moto jurisdiction, he needs to gather information from various external agencies …” 1. To order an inquiry information from external agencies must be needed to the Registrar/District Cooperative Officer. However, one should ensure that the source of such information is necessarily

6 from the Government Officers in regular line of official administration. 2. The petitions, PGRS petitions, Letters from MLAs, MPs, Minister endorsements shall not be made basis directly. On receipt of such petitions, the District Cooperative Officer or Divisional Cooperative Officer shall conduct or cause to be conducted the petition enquiry into such petitions. 3. The petition enquiry officer shall conduct clear proceedings of enquiry as far as possible, and must give specific findings by establishing the ‘prima facie basis’ towards occurring of fraud or irregularity. 4. Ensure that there shall be no successive or multiple enquiries on the same allegation, unless the petition enquiry was materialized with substantive defects or deficiencies. Conducting successive or multiple enquiries on same allegations come under harassment of the person against whom probed thereto. 5. The findings of the petition enquiry report shall be considered as external information to the Registrar/District Cooperative Officer to take a decision towards ordering an inquiry, on his/her own motion. (4).OFFICIAL BASIS TO BE MENTIONED IN THE INQUIRY ORDER : The petition enquiry report and its contents shall only be mentioned in the Inquiry order as prima facie basis in support of occurrence of irregularities, and it shall be made narrative to the effect that the Registrar/ District Cooperative Officer has formed into opinion to order inquiry. It is often found in the Inquiry order that mentioning as to, “the inquiry has been ordered into the contents of the petition in the reference read above.” This is not correct. Without forming opinion findings on the contents of such reference reports, it is inappropriate for the District Cooperative Officer to order an inquiry on his/her own motion.

7 (5).TERMS OF INQUIRY : 1. As per Rule 47 of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, an order authorizing any person to hold an inquiry into the constitution, working and financial conditions of a society shall, among other things, contain also the terms of inquiry. It is needful to mention specific terms of inquiry in the order itself. 2. In the order of inquiry, the terms of inquiry shall necessarily be mentioned with prima facie details with reasonable clarity. 3. The determination of terms of inquiry is crucial step in the office procedure. The terms shall be decided not only by nature of irregularity, but more importantly the quantitative volume of transactions both in terms of number and time periods. 4. For example, one allegation came with that there were benami loans in one PACS during the reign of AAAA president. In such cases, the Assistant Registrar/Credit working in the office of the District Cooperative Officer straight away putting up the file to the District Cooperative Officer for inquiry. The District Cooperative Officer is also ordering the said inquiry. 5. By squarely considering the issue of benami loans during the reign of AAAA president as the only considerable term, and if ordered inquiry to that effect, there is every possibility for incidence of time constraint to the respective inquiry officer. 6. Conducting inquiry is a herculean task. The inquiry officer ought to conduct inquiry covering heavy voluminous transactions encompassing the period of 10 years had if the said AAAA president worked for about two terms. This kind of analysis shall be done while determining the terms of inquiry. 7. Once the inquiry is ordered, the performance of the said inquiry officer is being weighed and reviewed only on the basis of prescribed time limit for completion of inquiry, and never thinks about such voluminous load of work, which is ultimately straining the

8 inquiry officer. It is one of the prime causes for the delay in completion of inquiries. 8. In addition to this, equity in the entrustment of inquiries among employees shall also be ensured, in such a way that no employee is overburdened. This will augment the quality of inquiry report and its timely completion. 9. It may be necessarily and essentially taken note that the conduct of inquiry is not a full-time function for the inquiry officer. He has to do such inquiry duty besides attending to other regular duties, including the proximate audit and periodical inspections. 10. Hence, there shall be rationality in determining the terms of inquiry in terms of both the volume of work as well as the period of work. There is no restriction in the law to order multiple inquiries in one particular society with different terms of inquiry. 11. The Assistant Registrar/Credit or Non-Credit, in the office of the District Cooperative Officer shall vigilantly work on this aspect. It may also be noted that the inquiry officer is entitled to report frauds other than those stipulated under terms of inquiry. 12. Therefore, it is necessary that the Assistant Registrars, Senior Inspectors having relevant field experience can be positioned, as far as possible, in DCO offices. If it is not possible, at least one fairly big inquiry must be entrusted to such officers, to know about field level implications in the conduct of inquiry. Doing such duties equip them securing necessary capabilities in the determination of terms of inquiry appropriately. 13. Not only to District Cooperative Office, the Assistant Registrars or Senior Inspectors or Junior Inspectors who have been working continuously in other offices and not having been experienced in this regard, must conduct at least one fairly big inquiry to get knowledge about field level implications.

9 14. Then only these officers working in the DCO office and DLCO office would assess the workload involved therewith in realistic pattern, and determine the terms of inquiry correctly in such a way that could limit overburdening the employees by means of entrusting highly voluminous work load inquiry or of multiple inquiries at time or in succession. (6).LEGAL IMPLICATIONS ON THE MATTER OF AUTHORITY OF THE DISTRICT COOPERATIVE OFFICER TO ORDER INQUIRY : 1. In general, at the last para of the inquiry order, it will be mentioned that “under the powers vested under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964,”. However, it is not sufficient. 2. In accordance with Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, “the Registrar can hold an inquiry or direct some person authorised by him by an order in this behalf to hold an inquiry”. 3. In a writ appeal filed recently, the appellant has contended that “since the inquiry has not been ordered by the Registrar nor it has been ordered to be conducted by the person authorized by him and in the absence of any specific order having been issued by the Registrar, any order passed by the District Cooperative Officer under Section 51 of the Act is unsustainable”. 4. Herein, the counsel for petitioner has argued that Registrar means Registrar of Cooperative Societies. The placing of order of delegation of powers connected thereto was not placed with appropriate interpretation and applicability. As a result, stay granted on this score alone. Thereafter, the said appeal was in fact recently disposed by the Hon’ble High Court of Andhra Pradesh, Amaravathi by holding that the District Cooperative Officer is not having powers to order an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, into the affairs of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society, on the reason that the powers under Section Sec.116-C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 alone delegated to the District Cooperative Officer, and not others, vide

10 item-(iii) of Sl.No.2 of Schedule of G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989 . 5. It is apparent and very much essential that the powers of the District Cooperative Officer shall be mentioned in the inquiry order, to avoid stay orders or final orders on this grounds. (7).LEGAL PROVISIONS DETERMINING THE AUTHORITY OF THE DISTRICT COOPERATIVE OFFICER TO ORDER INQUIRY : 1. Section 2(n) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 envisages as hereunder: “Registrar”, means the Registrar of Cooperative Societies appointed under Section 3(1) and includes any other person on whom all or any of the powers of the Registrar under this Act are conferred. 2. In G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989, the Government of Andhra Pradesh has appointed the authorities mentioned in column (2) of the schedule and confers on them in respect of societies mentioned in column (3) thereof, the powers of the Registrar under the said Act and the rules made thereunder, specified in the corresponding entry in column (4) of the said schedule. 3. The powers under Section 116-C(1) of the A.P. Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 which were exempted to District Cooperative Officers, as far as Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies as in item-ii of Sl.No.2 of Schedule of G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989, have been delegated to the District Cooperative Officers in respect of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies only, by inserting Item (iii) under Sl.No.2 of the Schedule, vide G.O.Ms.No.553 Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 13.07.1989. 4. In fact, the powers delegated to the District Cooperative Officers in respect of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies (PACS) confining to the power of ordering an inquiry under Section 51 was

11 delegated first through Item (ii) of Sl.No.2 of the Schedule appended to G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989. Subsequently, while delegating relevant additional powers to the District Cooperative Officers, the Government has issued G.O.Ms.No.553 Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 13.07.1989 by also giving powers exercisable under Section 116-C of the A.P. Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 by duly inserting in Schedule the additional provision, i.e., Item (iii) of Sl.No.2. 5. Therefore, the delegation of powers to the District Cooperative Officers under Section 116-C of the A.P. Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 by duly inserting in Schedule as the additional provision, i.e., Item (iii) of Sl.No.2 of the Schedule vide G.O.Ms.No.553 Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 13.07.1989, as far as P.A.C.S. is concerned, has not usurped the other powers (including under Sec.51) already delegated to the District Cooperative Officers under Item (ii) of Sl.No.2 of the Schedule appended to G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989. 6. In G.O.Ms.No.19, Agriculture and Cooperation (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 01.02.1995, the Government of Andhra Pradesh has amended schedule stating that the notification issued in the G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989, the words Special Cadre Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies working as “appearing in Col.2 against Sl.No.2 shall be deleted”. 7. Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 envisages as hereunder: ‘Primary Society’ means a society whose membership consists of:- (i) individuals; (ii) individuals and Government; or (iii) the individuals and societies, not belonging to the same class; or (iv) individuals and the financing Bank.

12 8. The 'Cooperative Urban Banks' and certain other cooperatives have been expressively categorized as 'Primary Societies' vide Rule 36-A(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964. 9. The schedule of G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989 read with G.O.Ms.No.19, Agriculture and Cooperation (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 01.02.1995 as far as the authority of the District Cooperative Officer is extracted herein: Sl. No. Authority Type of Cooperative Societies Extent of powers conferred. 2. District Cooperative Officer. (i). District Cooperative Central Banks and District Cooperative Marketing Societies. All the powers of the Registrar under the Act except those under sections 4(2), 7(1) (in so far as it relates to prescribing minimum share capital and membership), sections 17, 18, third proviso to sub-section (1) of section 31, sections 32(7), 34, 50, 60, 64(1), 86, 115, 116, 116-A and 116-C and the powers of the Registrar under Rules 4, 12, 27, 28, 29, 34(14), 39, 41, 45, 59 and 68. District Cooperative Officer. (ii). Other District level societies and other societies whose area of operation extends to more than one district and whose headquarters is situated in the district and which is not an Apex society and Primary Societies other than the societies under the administrative control of functional registrars. All the powers of the Registrar under the Act (except those under sections 4(2), 7(1) (in so far as it relates to prescribing minimum share capital and membership), sections 17, 18, third proviso to sub-section (1) of section 31, sections 50, 83, 115, 116, 116-A and 116-C and the powers of the Registrar under rules 4, 12, 27, 28, 29, 34(14), 39, 41, 45, 59 and 68. (iii). Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies. Powers of the Registrar under sub-section (1) of Sec.116- C(1) of the Act, subject to guidelines issued by Registrar of Cooperative Societies.

13 (8).MENTIONING IN THE INQUIRY ORDER ABOUT THE AUTHORITY OF THE DISTRICT COOPERATIVE OFFICER IN ORDERING THE INQUIRY : By taking the aforementioned legal provisions as basis, the conferment of authority on the District Cooperative Officer shall essentially and expressively be recorded in the Inquiry Order issued under Sec.51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. Due to the aforementioned legal scrutiny, it is now it is mandatorily relevant to show about delegation of powers to the District Cooperative Officer with reasonable description. The paragraph to be recorded in the Inquiry Order is shown below: Ex-1: P.A.C.S.: If the inquiry is to be ordered into the affairs of a PACS or FSCS: “The __________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, __________ is working under the control of Registrar and not under the Functional Registrar. The members of this cooperative society belong to the member categories prescribed under the provisions of Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, and hence lawfully it is a ‘primary society’. ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________ fall squarely under the category of ‘Primary Societies other than those under the administrative control of functional registrars’ vide Item (ii) for Sl.No.2 of the Schedule appended to G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989, as amended by G.O.Ms.No.19, dated 01.02.1995, and as such the powers under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 have been delegated to the District Cooperative Officer in respect of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies (PACS). In view of the provisions as in Section 51, Section 2(n) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 and the delegation of powers of the Registrar to the District Cooperative Officer vide G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989 and G.O.Ms.No.19, Agriculture and Cooperation (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 01.02.1995, the District Cooperative

14 Officer, _________, in his/her official capacity, is having sufficient lawful authority to order an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 into the affairs of ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________.” Ex-2: URBAN COOPERATIVE BANK: If the inquiry is to be ordered into the affairs of an UCB: “The __________ Urban Cooperative Bank Limited, __________ is working under the control of Registrar and not under the Functional Registrar. The members of this cooperative society belong to the member categories prescribed under the provisions of Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, and hence lawfully it is a ‘primary society’. The Urban Cooperative Banks have been expressively shown as ‘Primary Societies’ under Rule 36-A(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964. __________ Urban Cooperative Bank Limited, __________ fall squarely under the category of ‘Primary Societies other than those under the administrative control of functional registrars’ vide Item (ii) for Sl.No.2 of the Schedule appended to G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989, as amended by G.O.Ms.No.19, dated 01.02.1995, and as such the powers under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 have been delegated to the District Cooperative Officer in respect of Urban Cooperative Banks. In view of the provisions as in Section 51, Section 2(n) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; Rule 2(j), 36-A of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 and as per the delegation of powers of the Registrar to the District Cooperative Officer vide G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989 and G.O.Ms.No.19, Agriculture and Cooperation (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 01.02.1995, the District Cooperative Officer, _________, in his/her official capacity, is having sufficient lawful authority to order an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 into the affairs of __________ Urban Cooperative Bank Limited, __________.”

15 [Difference between vested powers and conferred powers : It is observed from the statutory orders from the District Cooperative Office and Divisional Cooperative Office that they are using the words like “under the powers vested in me under Section…”. It is inappropriate. The powers vested means such powers are vested alone with ONE authority as per law. For example, power to issue Section 4(2) directions alone vested with Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, and hence, they are vested powers. The powers exercising by the District Cooperative Officer and Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies are in fact the powers of the Registrar delegated through G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 18.01.1989. Hence, they are conferred powers. Hence, at these levels the orders must be with terminology like “under the powers conferred upon me” shall be used.] (9).AUTHORIZATION TO INQUIRY OFFICER : The Inquiry Order shall contain the details of officer who has been authorized to conduct inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. (10).PERIOD OF COMPLETION : The period for completion of inquiry shall be mentioned in the inquiry order. It is always advisable to provide statutory period of 4 months, since the duty of inquiry is not a full-time job for inquiry officer. As this period is maximum, certain specific period lesser than to that may also be prescribed depending on emergency nature of the fraud. Nevertheless, there is provision for extension of further 2 months beyond 4 months. Specific indication in this regard shall be placed in inquiry order. In consideration of above discussion, it may be observed that the Assistant Registrar working in the office of the District Cooperative Officer shall pay his attention to the above issues in drafting the inquiry order and shall be mentioned the following aspects essentially. 1. About the Preliminary enquiry report, its findings, prima facie basis. 2. Formation of opinion of the Registrar/District Cooperative Officer on the findings of preliminary enquiry report to arrive a decision to order an inquiry or inspection, on his own motion. 3. About the authority conferred to the District Cooperative Officer to order an inquiry under section 51.

16 4. Specific and reasonably narrated terms of inquiry. Rationality in terms of quantitative transactions and period of coverage shall be kept in mind while ordering inquiry. At any cost, the inquiry officer shall not be overburdened. 5. Authorization to Officer as Inquiry Officer. 6. The period in which inquiry shall be completed. It is always advisable to provide statutory period. The specimen inquiry order fulfilling all the above issues has been attached as below for practical reference. It may be observed that all the legal ingredients enshrined under Section 51 are depicted in the given specimen order. Such specimen can suitably be modified depending on the items of fraud and other circumstances.

17 SPECIMEN INQUIRY ORDER PROCEEDINGS UNDER SEC.51 OF APCS ACT, 1964 ISSUED BY THE DISTRICT COOPERATIVE OFFICER, _________________, ______ DISTRICT. Present: Rc.No. Dated. Sub : Cooperation Department –Inquiry under Section 51 of APCS Act, 1964 ordered into the affairs of _____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, __________, _________ Mandal, __________ district – Orders Issued. Read : 1. Enquiry Report, Rc.No.__, dated: ___ of the Sub Divisional Cooperative Officer,________. 2. Letter, Rc.No.___, Dated: _____ of the Divisional Cooperative Officer,________. @@@@@ [1]. Whereas in the reference 1 st read above, an enquiry was conducted by Sri.______________, Assistant Registrar, Office of the Sub Divisional Cooperative Officer, ___________ into the affairs of the _________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, ________, _______ Mandal, ______ district with particular reference to the irregularities occurred in sanction of LT & ST loans by pledging fake pattadar pass books and issue of benami loans. In the ref.2 nd read above, the Divisional Cooperative Officer, Kadapa has recommended to order an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. [2]. Whereas the Undersigned has examined the findings of the enquiry report cited in the ref.1 st read above and considered that there exists prima facie basis in the occurrence of irregularities in sanction of LT and ST loans by pledging fake pattadar pass books and issue of benami loans. The Undersigned suo moto held that an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 shall be ordered into the affairs of _________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, ________, _______ Mandal, ______ district on following terms of inquiry.

18 1. Sanction of LT loans by mortgaging fake pattadar pass books during the years 2022-2023 and 2023-2024. 2. Sanction of benami SAO loans during the years 2022-2023 and 2023-2024. 3. Short collections in respect of LT loans and their irregular write off occurred during the years 2022-2023 and 2023-2024. [3]. “The __________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, __________ is working under the control of Registrar and not under the Functional Registrar. The members of this cooperative society belong to the member categories prescribed under the provisions of Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, and hence lawfully it is a ‘primary society’. ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________ fall squarely under the category of ‘Primary Societies other than those under the administrative control of functional registrars’ vide Item (ii) for Sl.No.2 of the Schedule appended to G.O.Ms.No.34, Food & Agriculture (Coop.IV) Department, dated 16.01.1989, as amended by G.O.Ms.No.19, dated 01.02.1995, and as such the powers under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 have been delegated to the District Cooperative Officer in respect of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies (PACS). In view of the provisions as in Section 51, Section 2(n) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; Rule 2(j) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 and the delegation of powers of the Registrar to the District Cooperative Officer vide G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 16.01.1989 and G.O.Ms.No.19, Agriculture and Cooperation (Coop.IV) Dept., Dated 01.02.1995, the District Cooperative Officer, _________, in his/her official capacity, is having sufficient lawful authority to order an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 into the affairs of ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________.” Therefore, by virtue of powers conferred upon me under Sec.51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, I, the District Cooperative Officer, _______ do herby orders an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 into the affairs of ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________.

19 Sri.____________, Assistant Registrar, Office of the Divisional Cooperative Officer, _________ is hereby authorized to conduct inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 into the affairs of ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________, within a period of 4 months. Given under my digital signature and seal on this the ____ day, _______, 2025. District Cooperative Officer, _________, _____ District TO 1.Sri.____________, Assistant Registrar, Office of the Divisional Cooperative Officer, _________, through the Divisional Cooperative Officer, __________. 2.Chief Executive Officer, ____________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, _____________. 3.Branch Manager, __________ DCCB, _________. Copy to the Divisional Cooperative Officer, __________ with instructions to serve the inquiry order on the above, submit their dated acknowledgements to this office. Copy to the file. The inquiry order shall be served on the inquiry officer, on the respective society and on its financing bank by without any protraction, and must obtain specific dated acknowledgments from them and file in the record.

20 [II]. ACTION AT THE LEVEL OF INQUIRY OFFICER: (1).PRELIMINARIES : 1. The Inquiry Officer upon receipt of the order of inquiry, shall immediately acknowledge the receipt of said order. He has to endorse on the order his sign with the date of its receipt. 2. Then the inquiry officer should immediately visit the office of the District Cooperative Officer or the Divisional Cooperative Officer and shall obtain in full shape the preliminary enquiry report and other related documents. He has to carefully study the findings of preliminary enquiry report in correlation of the terms of inquiry given in the order, and must note down the possible array of transactions to be inquired. 3. Inquiry Officer must also obtain and examine the periodical inspection reports and audit reports for deriving valid points. 4. Inquiry Officer must visit to the society, conduct a summary verification to ascertain the occurrence of irregularities, period of irregularities, kinds of transactional heads of accounts, amount to be examined basing on actuals recorded in the respective heads of accounts, details about books of accounts, records, registers maintained, details of officers covered in such periods, etc. With that he may get an overall skeleton about the work to be performed. 5. Then inquiry officer should give a first notice to the Chief Executive Officer of society by requiring him to produce or keep ready the essential records for inquiry. On the same day, he shall submit an inquiry commencement report to the District Cooperative Officer under copy to the Divisional Cooperative Officer. If there is a suspicion for tampering of records, immediately seize the records required for the inquiry and keep in the custody of inquiry officer.

21 (2).CONDUCT OF INQUIRY: 1. The conduct of inquiry is unique action depending on the nature of irregularities or items of fraud. The inquiry shall be conducted keeping in view the points to be written in the inquiry report encompassing the terms of inquiry. 2. Strictly speaking one should understand thoroughly what is to be written in the inquiry report of which ultimately guide the inquiry officer how to conduct inquiry, what to note for the inquiry. Since these are case specific, not dealt in detail herein. 3. The inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 will be conducted into the constitution, working and financial position of the society. The inquiry is extensive than inspection. Nevertheless, the inquiry officer is never precluded to go beyond the terms of inquiry. (3).GATHERING REPORTABLE INFORMATION TOGETHER WITH SUBSTANTIVE EVIDENCES : The inquiry officer shall ultimately report the irregularities under three categories: 1. Civil Liability. 2. Criminal Liability. 3. Administrative Liability. Hence, while in the process of inquiry, the material facts and material evidences shall be noted down in his note book/ diary book, with reasoned clarity for mentioning in the inquiry report. For achieving this successfully, the inquiry officer shall study the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964; Allied laws applicable; Bye Law of society.

22 (4).BASIC STRUCTURE OF INQUIRY REPORT UNDER SECTION 51 : In fact, the law did not prescribe any structure for the inquiry report. The following basic structure is suggested for fulfillment of legally required aspects so as to take further follow up actions as well as for comprehensive coverage of essential facts and information. A.Covering Letter of Inquiry Officer addressed to the Authority who ordered inquiry, i.e., the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh or District Cooperative Officer. This is not part of inquiry report, but carries the inquiry report to the respective authority. B.INQUIRY REPORT : It shall not be drafted in covering letter format. No from and to details shall be mentioned. MODEL _________ Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, __________________. INQUIRY REPORT [UNDER SECTION 51 OF A.P.COOPERATIVE SOCIETIES ACT, 1964] 1.CONSTITUTION: 2. WORKING: 3.FINANCIAL POSTIION: 4.TERMS OF INQUIRY: 5.MODE OF SECURING RECORDS: 6.PERSONS EXAMINED IN INQUIRY: 7.INQUIRY REPORTING: 8.ABSTRACT REPORTING: 9.LIST OF EVIDENTIARY DOCUMENTS: 1. CONSTITUTION : About organization of society, its registration number, area of operation, about Bye Law, present membership, about objectives of the society and details about present business. 2.WORKING : About management of society. The details of Managing Committee committees worked during the periods in which irregularities occurred shall be mentioned including the existing Managing Committee

23 working while conducting inquiry. The names, designations, any breaks in period, any co-options shall also be mentioned. 3.FINANCIAL POSTIION : The financial position of the society is to be discussed herein. The brief evaluation of assets and liabilities of the society shall be discussed. The receipts and payments and final accounts at least be mentioned. The ongoing business and about financial irregularities, their effect on financial position can also be mentioned, if possible. 4.TERMS OF INQUIRY : The details about the basis considered for ordering the inquiry like petition enquiry, details of inquiry order like Rc.No., date, authority whom ordered shall be narrated. The terms of inquiry prescribed therefor shall be mentioned as indicated in the inquiry order. If not available in inquiry order, deduce the terms what you inquired, and mention in the inquiry report. The inquiry officer is never precluded to inquire into the irregularities, beyond the terms of inquiry. 5.MODE OF SECURING RECORDS : Describe about the notices, summons, letters issued to the society and others for securing the required records and evidences. 6.PERSONS EXAMINED IN INQUIRY : List out the details of persons who have been examined during the inquiry and about their recorded depositions for the purpose of inquiry. Abstract model given below: S.N. Name of the Person Position 1. AAAA Ex-President 2. BBBB Ex-Vice-President 3. CCCC Ex-Secretary 4. DDDD Ex-Treasurer 5. EEEE Petitioner. 6. FFFF Member 7. GGGG Director 8. HHHH Petition Enquiry Officer

24 Individually at least the following information of each deponent shall be obtained and mentioned either in the inquiry report or in deposition or in both. Deponent: 1 : AAAA : [D1] S.N. Particulars Details of the Deponent 1 Fathers’ Name 2 Age 3 Profession 4 Designation in the Society or relation to the society 5 Residencial Address 6 Mobile number 7.INQUIRY REPORTING : This portion is the actual reporting part on the frauds. The reporting of each fraud shall be written into at least three sub parts. For example, see the following per one fraud. [A]. Basis of Fraud/Irregularity : How first detected the irregularity and by whom shall be mentioned. Then narrate about the findings in brief on this irregularity by the petition enquiry officer. That means, information about this irregularity upto ordering of instant inquiry shall be mentioned. What the Sec.51 inquiry officer done with shall not be mentioned herein. [B]. Inquiry : Explain about every aspect of action taken up by the inquiry officer, records examined, persons examined, deposition given by deponents relating to the irregularity, etc. That means, the entire work of inquiry on the irregularity except findings, shall be mentioned. [C]. Inquiry Findings : Purely the findings of inquiry officer be mentioned. Finally, the civil liability, criminal liability, administrative liability shall be mentioned conclusively at the end of reporting of the irregularity. For example : 1. SANCTION OF LOANS ON PLEDGING THE FAKE GOLD AS SECURITY. 25 Gold Loans worth of Rs.7583565.00 were disbursed to three borrowers by pledging the fake gold, during the financial year 2024-2025.

25 1.Basis of Irregularity: ……. 2.Inquiry: …….. 3.Inquiry Findings: …….. The determination of civil liability, criminal liability, administrative liability at the touchstone of connected legal provisions has been dealt in detail in the succeeding module. 8.ABSTRACT REPORTING : After completion of all the frauds/ irregularities in the above said pattern, all those irregularities can be summarized by duly mentioning the abstract figures of civil liability, criminal liability and administrative liability. 9.LIST OF EVIDENTIARY DOCUMENTS : List out the documents and their description of which to be enclosed to the inquiry report. (5).SUBMISSION OF INQUIRY REPORT : The inquiry report prepared in the above pattern shall be submitted to the Authority who ordered the said inquiry, in proper channel. The records seized, if any shall be handed over to the Divisional Cooperative Officer or District Cooperative Officer concerned for safe custody. 6 COPIES : If the inquiry ordered by District Cooperative Officer, the report must be given: 1.Original copy (for surcharge authority), 2.DCO copy, 3.DLCO copy, 4.Society copy, 5.Additional copy (if criminal liability fixed), 6.IO copy. 7 COPIES : If the inquiry ordered by the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Guntur one more copy is needful. The inquiry officer shall necessarily adduce evidence in civil action and criminal action, hence it is advisable to keep one copy with him for ready reference. The specimen notices, summons, seizure notices, deposition, etc., are stipulated below.

36 MODULE-2: INSPECTION In order to determine the prerequisites required for ordering an inspection under Section 52 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, it is needful to know about law applicable and shall take appropriate steps to fulfill the said legal provisions. In accordance with Section 52 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, “the Registrar may inspect or direct any person authorised by him by a general or special order in this behalf to inspect the books of the society, in any of the following events: 1. of his own motion. 2. on the application of a creditor of a society. In general, the inspection can be ordered suo moto in majority cases. The ordering of inspection on the application of creditor is rarest of rare. In consideration of the above facts, it is needful to state that the Proceedings for ordering an inspection shall contain necessarily certain essentials. In the absence of such essentials, the delinquents are trying to find out the technical defects therein and invalidating the order of inspection during the court proceedings. Hence, the order must depict the legal prerequisites provided therefor. The methodology described for ordering an inquiry under Section 51 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 equally applicable while ordering the inspection under Section 52 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. However, there are certain distinct differences in between inquiry and inspection. In consideration of above discussion, it may be observed that the Assistant Registrar working in the office of the District Cooperative Officer shall pay his attention to the following issues in drafting the inspection order and shall be mentioned the following aspects essentially.

37 1. About the Preliminary enquiry report, its findings, prima facie basis. 2. Formation of opinion of the Registrar/District Cooperative Officer on the findings of preliminary enquiry report to arrive a decision to order an inspection, on his/her own motion. 3. About the authority conferred to the District Cooperative Officer to order an inspection under section 52. 4. Specific and reasonably narrated terms of inspection. Rationality in terms of quantitative transactions and period of coverage shall be kept in mind while ordering inquiry. At any cost, the inspection officer shall not be overburdened. 5. Authorization to Officer as Inspection Officer. 6. The period in which inquiry shall be completed. It is always advisable to provide statutory period. PERIOD OF COMPLETION: The period for completion of inspection shall be mentioned in the inspection order. It is always advisable to provide statutory period of 3 months, since the duty of inspection is not a full-time job for inspection officer. As this period is maximum, certain specific period lesser than to that may also be prescribed depending on emergency nature of the fraud. Nevertheless, there is provision for extension of further 2 months beyond 3 months. Specific indication in this regard shall be placed in inspection order. The model inspection order fulfilling all the above issues could be developed from the specimen inquiry order by making suitable changes depending on needy circumstances. REPORTING OF SEC.52 INSPECTION REPORT : Technically speaking there is no difference in reporting in both cases. For easy to understand and easy to learn and work, it is advised to follow the pattern suggested for inquiry report. It may be seen that the model content prescribed for inquiry in the preceding paragraphs under inquiry module is also essential for inspection report. But substitute certain terms: use ‘organization’ in the place of ‘constitution’; use ‘management’ in the place of ‘working’; and keep ‘financial position’ intact. For every society, those particulars are essential even for dealing with inspection report.

38 MODULE-3: SPECIAL AUDIT REPORT 1. As per Section 50(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, there shall be a separate wing for audit in the Co-operative Department headed by the Chief Auditor who will work under the general superintendence and control of the Registrar of Co- operative Societies. 2. The Chief Auditor shall audit or cause to be audited by a person authorised by him by a general or special order in this behalf, the accounts of a society at least once in every year and shall issue or cause to be issued an audit certificate with such particulars as may be prescribed, before the end of the succeeding cooperative year. 3. Such audit shall primarily cover: [i].Examination of the debts, overdue, if any. [ii].Verification of the cash balance and securities. [iii].Valuations of the assets and liabilities of the society, [iv].Prudent management of the affairs of the society in accordance with the Act, rules and byelaws. 4. The serious financial and non-financial irregularities are being reported under Part-A defects sheet, besides submitting special report. 5. The auditors often did not report comprehensively and are leaving the matter again to the inquiry or inspection. This is not always fair. 6. The audit report/special report are sufficient for initiation of appropriate follow up actions as per statute. The irregularities shall be mentioned in the special report by covering complete details. The special report shall be drafted in letter format. 7. The specimen notice, summon, deposition, notice of seizure of records stipulated under inquiry module, can be utilized by the Auditor by making suitable changes including the reflection of audit function.
