45 ACTION AT THE LEVEL OF OFFICES AFTER RECEIPT OF INQUIRY REPORT OR INSPECTION REPORT OR SPECIAL AUDIT REPORT: 1. Consequent on receipt of inquiry/inspection report in office of the Divisional Cooperative Officer, the same shall be communicated to the District Cooperative Officer/Registrar in proper channel. The authority who ordered the inquiry/inspection, i.e., Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh or the District Cooperative Officer, as the case may be, shall issue review on such findings of the inquiry/inspection report. 2. Consequent on receipt of special audit report in office of the District Cooperative Audit Officer, the same shall be communicated to the District Cooperative Officer. The copy shall also be marked to the concerned Divisional Cooperative Officer. The District Cooperative Officer shall issue review on such findings of the special audit report. The District Cooperative Officer can communicate the special audit report with specific review direction of follow up action to be taken up by the respective Divisional Cooperative Officer. 3. Consequent on receipt of the review, the Divisional Cooperative Officer shall prepare an order under Section 54 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 by giving specific directions to the Managing Committee and Chief Executive of the respective society to complete the rectifications as per stipulated liabilities, besides keeping the report in the General Body meeting. 4. In respect of civil liability and criminal liability, files shall be run in the offices of the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies. Depending on delegation of powers and category of societies, these official positions shall change accordingly. 5. The action for conditional attachment of the properties of the delinquents shall also be taken up as per law in force.

46 MODULE-4: GOVERNING LEGAL PROVISIONS In the Modules stated supra, it has been categorically mentioned that the Inquiry Officer or Inspection Officer or Auditor shall necessarily submit the inquiry report or inspection report or special report by duly enumerating the civil liability, criminal liability and administrative liability. The knowledge of connected legal provisions is the utmost essentiality on the part of the Departmental Functionaries so as to determine ‘what’, ‘why’, and ‘how’ of the instrumentalities of civil, criminal, administrative actions materialize in the investigation and reporting processes. 1. It is easy to know about structure of various statutory reports and even their preparatory pattern. But what matters most is content reported in findings. This is crucial area where departmental officers shall pay at most attention and care. 2. Because the inquiry officer/inspection officer/auditor is not entitled to write the inquiry report/inspection report/special audit report at his whims and fancies, but permitted to the extent needful for the follow up actions which are required to be performed as per law. 3. That means, the reporting in inquiry/inspection/special reports shall fulfil the needs or prerequisites essential for the statutory follow up actions, and not otherwise. 4. Every inquiry officer/inspection officer/auditor must know which are the follow up actions contemplated to be taken up subsequent to the submission of inquiry/inspection/audit/special report. 5. The follow up actions in general can be categorized into three parts. [i].Civil Liability : The action under Section 60 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 for recovery of misappropriated amounts, unlawful loss, contrary payments, etc. [ii].Criminal Liability : The institution of criminal action on the culprits as per the penal provisions laid down under A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, BNS, etc.

47 [iii].Administrative Liability : The actions required to be taken up by the department, management of the society to rectify the inconsistencies reported in the inquiry/inspection/audit/special reports. The Registrar shall issue order under Section 54 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. 6. The reporting of inquiry/inspection officer/auditor is statutory in nature and shall be an important document for taking all further statutory actions. 7. The reporting under civil liability of inquiry/inspection/audit report shall meet the requirements of action to be taken up under Section 60 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. 8. The reporting under criminal liability shall meet the requirements of action laid down under A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 and BNS. 9. The reporting under administrative liability shall meet the requirements of action under various provisions laid down under A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, Bye Law, Allied Laws. [I].CIVIL LIABILITY: (A).Persons on whom civil liability to be fixed: 1. Inquiry/Inspection Officer/Auditor shall know on whom civil liability shall be fixed. He shall not recommend on any person except those falls within the meaning of “ person ” as laid down under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. 2. In accordance with Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, “ any person who is or was entrusted with the organisation, affairs or management of the society ” or “ any past or present officer or servant of the society ” are liable to restore

48 funds to the society if surcharged therefor. The inquiry/inspection officer/auditor shall fix civil liability against such persons alone. 3. The persons entrusted with organisation and affairs of society relate to the members of respective society. The persons entrusted with management of society relate to the Managing Committee members of the respective society. 4. In accordance with Section 2(k): ‘Officer’ includes a person elected or appointed by a society to any office of such society according to its bye-laws and a president, vice president, chairman, vice- chairman, secretary, assistant secretary, treasurer, manager, member of committee, liquidator or any other person elected or appointed under this Act, Rules or the bye-laws to give directions in regard to the business of the society. 5. Hence, the inquiry/inspection officer/auditor is authorized to fix civil liability against the above persons alone. Under any circumstances, he shall not recommend civil liability against the persons not covered in the above categories. More importantly, the above categorized persons belonging to or related to a society shall be surcharged while in the restoration of funds to that particular society. 6. The Hon’ble High Court of Andhra Pradesh, has adjudicated that the persons belonging to other societies shall not be surcharged for the irregularities occurred in one particular society. That means the term ‘society’ mentioned in Section 60(1) shall be applicable for only one society while dealing with civil liability. Example: In a lending activity, to certain stages the involvement of DCCB staff will also involve, besides staff and management of PACS. In the event of irregularity in such lending, when the inquiry/inspection ordered into the respective PACS, or in the audit, it is possible to fix civil liability against members, Managing Committee members, officers of PACS alone and not belonging to the DCCB. However, such staff of DCCB are liable for disciplinary action but not for civil action under Sec.60. If the inquiry ordered into DCCB, vice versa with respect to PACS. This could remain intact until contrary adjudicated in the Court of Law.

49 (B).Category of Financial Fraud to be reported for recovery: Inquiry/ Inspection officer/Auditor shall be entitled to report financial fraud/irregularity as one among the specific categories mentioned under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 and not others. The civil liability shall be fixed against persons when they do following acts. [i].Person has misappropriated . (or) [ii].Person fraudulently retained any money or other property. (or) [iii].Person has been guilty of breach of trust in relation to the society (or) [iv].Person has caused any deficiency in the assets of the society by breach of trust or wilful negligence . (or) [v].Person has made any payment contrary to the provisions of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964; or the bye-laws. [i].Person has misappropriated: In general, inquiry/inspection officers/ auditors have been using the term ‘misappropriated’ for all categories of financial irregularities. It is not correct. It is needful to visit the other laws to know what constitutes ‘misappropriation’. As per Section 314 of BNS, w hoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property. As per Section 79(1)(f) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, an officer, or an employee of the society including the paid Secretary dishonestly or fraudulently mis- appropriates or otherwise converts for his own use. Though there is versatile applicability in between misappropriation and breach of trust, it is conductive that the financial irregularities in which the person who committed such irregularity be proved with guilty of personal benefit, i.e., for his own use of property, can be treated as misappropriated.

50 Misappropriation is where the money or property has been absorbed, expanded or dissipated on his own account by the wrong doer. It is more important to prove such transactions have been committed with dishonestly or fraudulently. Sec.2(7) of BNS: “Dishonestly” means doing anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person. Sec.2(36) of BNS: “Wrongful gain” means gain by unlawful means of property to which the person gaining is not legally entitled. Sec.2(37) of BNS: “Wrongful loss” means the loss by unlawful means of property to which the person losing it is legally entitled. Sec.2(38) of BNS: “Gaining wrongfully” and “losing wrongfully”: A person is said to gain wrongfully when such person retains wrongfully, as well as when such person acquires wrongfully. A person is said to lose wrongfully when such person is wrongfully kept out of any property, as well as when such person is wrongfully deprived of property. Section 2(9) of BNS: “Fraudulently” means doing anything with the intention to defraud but not otherwise; Sec.2(21) of BNS: “Movable property” includes property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything which is attached to the earth. The property other than movable as above, can be treated as immovable property. It may be noted that while reporting about misappropriation in their reports, the inquiry/inspection officer/auditor shall necessarily fulfil the conditions earmarked to the terms: wrongful gain, wrongful loss, dishonestly, fraudulently. In the reporting, this terminology shall essentially be mentioned.

51 Case Example-1: AAA is the Chief Executive Officer of YYY cooperative society. He has taken an advance of Rs.100000/- on 28-02-2025 for purchase of fertilizers. He purchased fertilizers for 100 bags from a private firm MMM, filed the purchase bills, entered the transactions in the books of accounts by recording credit entry for Rs.100000/- towards his sundry debtor account. During the audit/inspection/inquiry it is found from the invoice that cost of each bag is Rs.800/- and total cost of 100 bags comes to Rs.80000/-. It was detected that an amount of Rs.20000/- was unaccounted. The said Chief Executive Officer has accounted his advance by irregular accounting practices. Reporting as Misappropriation : AAA is working as Chief Executive Officer of YYY cooperative society from 06-04-2018 to till date, vide his appointment order dated 04-04-2018 and his joining report dated 06-04- 2018. On 28-02-2025 there is an opening cash balance of Rs.165000/- in the society. He has given a voucher no.40 dated 28-02-2025 against the receipt of cash advance of Rs.100000/- for the purpose of purchase of fertilizer. He has purchased 100 bags of fertilizer from MMM firm, recorded the cost of fertilizer bags as Rs.100000/- by crediting to his sundry debtor (advance) account. On examination of purchase invoice issued by MMM firm, the purchase cost of each bag is Rs.800/- only, and as such the cost of 100 bags determined as Rs.80000/-. This infers that the fertilizer worth of Rs.20000/- not accounted to the society nor cash of Rs.20000/- was remitted into the society. Unaccounted amount of Rs.20000/- is wrongful loss to the society , since fertilizer worth of Rs.20000/- not accounted to the society. In accordance with Section 83-B of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, the AAA, Chief Executive Officer of YYY cooperative society is ought to account for the said advance of Rs.20000/- either by purchase of fertilizer or refund of cash, failing which it shall be construed that he has deemed to be misappropriated the said amount of Rs.20000/- which is wrongful gain to AAA. Since AAA has not purchased the fertilizer worth of Rs.20000/-; nor refunded the same to the society; not produced any evidence to the effect of usage of Rs.20000/- in favour of society; fraudulently entered Rs.100000/- being purchase cost instead of Rs.80000/- with intent to defraud the difference amount of Rs.20000/- for his own use. This amount of Rs.20000/- shall be restored to the society under due process of law.

52 Therefore, AAA, Chief Executive Officer of YYY cooperative society has misappropriated funds of YYY cooperative society to the tune of Rs.20000/- and shall be recovered from him under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 with a further interest @ 18% from the date of advance payment till the date of realization . [ii].Person fraudulently retained any money or other property : The key term herein is fraudulently. The property is not misappropriated, but possess with intent to defraud the same. Case Example-2: BBB is the President of XXX cooperative society. He has taken an advance of Rs.10000/- on 14-11-2024 for arrangement required facilities on the eve of Cooperative Week Celebrations in the society. After the event, the said President has given certain bills to the Chief Executive Officer for an amount of Rs.6000/- and stated he will refund the balance advance of Rs.4000/-. During the audit of 2024-2025, the auditor has conducted audit on 10-05-2025 and found that the said balance of Rs.4000/- not refunded by the President. The auditor has decided to enlist the said irregularity in his special report. Reporting as Fraudulent retention of money : BBB is functioning as President of XXX cooperative society from 06-04-2022 to till date, vide his election dated 06-04-2022. On 14-11-2024 there is an opening cash balance of Rs.15000/- in the society. He has given voucher no.32 dated 14-11-2024 against the receipt of cash advance of Rs.10000/- for the purpose of cooperative week celebrations. He has incurred expenditure to that effect and submitted vouchers/bills for an amount of Rs.6000/-. Since the purpose of advance was elapsed and the balance advance of Rs.4000/- was not repaid to the society till date, it shall be construed that BBB, President of XXX cooperative society has fraudulently retained Rs.4000/- with intent to defraud the same. This amount of Rs.4000/- shall be restored to the society under due process of law. Therefore, BBB, President of XXX cooperative society has fraudulently retained funds of XXX cooperative society to the tune of Rs.4000/- and shall be recovered from him under Section 60(1) of the

53 A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 with a further interest @ 18% from the date of advance payment till the date of realization . [iii].Person has been guilty of breach of trust in relation to the society : The key term herein is breach of trust in relation to the society. In order to understand objective of this phrase, one should know about the term “breach of trust”. Section 316 of BNS: Criminal breach of trust: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits “criminal breach of trust”. The breach of trust comes after entrustment of trust. So, it shall be reported on how the trust was entrusted, what provided for discharge of such trust. The financial irregularities involving misappropriation, dishonest use, dispose of property in violation of any direction of law can be reported under this category. Case Example-3 : The President of a KML Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, KML has paid an amount of Rs.175000.00 to an insurance company being the premium on behalf of health insurance of 5 employees working in the said society. Such a payment was made as direct payment from the bank balance funds. The Chief Executive Officer has processed the file for payment after the approval from the General Body and Managing Committee. All the employees have represented for getting the said payment. The auditor who conducted audit has objected this transaction and want to submit a special report aiming to restoration of such irregular expenditure. Reporting : The trust of management of the society was entrusted on the Managing Committee by way of election held in 2022. The trust of executive administration was entrusted on KKK, the Chief Executive Officer of society, by way of his appointment as Chief Executive Officer on

54 12.09.2016. As such the funds, properties of the society shall be in their control. In order to discharge such trust in the matter of sanction of financial benefits to the employees, the provisions of HR policy as in approved Service Byelaws are applicable. In accordance with these lawful provisions, the employees are not entitled for payment of health insurance premium from the funds of the society. Nevertheless, the Managing Committee and Chief Executive Officer of the society, have paid the health insurance premium of Rs.175000.00 on behalf of the said 5 employees by violating the provisions of HR policy as in approved Service Byelaws. The Chief Executive Officer and other employees have submitted representation on 02.04.2024 by requesting the payment of health insurance premium. The required applications signed by them, quotation of premium to be paid have also been enclosed to the said representation. The Managing Committee have resolved on 06.04.2024 for payment of health insurance premium of Rs.175000.00. The Chief Executive Officer has accordingly processed payment, and ultimately paid the amount through cheque jointly signed by the President and Chief Executive Officer on 17.04.2024. The payment of health insurance premium of Rs.175000.00 is an unlawful loss to the society, and also nothing but an unlawful gain to the said 5 employees. The Chief Executive Officer and the remaining 4 employees have instigated the Managing Committee to get the said ineligible financial benefit. Nor the said financial benefit was made out of such and specified fund allocated from net profit as per Section 44 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. Therefore, Managing Committee, said 5 employees of the society by breach of trust have dishonestly caused payment of health insurance premium of Rs.175000.00 in violation of the governing provisions of HR Policy and Service Byelaws and thereby also caused unlawful loss to KML Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Society Limited, KML to the tune of Rs.175000.00. Hence, the said irregular expenditure of Rs.175000.00 shall be recovered from the respective employees in terms of actuals of payment, since they have been provided with unlawful gain to the extent of premium paid against each. The interest @ 18% on Rs.175000.00 from the date of payment till the date of realization shall be recovered jointly severally from the Managing Committee members who approved the resolution for making such irregular payment of health insurance

55 premium. Accordingly, the restoration of funds shall be made under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. [iv].Person has caused any deficiency in the assets of the society by breach of trust or wilful negligence : The key terms to be known herein are ‘breach of trust’ and ‘wilful negligence’. The breach of trust was explained supra. The wilful negligence means knowingly doing the inconsistency. If any person within the meaning of Section 60(1), caused deficiency in the assets of the society by his intentional and purposeful omission, it can be called as wilful negligence. Wilfulness imports pre-medication of knowledge and consciousness that an injury or loss is likely to result from the act done or from the omission to act. In examining whether there is wilful negligence, it has to be seen first whether the person concerned is guilty of negligence and if so, whether that negligence is wilful and secondly, whether the person concerned is guilty of negligence and if so, whether the said wilful negligence is the proximate cause of the injury or loss sustained. In order to fix civil liability against a person entrusted with the organization and management of a cooperative society or an officer or servant thereof, such persons should have done an actionable wrong, either by commission or omission, in a deliberate and reprehensible manner, with reckless callousness and with a supine indifference, without taking due care and precaution ordinarily expected from a reasonable and prudent man under those existing circumstances. By examining all the above, it is easy to establish the guilty ‘by breach of trust’ rather than ‘by wilful negligence’. Case Example-4 : In DDD cooperative society, LT loan of Rs.500000/- was issued to a member. He defaulted in repayment. The OD interest levied for Rs.400000/-. The total loan dues are thus reached to Rs.900000/-. FFF, CEO of the said society, has reduced the loan dues to Rs.500000/- while repaying by the loanee, say for certain hidden benefits mutually agreed to. In an inspection, it was specifically detected by the inspection officer, and liable to report in his inspection report.

56 Reporting : (by wilful negligence) In DDD cooperative society, LT loan of Rs.500000/- was issued to a member. He defaulted in repayment. The OD interest levied for Rs.400000/-. The total loan dues are thus reached to Rs.900000/-. The duty of CEO is to collect Rs.900000/- from the respective borrower. This duty is entrusted to him by way of loan policy guidelines. The recovery of such loan is a duty known to him. He is further aware that he is not authorized as per law to reduce the loan dues recoverable to the society. However, FFF, CEO of the said society, has reduced the loan dues to Rs.500000/- while repaying by the loanee, which shall be considered as actionable wrong. He is very well aware that any reduction in such loan dues would result apparent loss to the society. In spite of all these repercussions, the said CEO has caused deficiency in the asset of the society to the tune of Rs.400000/- by wilful negligence, and such funds of Rs.400000/- are liable for restoration to society in due process of law. Therefore, FFF, CEO of DDD Cooperative Society has caused deficiency in the assets of the society by wilful negligence to the tune of Rs.400000.00, and the same shall be recovered from him under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, with a further interest @ 18% from the date of loan closure till the date of realization. Reporting : (by breach of trust) In DDD cooperative society, LT loan of Rs.500000/- was issued to a member. He defaulted in repayment. The OD interest levied for Rs.400000/-. The total loan dues are thus reached to Rs.900000/-. By appointment order dated 15-07-2014 the trust of duties of CEO was entrusted on FFF. The duty of CEO is to collect Rs.900000/- from the respective borrower. He has to discharge such trust of recovery of loan by way of loan policy guidelines. He is not authorized as per law to reduce the loan dues recoverable to the society. However, FFF, CEO of the said society, has reduced the loan dues to Rs.500000/- while repaying by the loanee. The said non repayment of Rs.400000/- is wrongful gain to the respective loanee. As a result, the society has incurred wrongful loss to the tune of Rs.400000/-. Therefore, the said CEO has caused deficiency in the asset of the society to the tune of Rs.400000/- by breach of trust, and such funds of Rs.400000/- are liable for restoration to society in due process of law.

57 Therefore, FFF, EO of DDD Cooperative Society has caused deficiency in the assets of the society by breach of trust to the tune of Rs.400000.00, and the same shall be recovered from him under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, with a further interest @ 18% from the date of loan closure till the date of realization. [v].Person has made any payment contrary to the provisions of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964; A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964; or the bye-laws: This category is convenient to report by inspection/inquiry officer or auditor. Any payment contrary to the provisions of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 or/and A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, or/and the bye-laws of society. Payment in respect of establishment expenditure in excess of the ceiling prescribed under Section 116-C(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 and Rule 36- B of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 is one example. [II].CRIMINAL LIABILITY : The institution of criminal action on the culprits as per the penal provisions laid down under A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, BNS, etc. shall have to be reported by the inquiry/inspection officers or auditors. Hence, they shall understand comprehensively about the said provisions. The important such provisions are mentioned below. While in the reports, the offences shall categorically be reported with diligence and care. (i).Section 79 of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964: (Main provisions) (1) It shall be an offence under this Act, if- (e) the committee or a member thereof wilfully fails to produce books, accounts, documents, records, securities, cash and other properties belonging to or in the custody of the society; (f) an officer, or an employee of the society including the paid Secretary dishonestly or fraudulently mis-appropriates or otherwise converts for his own use or intentionally causes loss to the property of the society entrusted to him or under his control as such officer or employee or allows any other person so to do;

58 (g) an officer, or an employee of the society is or proved to have been in possession of any property of the society reasonably suspected to have been stolen or unlawfully obtained; (ii).Section 79A of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964: (1) Where any officer of a society or an employee or a paid servant or any member of such society, — (a) commits any irregularity in receipt or sanction of loans or in purchases or causes, deficit in stocks; or (b) accepts or obtains or agrees to accept or attempts to obtain from any person for himself or for any other person, any gratification whatever, other than legal remuneration as a motive or reward for doing or forbearing to do any official act or showing or forbearing to show, in the exercise of his official functions or duties favour or disfavour to any person or rendering or attempting to render any service or dis-service to any person; or (c) uses or allows the use of funds of the society otherwise than in accordance with the provisions of this Act, rules or the bye-laws of the society; or (d) signs in the minutes books of committee meetings of the society without actually attending such meetings; or (e) passes a meeting without proper quorum as valid, he shall be deemed to be guilty of a corrupt practice in relation to the society. (iii).BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA, 2023 : Section 314: Dishonest misappropriation of property: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than six months but which may extend to two years and with fine. Section 316: Criminal breach of trust : Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law

59 prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits “criminal breach of trust”. (iv).CIVIL LIABILITY AND CRIMINAL LIABILITY ARE SEPARATELY UNIQUE: 1. This crucial factor to be kept in mind while fixing criminal liability against any delinquent. The probability of evidence is sufficient for fixation of civil liability whereas the availability of errorless evidence is alone required to fix criminal liability. In order to understand difference in between these two assumptions, one should first get clarity over the evidentiary status. 2. By without knowing these legal prerequisites, the inquiry/inspection officers/auditors have been simply recommending criminal liability by just mentioning the legal sections bereft of sufficient proof of foundational facts involved in the said legal sections. 3. Frankly speaking, many officers have just seen the sections and provisions in the Diaries and simply recommending criminal liability in their reports, by without writing sufficient details of offence and also by without substantiating the foundational facts involved in the commission of offence. These are the main reasons for failure of criminal cases in the Hon’ble Courts. 4. STANDARD EVIDENCE BY “PREPONDERANCE OF PROBABILITIES” : In civil liability cases, the evidence by preponderance is sufficient. Preponderance of probability is the standard of proof applied in civil cases, where a fact is said to be proved if its existence is more probable than its non-existence. It is based on Section 3 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, which requires the court to act upon facts that a prudent person would accept as probable. The civil liability can be decided basin on proof even not beyond reasonable doubt, in favour of the party whose version appears more likely on the balance of probabilities.

60 Burden of Proof and Shifting: • Initially, the plaintiff bears the burden (Section 101) • Once prima facie proof is shown, the burden shifts to the defendant. • If the defendant fails to rebut, the court may decide in favour of the plaintiff based on probabilities alone. 5. STANDARD EVIDENCE “BEYOND REASONABLE DOUBT”: Unlike civil cases, the standard proof must be established beyond reasonable doubt. Proof beyond reasonable doubt is the highest standard of proof known to law and is mandatorily applied in criminal trials. It means that the prosecution must establish the guilt of the accused with such certainty that no reasonable doubt remains in the mind of the court regarding commission of the offence, and involvement of the accused. If such reasonable doubt exists, the accused is entitled to benefit of doubt . ‘Presumption of Innocence’ come into play in such circumstances. Burden of Proof: • The entire burden lies on the prosecution. • The burden never shifts to the accused. • The accused is not required to prove innocence. • Even a single reasonable doubt is sufficient to result in acquittal . 6. Therefore, the inquiry/inspection officers/auditors shall recommend criminal liability only in the event of having highest standard of proof beyond reasonable doubt to establish the guilty on the delinquent. Such evidence is not possible to secure due to certain limitations. In such cases, recommend only civil liability. If no evidence with such highest standard, there shall be no recommendation of criminal liability. Even single reasonable doubt shall not appear while instituting the criminal liability. 7. The reporting of criminal liability is only informative basis. The actual reporting shall be done by the Divisional Cooperative Officer. Hence, the section staff shall necessarily examine the reports with the

61 testament of aforementioned evaluation. Upon fulfilment of aforesaid provisions, further action needs to be proceeded therefor. (V).REPORT ON MISAPPROPRIATION : The report on misappropriation shall be written under two parts: A and B. PART-A: 1. Preliminary. 2.Delinquents. 3.Items of fraud. 4.Legal offences. 5.Investigation. 6.Motive for the Crime. 7.Evidence. PART-B: 1. Course of investigation from detection to reporting. 2. Explain about no more frauds exist than reported under part- A. 3. About misappropriation, its effect on financial position, recovery, etc. 4. About office bearers who worked during the period of frauds. 5.About dept officers who visited the society and about non detection. 6.About officials of financing bank or its apex societies who visited the society and about non detection. [III]. ADMINISTRATIVE LIABILITY : The actions required to be taken up by the department, management of the society to rectify the inconsistencies reported in the inquiry/inspection/audit/special reports. The Registrar shall issue order under Section 54 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. Case Example-5 : VVVVV has got dual membership both in AAA Non- Gazetted Officers Cooperative Building Society Limited and in BBB State Govt Employees CHBS Ltd. This is one among the terms of inquiry. Reporting : VVVVV has got admission in AAA Cooperative Building Society Limited with G.No.5414 on 21-04-1993. He was allotted with house plot no.4418 on 25.3.2021 from AAA Cooperative Building Society

62 Limited. He has also got admission in BBB CHBS Ltd with G.No.2084 on 20-3-2017. In accordance with the provisions laid down under Section 19(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 read with Bye Law No.5, any person who already possessed with membership in a cooperative house building society is not eligible to seek admission in another Cooperative Building Society. The members who have taken admission by violating these provisions, shall automatically acquire disqualification under Section 21(aa) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 to continue as member and are liable for removal from membership in accordance with the procedure laid down under Section 21(3) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. Since he was already a member of AAA Cooperative Building Society Limited as on 20-3-2017, he is not eligible to get admission in BBB CHBS Ltd. The Managing Committee of BBB CHBS Ltd shall take necessary action for removal of VVVVV from the membership of BBB CHBS Ltd by duly following the procedure as per law. Case Example-6 : During the course of inquiry, the inquiry officer has noticed that a s per Bye Law No.37(e), the quorum for the general meeting shall be 1/5 th of the total number of members on rolls or 150 whichever is less. By using this provision, the society has succeeded in getting registration of amendment to bye law, of which stood as basis behind occurrence of a serious irregularity in a cooperative building society. Reporting : It is noticed that there were 10086 members on rolls as on 31- 3-2017 vide FAR of 2016-2017. As such, the society is having 10086 members on rolls as on date of General Body meeting held on 24-9-2017. The quorum prescribed under law is 1/10 th of total members, i.e., 1009 (1008.6). But the members attended and signed the proceedings are only 469, and hence, the General Body meeting held on 24-9-2017 is not valid and contrary to the provisions laid down under Section 32 of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, Rule 23(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964. In order to approve the amendment, the resolution must be passed by not less than 673 (2/3 rd of 1009) members when the general meeting assumed to be conducted with ultimate minimum quorum of 1009. But it was passed only by 469 members, as such the said resolution for amendment of Bye Law No.42-A is illegal. As per Bye Law No.37(e), the quorum for the general meeting shall be 1/5 th of the total

63 number of members on rolls or 150 whichever is less. There lies inconsistency in between Bye Law No.37(e) and Rule 23(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964, where latter prevails over former, hence, the quorum limited to 150 members is illegal, and validating amendment proposal on such illegal standing is ipso facto unlawful. Therefore, the proposal submitted by the President, AAA Cooperative Building Society Limited as well as the Procs.Rc.No.1711/2017-E, Dated 29-3-2019 of the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies in which issued orders duly amending Bye Law No.42(a) were based on aforesaid illegal General Body resolution, hence void under law. Therefore, the amendment of bye law no.42-A is bad at law and not liable for enforcement. The Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies, is requested to issue specific order under Section 54 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, specifying rectification of defective quorum existing in Bye Law No.37(e), by duly following the provisions of Section 16(5) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964.

64 MODULE-5: SURCHARGE It is very unfortunate to cite that the surcharge orders passed by the Deputy Registrars have been setting aside by Hon’ble A.P.Cooperative Tribunal at Vijayawada or Hon’ble High Court of Andhra Pradesh, mainly because of technical defects, procedural lapses. Though efforts were made from our Head of the Department in the past, true procedural compliance still not attained in majority cases. [A].SECTION 60 OF A.P.COOPERATIVE SOCIETIES ACT, 1964: [ BASIS ]: Notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force where in the course of an audit under Section 50 or an inquiry under Section 51 or an inspection under Section 52 or Section 53, to the winding up of a society, it appears that: [ WHO ]: [1].Any person who is or was entrusted with the organisation, affairs or management of the society or [2].any past or present officer or servant of the society. Section 2(k): ‘Officer’ includes a person elected or appointed by a society to any office of such society according to its bye-laws and a president, vice president, chairman, vice-chairman, secretary, assistant secretary, treasurer, manager, member of committee, liquidator or any other person elected or appointed under this Act, Rules or the bye-laws to give directions in regard to the business of the society. [ DONE WHAT ]: has [1].misappropriated; or [2].fraudulently retained any money or other property; or [3].has been guilty of breach of trust in relation to the society; or [4].has caused any deficiency in the assets of the society by breach of trust or wilful negligence; or [5].has made any payment contrary to the provisions of this Act, the rules or the bye-laws, [ SURCHARGE AUTHORITY ]: the Registrar himself, or any person specially authorised by him in this behalf, of his own motion or on the application of the committee, liquidator or any creditor or contributor, may inquire:

65 [ INQUIRES WHAT ]: into the conduct of such person or officer or servant and make an order requiring him to repay or restore the money or property or any part thereof with interest at such rate as the Registrar or the person authorised as aforesaid thinks just or to contribute such sum to the assets of the society by way of compensation in respect of the misappropriation, misapplication of funds, fraudulent retention, breach of trust, or wilful negligence as the Registrar or the person authorised as aforesaid thinks just : [ OPPORTUNITY ]: Provided that no order shall be passed against any person referred to in this sub-section unless the person concerned has been given an opportunity of making his representation. [B].PRINCIPLES OF NATURAL JUSTICE OR PROVIDING REASONABLE OPPORTUNITY : The majority of surcharge orders are getting set aside due to violation of principles of natural justice or non- providing reasonable opportunity. All departmental officers shall know keen about these legal prerequisites. 1. Natural Justice refers to fundamental rules of fair play, equity, and good conscience which must be followed by courts, tribunals, and administrative authorities while taking decisions affecting rights of individuals. It is not codified, but is inherent in Article 14 (Equality) and Article 21 (Procedure established by law) of the Constitution of India. 2. The principles of natural justice, rooted in the concept of fairness, ensure a just and impartial legal process, encompassing the right to a fair hearing ("audi alteram partem") and the rule against bias ("nemo judex in causa sua"). The following are more detailed explanations: 3. "Audi Alteram Partem" (Right to be Heard): This principle means that no one should be condemned unheard, and individuals have the right to present their case and defend themselves before a decision is made against them. Literally, it mandates “Hear the Other side”. The concept of “Fair Hearing” is inflicted into the judicial proceedings to deal with this legal obligation. The proceedings of

66 Fair Hearing provide certain rights to the delinquents/accused persons. (a) Right to Notice: Clear, specific, and reasonable notice of charges. (b) Right to Know the Evidence: Supply of documents relied upon. Access to material used against the person. (b) Right to Present One’s Case: Opportunity to submit explanation and evidence. (c) Right to Cross-Examination: Where statements are relied upon. (d) Right to Reasoned Order: Decision-makers should provide reasons for their decisions, promoting transparency and accountability. [The above principles have been set forth in certain Leading Case Laws: Ridge v. Baldwin (1964); State of Orissa v. Dr. Binapani Dei (1967); Mohinder Singh Gill v. Chief Election Commissioner (1978)] 4. Therefore, a fair hearing involves cardinal aspects of providing adequate notice, a reasonable opportunity to be heard, and the right to present evidence and cross-examine witnesses. 5. "Nemo Judex in Causa Sua" (No One Should Be a Judge in Their Own Case): This principle emphasizes the importance of impartiality, ensuring that decision-makers are free from bias or any personal interest in the outcome of a case. 6. Impartiality: Decision-makers must be free from bias or any appearance of bias, ensuring that decisions are made fairly and objectively. 7. Duty to Act Expeditiously: Decisions should be made within a reasonable timeframe, avoiding undue delays that could prejudice the parties involved.

67 8. Fairness: The principles of natural justice are designed to ensure that legal processes are fair and just, protecting the rights and interests of individuals. 9. All the above principles need to be followed in establishing the provision of reasonable opportunity. Now one can perceive why the surcharge orders are being remanded back frequently. It is apparent that the surcharge orders are materializing without fulfilling the aforementioned precedents. 10. In fact, the following stages among others were devised in the Civil Procedure Code,1908 to ensure fulfilment of principles of natural justice. However, the said procedures could not be implemented as it is in surcharge proceedings. With suitable changes, such stages shall be completed in surcharge proceedings to ensure fulfilment of the provision of reasonable opportunity as provided under Section 60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. [C].DOCKET SHEET : 1. The Hon’ble Courts are examining the LCR in order to ascertain the fulfilment of various stages required to be taken up in the surcharge proceedings. 2. In Civil Courts, the docket sheet wrapped up with case file and recording of various actions thereupon by the Learned Judges have been considering appropriate in the higher-level courts, since they are with judicial qualifications with apt professionalization. 3. However, the Hon’ble courts are examining our files as if entertained by the authorities having no law qualifications and weighing our performance being the non-professional authorities. 4. As such, it is necessary to record the docket sheet in detail depicting various stages with sufficient signatures of parties. Every aspect relating to surcharge shall be recorded in the docket sheet.

68 5. It is also necessary to reflect in the surcharge orders to show case fulfillment of all stages involved therein. 6. Majority of defence counsels are succeeding in our cases by basing on procedural insufficiency. This could be alleviated by duly reflecting our actions both in docket sheet and surcharge order, which ultimately substantiate compliance to Principles of Natural Justice. Model docket sheet is appended for appropriate guidance. [D].STAGES OF SURCHARGE : 1. In fact, no expressive procedure has been prescribed under Section 60 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, on how to conduct surcharge inquiry. 2. The Hon’ble Courts are scrutinizing the surcharge orders on the fulfilment of Principles of Natural Justice in terms of the stages enshrined under Civil Procedure Code. 3. Majority of surcharge orders have been remanding back on the reason of providing no reasonable opportunity and violation of principles of natural justice. 4. Therefore, it is essential to reflect in the surcharge orders about the stages laid down in CPC in order to substantiate the fulfillment of providing reasonable opportunity to both the sides with absolute fairness. 5. It may be kept in mind that the Surcharge Authority is meant to conduct “ independent inquiry”. 6. The Surcharge Authority shall not incline towards any side of the parties, including the Department and plaintiff cooperative society. Strictly speaking, the surcharge authorities shall not incline towards proving the findings of inquiry report/inspection report/audit report on the pretext that the same was done by the departmental officer and such an action favours the plaintiff society.

69 7. As such, the surcharge authority shall deliver surcharge order by preponderance of evidence established by one party over the other party, and not otherwise. 8. The following are the stages of surcharge to be followed by the surcharge authorities to avoid setting aside of orders and remanding backs. The following stages are devised at relevant levels with the sole objective of providing reasonable opportunity to both the sides to ensure fair, impartial and independent surcharge proceedings. 9. Though the surcharge inquiry is conducting by the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies and issues the surcharge order, the Assistant Registrars and Assistants of offices should also know about the procedures involved therein so as to associate with the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies. 10. Ensure that the Assistant Registrar or Assistant shall not endorse initials in the surcharge notices and surcharge orders. However, they can prepare under the instructions of the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies and put up the same for decision and action. (1).PLAINTIFF AFFIDAVIT : 1. Though the inquiry/inspection report or special audit report/audit report serve as plaint document, it is always safe to involve the officials of plaintiff society. If anything, adverse happened to the society, there is every possibility that the society may approach the Court of law stating that no opportunity provided for submission of pleadings on the part of plaintiff society. 2. Consequent on communication of inquiry report/inspection report/special audit report, a summon shall be issued to the chief executive or/and president of the plaintiff society by requiring them to file a Plaintiff Affidavit basing on the findings of inquiry report/inspection report/audit report/special report, as the case may be. 3. Order VII, Rule 1 CPC deals with submission of plaint by the plaintiff. The civil case proceedings will usually be commenced out of this plaint. However, this procedure is altogether different in surcharge

70 proceedings, since the latter materialize on the basis of inquiry report/inspection report/audit report/special report. Hence, getting ‘Plaintiff Affidavit’ would fulfil this legal obligation. 4. Nevertheless, the inquiry report/inspection report/audit report/ special report shall be considered as plaint document to commence the surcharge proceedings. Plaintiff Affidavit, if filed, be treated as part of plaint document. (2).PREPARATION AND ISSUE OF SURCHARGE NOTICE : 1. The surcharge notice shall be prepared carefully encompassing all the details enumerated in the inquiry/inspection/audit report as well as plaintiff affidavit of the plaintiff society, if filed. Order IV of CPC deals with scrutiny of plaint documents. Hence, it is necessary to thoroughly examine the inquiry/inspection/audit report/special report as well as plaintiff affidavit before taking of the same into cognizance to commence surcharge proceedings on behalf of the plaintiff society. 2. In many cases, the surcharge notices are preparing in just 1 or 2 pages without sufficient details. It is wrong and insufficient. Surcharge notice is intended for the purpose of calling written statement of defence from the delinquents. Giving insufficient information in surcharge notice could not enable the delinquents to submit appropriate defence from their side, and it comes under providing no reasonable opportunity. 3. Order V of CPC prescribes to issue summons to the defendants by requiring them to file written statement of defence and appear for hearing. However, in surcharge proceedings, the Registrar issues surcharge notice instead of summon. The purpose is same in both the cases. 4. The first surcharge notice prepared is ultimate and shall not be issued second time. Even in the remanded back cases also, the surcharge notice issued at first instance holds good, and subsequent events only to be conducted afresh. The Hon’ble courts are now considering these aspects with serious concern.

71 5. It is very important to know that the date of hearing for both the parties shall be fixed in the surcharge notice. It may be ensured that there exist 30 clear days in between issue of surcharge notice and such hearing. In the subsequent events notice period may be limited to 2 weeks. 6. The surcharge notice shall be communicated properly, obtain dated acknowledgements in token of receipt of surcharge notice by both the parties, and file in the record. Even if it is returned by post or rejected by delinquent, it is acceptable for further action and such details shall be necessarily recorded in the docket sheet duly filing the evidence. 7. Take note that by without serving the surcharge notice in the prescribed manner, further proceedings could not be conducted and the surcharge order shall not be passed. 8. Hence, the communication of details of irregularities to the extent reasonable, calling on the delinquents to appear in person on the specified date, calling on submission of written statement of defence from defendants shall be the core ingredients of the surcharge notice. The copy of inquiry report/inspection report/audit report/special report (at least without enclosures) shall be enclosed to the surcharge notice. (3).WRITTEN STATEMENT OF DEFENCE BY DEFENDANTS : 1. Order VIII, Rule 1 CPC deals with the filing of written statement by the delinquents, in compliance to the surcharge notice issued to that effect. The time of 30 days shall be provided to the delinquents to file their written statement. However, this shall be extendable on the reasonable request from the delinquents, but not beyond 90 days. 2. It is the opportunity provided to the defendants to prove their innocence. The delinquents shall necessarily file on or before specified date, their written statement of defence wherein they could ought to represent their grounds of rebutting against the facts in this regard.

72 3. It shall not be sufficient for a delinquent in his written statement to deny generally the grounds alleged in the surcharge notice including findings/facts in the evidences, but the delinquent must deal specifically with each allegation of fact of which he does not admit the truth. 4. It is also informed that where the delinquent bases his defence upon a document in support of his defence or claim for set-off or counter- claim, he shall enter such document in a list, and shall produce it before the surcharge authority when the written statement is presented by him and shall, at the same time, deliver the document and a copy thereof, to be filed with the written statement. 5. On or before the specified day of hearing, it is necessary to ascertain filing of written statements. If not filed, another opportunity may be provided at the request of the delinquents. If the delinquents file the written statement, the same shall be signed, and filed to the record. If the delinquent not attends before the surcharge authority together with non-submission of the written statement, his/her case shall be treated as ex-party. (4) . INITIAL EXAMINATION OF PARTIES : 1. Order X of CPC deals with first hearing and initial examination of parties. The surcharge authority after satisfying with the propriety of plaint and written statement, shall fix the date for first hearing of the independent inquiry for the purpose of initial examination of the parties. 2. The surcharge authority would examine both the parties to ascertain the matters of dispute/controversy. 3. The surcharge authority first takes the stand of the plaintiff society representative and inquiry officer/inspection officer/auditor as to they still rely on the contents and findings laid down under plaint and inquiry/ inspection/ audit report. Their response must be recorded in the docket sheet.

73 4. Then the surcharge authority shall read over in brief the allegations/ averments from the plaint documents and takes the stand of the defendant side as to their admitting or denial of such allegations/ averments. The same shall be recorded in the docket sheet. 5. Consequent to hearing of both sides, if the delinquent side accepts the allegations, then deduce such statements in the form of sworn depositions and file in the record. In such case, it is not necessary to follow the stages as follows. However, the plaintiff side shall be examined, recorded their depositions, mark the evidences and file on to the record for passing the order. 6. In the event of non-production of written statements of defence and non-appearance of the delinquents for at least 3 hearings, the case shall be made ex-parte. In such case also, examination of plaintiff side, recording their sworn depositions, marking evidence shall be completed to pass surcharge order. Ensure all the events be recorded in the docket sheet. (5).FRAMING OF ISSUES : 1. Order XIV of CPC deals with framing of issues. The surcharge authority shall frame the issues basing on the plaint and written statement. The issues may be of ‘issues of fact’ or/and ‘issues of law’. 2. In the event of denial of the allegations/averments by the delinquents, then contrary prevails over therewith. In such instances, the surcharge case shall be posted for trial, by duly framing the issues to be inquired in the said surcharge proceedings. 3. The surcharge proceedings shall as far as possible be conducted in and around these framed issues. (6).EXAMINATION OF PLAINTIFF SIDE & CROSS EXAMINATION BY DEFENDANT SIDE : 1. Order XVIII, Rule 4 of CPC deals with this stage of which is intended for leading of evidence by Plaintiff side.

74 2. During the trial proceedings, the inquiry/inspection officer or auditor shall become the plaintiff witness. In many cases, the surcharge authorities are not taking the evidence from the officer who conducted inquiry/inspection/audit. The Hon’ble Courts are pointing this inadequacy on the part of surcharge authorities. 3. The inquiry/inspection officer/auditor shall be called on to the trial proceedings by duly communicating the notice, examine him in trial, deduce his statement in the form of sworn deposition, mark the evidences proved by him. His deposition shall be treated as “Affidavit-in-Examination of Chief” of “Chief Affidavit” or “Chief Evidence”. 4. Then allow the defendants or their counsel to cross examine the deponent and record the cross-examination proceedings in the form of interrogatories. All the events must be recorded in docket sheet. 5. The plaintiff representative shall also be examined, deduce his/her statement as sworn deposition, allow the defendants or their counsel to cross examine the deponent and record all the events in docket sheet. The same procedure shall be followed in respect of other plaintiff witnesses. 6. In many cases the delinquents are challenging in the Courts that opportunity for cross examination not provided to them, and obtaining stay orders or remand backs. 7. The aspect of cross examination is more important in surcharge proceedings and core basis behind fair and impartial trial. The opportunity of cross examination shall be provided to both the parties who participated in the trial proceedings. 8. However, the cross examination shall be performed by the counsels appointed by the parties. If anyone did not appoint Advocate/Counsel, such person is not allowed to straight away cross examine the deponent, instead he/she explain their objections to the notice of the surcharge authority who in turn interrogates the deponent for counter statement.

75 9. All the events shall be recorded in the docket sheet, deposition and obtain the signatures of such parties. If any side states nil cross examination against the other side, such instance shall be necessarily recorded in docket sheet as well as at the end of the sworn deposition of the deponent. 10. It is quite often the authorities using the words ‘evidences available on record’ for the entire leaves of the file maintained in the office. It is wrong perception. 11. Material brought on to the record with sufficient marking only constitutes ‘evidences available on record’. The evidences marked in the prescribed manner shall only be considerable as evidence to decide the allegation/ dispute/issue, and not others. The Hon’ble Courts are setting aside the surcharge orders on the reason of evidences not marked. 12. In general, the plaintiff side documents to be marked shall be submitted while filing the plaint. This stage is not practically possible in surcharge proceedings, since the inquiry/inspection/audit reports are becoming the major plaint documents, and hence, possible to mark evidences on their part in surcharge proceedings. However, the defendants shall file their documents along with written statement of defence. 13. The marking of evidence involves production, examination and proof. Such documents shall be marked with relevant stamping over such documents. 14. The production of document by whom shall be recorded. The examination of such document shall be performed in the trial proceedings as to ensure its propriety in all respects. The proof of such document is also necessary, since the plaintiff side/ defendant side, as the case may be shall accept existence and genuineness of such document. All these details are properly stamped on the document. 15. The marked documents are called as exhibits. Each exhibit must possess with a unique number. The plaintiff side exhibits can be numbered as Ex.No.P1, Ex.No.P2, and so on. The delinquent side

76 exhibits can be numbered as Ex.No.D1, Ex.No.D2, and so on. The model marking of evidences is appended herewith. 16. Once the examination of plaintiff, plaintiff witnesses, their cross examination, and marking of evidences is over, then the surcharge authority shall close the evidence on the part of plaintiff side. (7).EXAMINATION OF DEFENDANT SIDE & CROSS EXAMINATION BY PLAINTIFF SIDE: 1. Order XVIII of CPC deals with this stage of which is intended for leading of evidence by Delinquent side. 2. During the trial proceedings, the defendants and their witnesses shall be examined, deduce his/her statement as sworn deposition, mark the evidences proved, allow the plaintiff or its counsel to cross examine the deponent and record the cross-examination proceedings in the form of interrogatories. If there lies no cross examination, record ‘nil cross examination from delinquent side’. All the events in docket sheet. 3. Consequent to the examination of defendants, defendant witnesses, their cross examination, and marking of evidences, then the surcharge authority shall close the evidence on the part of delinquent side. (8).ARGUMENTS FROM BOTH THE PARTIES: 1. Order XVIII, Rule 2 of CPC deals with the events of this stage. Subsequent to the completion of aforementioned stages, the surcharge authority shall post the case for arguments by fixing the date of hearing. 2. On the day fixed for arguments, both the parties are allowed to advocate the arguments from their side. 3. Any material case laws to the effect stated in arguments the same could be placed before the surcharge authority for consideration. The parties can also opt to file written arguments.

77 4. During hearing of the arguments, the surcharge authority shall make note of important and substantive grounds advanced by the parties for consideration at the time of passing the order. Consequent on completion of arguments, the case shall be posted for pronouncing the Surcharge Order. (9).SURCHARGE ORDER : 1. Order XX of CPC deals with passing of Order in civil cases. 2. On the day fixed for pronouncement of the Surcharge Order, the Surcharge Authority shall deliver the Surcharge Order in the open court. 3. Subsequently, the said order typo graphed, signed and issued for communication to both the parties. The model surcharge order is appended herewith. 4. The surcharge orders drafted in 1 or 2 pages also seen, which is not tenable. It may be ascertained that the surcharge orders passed after fulfilling the above stages would withstand the legal scrutiny of the Hon ’ ble Courts. The surcharge orders cannot be set aside with ease and the only probability is to go into the details of frauds of which in general, the departmental officers mention with strong points backed by substantive and sufficient evidences. 5. Finally reiterated that the surcharge order must be reasoned order. 6. It may also be observed that the Counsels working aside the delinquents often stating before the Hon ’ ble Courts that the prescribed stages of civil case proceedings not followed by the surcharge authority. The counsels are showing the one page/ two- page surcharge orders as basis behind such pleadings. They are also pleading such contentions by pointing out the contents of the surcharge order. As such, it is always necessary to describe the fulfilment of all aforementioned stages of proceedings in the surcharge order.

78 7. The surcharge order must be drafted in such a way that the flow of events shall be recorded properly, like about inquiry/inspection/audit report and its findings; filing of plaintiff affidavit; issue of surcharge notice; filing of written statement; initial examination of both the parties; framing of issues; posting of trial; examination of plaintiff side; marking of plaintiff side evidences; cross-examination of plaintiff side by the delinquent side; closing of evidence from plaintiff side; examination of delinquent side; marking of delinquent side evidences; cross-examination of delinquent side by the plaintiff side; closing of evidence from delinquent side; posting of case for arguments; arguments advanced by the parties; posting of case for surcharge order.

79 MODULE-6: ARBITRATION [I].ACTION AT OFFICE LEVEL: The person who seeks settlement of any dispute may prefer an application before the Registrar. Section 61(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 specified about the disputes which may be entertained for resolution. (1) Notwithstanding anything in any law for the time being in force, if any dispute touching the constitution, management or the business of a society, other than a dispute regarding disciplinary action taken by the society or its committee against a paid employee of the society, arises- (a) among members, past members and persons claiming through members, past members and deceased members; or (b) between a member, past member or person claiming through a member, past member or deceased member and the society, its committee or any officer, agent or employee of the society; or (c) between the society or its committee, and any past committee, any officer, agent or employee, or any past officer, past agent, or past employee or the nominee, heir or legal representative of any deceased officer, deceased agent or deceased employee of the society; or (d) between the society and any other society, such dispute shall be referred to the Registrar for decision. (2) If any question arises whether a dispute referred to the Registrar under this section is a dispute touching the constitution, management or the business of a society, such question shall be decided by the Registrar. (3) Every dispute relating to, or in connection with, any election to a committee of a society shall be referred for decision to the Tribunal having jurisdiction over the place where the main office of the society is situated, whose decision thereon shall be final. (4) Every dispute relating to, or in connection with any election shall be referred under sub-section (3) only after the date of declaration of the result of such election.

80 By examining the application in terms of compliance to above provisions, the file shall be opened afresh by duly assigning the arbitration number. Section 62 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 states about action to be taken by the Registrar on arbitration reference. (1) The Registrar may, on receipt of the reference of a dispute under Section 61: (a) elect to decide the dispute himself; or (b) transfer it for disposal to any person who has been invested by the Government with powers in that behalf ; or (c) refer it for disposal to an arbitrator. (2) Where the reference relates to any dispute involving immovable property, he Registrar or such person or arbitrator may order that any person be joined as a party who has acquired an interest in such property subsequent to the acquisition of interest therein by a party to the reference and any decision that may be passed on the reference by the Registrar or the person or the arbitrator aforesaid, shall be binding on the party so joined as if he were an original party to the reference. (3) The Registrar may, by order for reasons to be recorded therein, withdraw any reference transferred under Clause (b) of sub-section (1) or referred under Clause (c) of that sub-section and may elect to decide the dispute himself or transfer it to any other person under Clause (b) of sub- section (1) or refer it to any other arbitrator under Clause (c) of that sub- section. (4) The Registrar, such person or arbitrator shall decide the dispute in accordance with the provisions of this Act and the rules and bye-laws and such decision shall, subject to the provisions of Section 76, be final. Pending final decision on the dispute, the Registrar, such person or arbitrator, as the case may be, may make such interlocutory orders as he may deem necessary in the interest of justice.

81 As per the decision of the Registrar, the dispute will be settled by him suo moto or through an arbitrator. If chosen for arbitrator, order to that effect shall be issued by the Registrar. [II].ACTION AT ARBITRATOR LEVEL: The action at arbitrator level is more or less synonymous with the stages of surcharge proceedings explained in preceding paragraphs, provided with following differences to be followed in arbitration proceedings. All the stages mentioned for surcharge are equally essential in arbitration proceedings culminated with following changes. 1. The term ‘surcharge’ shall not be used. Instead, the term ‘arbitration’ be used. In surcharge, only monetary allegations will be inquired whereas settlement of both monetary and non-monetary disputes is possible under arbitration proceedings. 2. The statutory prerequisites like inquiry/inspection/audit reports shall not be necessary. 3. Arbitration proceedings shall be conducted strictly in accordance with the procedures laid down under CPC. The arbitrator shall act in crystal clear independent state. 4. The application filed for settlement of dispute shall be treated as plaint document. Hence, the documents to be relied by the plaintiff shall necessarily enclose to the plaint application. The disputes referred to in plaint shall only be settled. 5. The ‘summons’ shall be substituted in the place of ‘surcharge notices’ so as to call the parties to the hearings. 6. The arbitrator will issue “Award”, whereas ‘surcharge order’ is passed in surcharge proceedings. The Arbitrator shall handover complete case file together with Award in original to the Registrar. If needed he can retain xerox copy with him.

82 NOTE : This booklet is prepared not to give complete law, since one can read the same from bare acts. The utilization of legal provisions has been made minimal and importance given to practical aspects. That means, importance given to ‘what to do consequent to law’ rather than ‘what to know about law’. So, officers are requested to use this booklet by duly conjoining the bare acts. I prepared this booklet involving think tank and drafting on my own. The models provided herein are directive as well as suggestive. Hence, requested to suitably modify by the needy persons duly changing required details. The guidelines, procedures, etc., already given in manuals, circulars, directions, etc., have not been reiterated, since the same are in force. Any suggestions from anybody are appreciated. Thanks & Regards. J. SURESH KUMAR M.Sc., H.D.C.M. DIVISIONAL COOPERATIVE OFFICER, TIRUPATI
