2 This Surcharge Case came up finally on 30-11-2023 for hearing before me in the presence of representative of plaintiff and delinquents and having stood over for consideration till this day, this Surcharge Authority delivered the following: SURCHARGE ORDER [1]. A.Pavan Kumar, Assistant Registrar, Office of the Sub Divisional Cooperative Officer, Badvel has conducted audit of accounts of Andhra Pragathi Farmers Service Cooperative Society Ltd., Thallaproddatur under Sec.50 of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, for the financial year 2020-2021 and submitted a Special Report. In his Lr.Rc.No.1379/2021/C, Dated: 15-09-2021, the District Cooperative Audit Officer, Kadapa has communicated the special report for further action in this matter. In accordance with the delegation of powers ordered in G.O.Ms.No.34, Food and Agriculture (Coop.IV) Dept., dated: 18-01-1989, the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Proddatur is competent authority to conduct surcharge proceedings under Sec.60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. [2]. Brief Facts of the Case: Whereas in his Lr, Dated: NIL addressed to the District Cooperative Officer, Kadapa, A.Pavan Kumar, Assistant Registrar, Office of the Sub Divisional Cooperative Officer, Badvel has submitted special report by reporting certain financial irregularities occurred in the affairs of Andhra Pragathi Farmers Service Cooperative Society Ltd., Thallaproddatur during the financial year 2020-2021. The irregularities include payment of excess gratuity of Rs.1185188.00 to P.Ganganna, retired employee; irregular payment of DA/IR of Rs.497592.00 to employees; irregular payment of medical expenses of Rs.8000.00 without having provision for such purpose; irregular payment of ex-gratia of Rs.182680.00 to employees; irregular payment of Rs.91432.00 for being encashment of privilege leave to employees; and irregular transport and levelling charges of Rs.410385.00. The said irregularities have also been reported under Part-A defects sheet of FAR No.390/2020-2021 of plaintiff society for 2020-2021. [3]. The surcharge notice was issued on 31.5.2023 to the delinquents, plaintiff, by requiring them to appear personally before the surcharge authority on 05.7.2023 for the purpose of the inquiry under Sec.60(1) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies

3 Act, 1964. The 2 nd to 4 th delinquents have attended for hearing on 05.7.2023 and filed their joint written statement of defence on 05.7.2023. The delinquent-1 has abstained on 05.7.2023 nor submitted her written statement. The Auditor/Plaintiff witness is also abstained as he was under medical leave due to heart surgery. Hence, further hearing postponed to 05.10.2023 and then to 01.11.2023. The 2 nd and 4 th delinquents have attended to the hearing on 01.11.2023, and 1 st and 3 rd delinquents were absent. Upon initial examination held on 01-11-2023, the delinquents so attended have stated to consider the details mentioned in their written statements and the same holds good further, of which found inconsistent with the findings of special report. As inconsistency exists in between the pleas of both parties, it is required to resolve disputed issues involved therein. Thereby, the case posted for trial proceedings to inquire into the contentious issues. [4]. In the trial proceedings held on 01-11-2023, 2 nd Delinquent was examined and permitted him to give evidence by sworn deposition and to adduce proof of documentary evidences. Thereby, he has given oral evidence and the same was recorded as his sworn deposition. Consequently, the plaintiff who attended the hearing has been provided with the opportunity to cross examine the deponent. The plaintiff stated nil cross examination. In the trial proceedings held on 21-11- 2023, the Auditor/Plaintiff Witness was examined and recorded his sworn deposition. The delinquents who attended to the said hearing were provided the opportunity to cross examine the plaintiff witness. The delinquents have stated nil cross examination on their behalf. The Delinquents-3,4 were examined and permitted them to give evidence by sworn depositions and to adduce proof of documentary evidences. Thereby, they have given oral evidence on 21.11.2023 and the same were recorded as their sworn depositions. Consequently, the plaintiff who attended the hearing has been provided with the opportunity to cross examine these deponents. The plaintiff stated nil cross examination. The 1 st delinquent has not at all attended to the hearings held nor submitted her written statement of defence, hence shall be treated as ex-parte. The details of witnesses and evidence are mentioned in the annexure appended to this order. The case is further posted to 30.11.2023 for arguments. On the day fixed for arguments, i.e., on 30.11.2023, the plaintiff side has not adduced any

4 arguments. The delinquents-4 has adduced oral arguments of which have been recorded. Thereafter, the case is posted to 21.12.2023, and then to 31-01-2024 for Order. [5]. In their joint written statement, the delinquents-2,3,4 have stated their grounds of defence allegation wise upon the allegations levelled in the surcharge notice dated 31.5.2023. The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that P.Ganganna, employee was retired from service on 31-7-2020; paid retirement benefits as indicated under Staff Service Regulations, 1985; paid such benefits with the approval of then Person-in- Charge committee; emoluments paid: gratuity of Rs.972801.00, leave encashment value of Rs.226440.00 for 102 days; arrear salaries from Nov-2019 to Jan-2020 of Rs.185947.00, total of Rs.1385188.00; heads of accounts for leave encashment and arrear salaries shown under reserves/funds by mistake and those can be reclassified by adjustment transactions; and ultimately requested to drop this allegation. The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that the DA/IR amounts were paid to its employees after obtaining Person-in-Charge committee approval; the account head was mistakenly entered under reserve fund and of which could be rectified by reclassification; the said expenditure formed within the limit prescribed under cost limitation; and ultimately requested to drop this allegation. The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that the medical reimbursement was paid to staff as per miscellaneous s.no.53 of chapter-vii of Staff Service Regulations, 1985 and requested to drop the said allegation. The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that the Andhra Pragathi Farmers Service Cooperative Society Ltd., Thallaproddatur was following the procedures in this regard as per Andhra Pragathi Grameena Bank since 5 decades; allocating certain portion in the profits to gratuity fund; the present ex-gratia was paid out of such fund by duly obtaining the approval of Managing Committee; and ultimately requested to drop the allegation. The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that the encashment of privilege leaves was paid to staff as per point.50 of chapter-iv of Staff Service Regulations, 1985 by duly taking approval from the Managing Committee and requested to drop the said allegation.

5 The delinquents-2,3,4 have stated that Thallaproddatur village was submerged under GNSS and the society land remained low level; soil sourced at free of cost; transport and levelling charges incurred to the tune of Rs.410385.00; the said expenditure was incurred through an estimate and under the supervision and approval of the Person-in-Charge committee; the said expenditure was feasible in the interest of society land; and ultimately requested to drop the allegation. [6]. Upon consideration of the facts mentioned in special report of the auditor vis- à-vis with the contentions put forth by the delinquents in their written statements, it is found existence of dispute between the averments of both the parties, hence case taken up for trial proceedings to decide the disputed issues involved in the matter. [7]. In the trial proceedings, A.Pavan Kumar, Assistant Registrar, office of the Sub Divisional Cooperative Officer, Badvel and the Auditor of Andhra Pragathi Farmers Service Cooperative Society Ltd., Thallaproddatur for 2020-2021 has deposed that he has conducted audit of accounts of Andhra Pragathi Farmers Service Cooperative Society Ltd., Thallaproddatur for the financial year 2020-2021; he has noticed certain financial irregularities during the course of audit upon which he has submitted a special report; the financial benefit to the employees were paid as per the APGB regulations and by contravening the Cooperative Law connected thereto and hence, he objected them as irregular expenditures/payments in his special report; supported the findings given by him in his special report with relevant evidences; and finally stated to consider his special report in this surcharge inquiry. [8]. In the trial proceedings, 2 nd Delinquent has deposed that the written statement was already submitted on 05.7.2023 of which could be considered for the purpose of deposition; nothing to state further more. Similarly, the 3 rd delinquent has also deposed the same averments on his side. In the trial proceedings, Delinquents-4 has deposed that P.Ganganna, retired employee was paid terminal benefits to the tune of Rs.1385188.00 by obtaining approval of Person-in-Charge committee on 31.7.2020 and by following Staff Service Regulations, 1995; he would make necessary corrective transactions

6 to reclassify the heads of accounts of which were mistakenly taken for such expenditure; DA/IR of Rs.497592.00 paid by taking approval from the Person-in- Charge committee; paid an amount of Rs.8000.00 towards medical allowance to staff and he agreed that he did not collect any medical expenditure bills from the respective staff; paying medical allowance at the rates of Rs.2500.00 to Chief Executive Officer, Rs.2000.00 to clerks, and that to once in a year; agreed that an amount of Rs.182680.00 was paid as ex-gratia to staff by following APGB procedures; paid an amount of Rs.91432.00 as privilege leave encashment; he has incurred an amount of Rs.410385.00 towards levelling of site of the society with an intention to build office over such site; estimate of work and work evaluation report after completion were obtained and furnished for consideration. In the arguments adduced by the Delinquent-4 on 30.11.2023, it was argued that: i. Chairman, Rayalaseema Grameena Bank, HO, Cuddapah, vide his Lr.No.915/FSS/STF/85, dated 24-10-1985, communicated the approved model service regulations to the society for adoption and effective from 01.7.1985. ii. The BOD of plaintiff society, in its 2 nd Board Meeting held on 22-11-1985 has adopted these service regulations effective from 01.7.1985, vide its Resolution no:5, dated 22-11-1985. iii. The BOD of plaintiff society in its 3 rd board meeting held on 09.4.1986 resolved on services of employees. iv. Since the above service regulations are in force till date and applicable as per law to the society. v. The plaintiff society has not availed Vaidyanathan package, does not fall under chapter-XIIIB of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, and latest service byelaws were not adopted to the society by the General Body. vi. As such the pay and allowances, benefits drawing now do become lawful and unobjectionable, and to drop recovery. [9]. During the hearings, I have heard the delinquents and plaintiff witness during their examination or cross examination, as the case may be. I have perused the evidences brought on record to decide the contentious issues in this inquiry. To substantiate their respective stands taken, both the parties led their documentary as well as oral evidence. After hearing the contentions on behalf of both the sides

7 and the observations made in the evidences, decisions, now the matter has to be decided in this inquiry, especially upon the following points. I. Whether or not an amount of Rs.1185188.00 paid to P.Ganganna, retired employee being the excess gratuity is recoverable from the delinquents. II. Whether or not an amount of Rs.497592.00 paid to employees for being the DA/IR is recoverable from the delinquents. III. Whether or not an amount of Rs.8000.00 paid to employees for being medical allowance is recoverable from the delinquents. IV. Whether or not an amount of Rs.182680.00 paid to employees for being the ex-gratia is recoverable from the delinquents. V. Whether or not an amount of Rs.91432.00 paid to employees for being the encashment of privilege leave is recoverable from delinquents. VI. Whether or not an amount of Rs.410385.00 spent on transport and levelling charges is recoverable from delinquents. The above items of fraud, except VI, pertains to the service benefits of employees, which could be determined basing on the service conditions duly approved by the competent authority. As such it is necessary to ascertain which regulations form basis for payment of such service benefits including the legal validity of such service conditions. The 4 th delinquent has stated in his arguments that the Chairman, Rayalaseema Grameena Bank, HO, Cuddapah, vide his Lr.No.915/FSS/STF/85, dated 24-10-1985, communicated the model service regulations to the society for adoption and effective from 01.7.1985. The BOD of plaintiff society, in its 2 nd Board Meeting held on 22-11-1985 has adopted these service regulations effective from 01.7.1985, vide its Resolution no:5, dated 22-11-1985. As such, the society has made the said regulations effective for operation in the plaintiff society effective from 01.7.1985 and the same are found to be in operation till date. I have perused the said Service Regulations, 1985 in order to ascertain their validity for implementation. The Resolution no:5, dated 22-11-1985 of the then Managing Committee of the plaintiff society was recorded in the minutes book by resolving adoption of Service Regulations, 1985. However, the then Managing

8 Committee have not signed/approved the said Service Regulations, 1985 at the end of text of such model regulations received in Lr.No.915/FSS/STF/85, dated 24-10-1985 of the Chairman, Rayalaseema Grameena Bank, HO, Cuddapah. Nor such service conditions were recorded in detail at least in the minutes book against such Resolution no:5, dated 22-11-1985 of the then Managing Committee of the plaintiff society. The recording of mere ‘ action of approval ’ in the minutes book about the subject matter by without actually approving the said service regulations, did not substantiate the function of approved validation of the service regulations. The Bye Law of plaintiff society was registered in Rc.No.4154/78/B, Dt.21-8-1979 of the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Proddatur. As per Bye Law No.24(xviii) of the plaintiff society, the Managing Committee shall regulate from time to time, within the limits of the budget sanctioned, the strength of the society ’ s staff, their salaries, allowances and service conditions. Thus, the then Managing Committee has to record/endorse the certificate of adoption at the end of text portion of the regulations in order to show sign of acceptance/adoption and also to prevent tampering in the said service regulations. It is found that the said service regulations did possess with no such endorsement, and the same have remained as mere typographic matter in a booklet devoid of authorized signatures. As such, the said Staff Service Regulations, 1985 of which devoid of authorized signatures of adoption, would become ipso facto invalid for operation and the service conditions whatsoever mentioned therein were not liable for implementation in the affairs of plaintiff society. Therefore, all the contentions of the delinquents of which were based upon Staff Service Regulations, 1985 and made in this inquiry would become baseless owing to illegality of such service regulations. In accordance with the provisions laid down under A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, the powers of either General Body or Managing Committee of plaintiff society are not at all ultimate, but shall be exercised subject to the provisions of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 and A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 and the Bye Laws. The plaintiff society was a ceded society affiliated to Rayalaseema Grameena Bank, Kadapa. The financing bank may suggest the model service regulations, but it has no powers to approve or enforce such service regulations in the affairs of plaintiff society, since such powers are vested with the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh.

9 By Act No.21 of 1985, effective from 22-4-1985, Section Sec.116-C(1) was inserted in the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. In accordance with Sec.116- C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, “ a society shall have power to fix the staffing pattern, qualifications, pay scales and other allowances for its employees with the prior approval of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies ” . In the said law, there was no mention about granting exemption of ceded societies from the purview of Sec.116-C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, hence the provisions of Sec.116-C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964 are applicable to plaintiff society. Thereby the then Managing Committee of the plaintiff society should have obtained prior approval of the Registrar, under Sec.116-C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, for Staff Service Regulations, 1985 of which were communicated to the plaintiff society in Lr.No.915/FSS/STF/85, dated 24- 10-1985 of the Chairman, Rayalaseema Grameena Bank, HO, Cuddapah, before adoption of the same through Resolution no:5, dated 22-11-1985 of the then Managing Committee of the plaintiff society. I found that the events of receipt of model service conditions from the financing bank and their adoption by the plaintiff society were occurred subsequent to the date of enforcement of Act No.21 of 1985, i.e., 22-4-1985, hence prior approval of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad is essential for enforcing the Staff Service Regulations, 1985 from 01.7.1985. I found during the inquiry that the authorities of plaintiff society have not obtained prior approval of the Registrar for the said Staff Service Regulations, 1985, and not even post approval at least. In this entire episode, the then Managing Committee of plaintiff society have had acted contrary to the provisions of Sec.116-C(1) of A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964. By taking note of these facts and circumstances, I am of the considered view that the Staff Service Regulations, 1985 upon which the delinquents relying their defence, have no legal validity and as such, whatever the financial service benefits compiled basing on such invalid service regulations, ipso facto becomes unlawful. I found from the records that an unbecoming act of sense observed from the society that it is always reluctant to move out of the Staff Service Regulations, 1985 by without even noticing its illegality. The authorities of plaintiff society have willfully not adopted the Service Bye laws approved by the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh on the pretext that such adoption would render the reduction of salaries and allowances now drawing by the employees on the guise of unlawful Staff Service Regulations, 1985.

10 Nevertheless, it is also essential for me to ascertain what shall stand as basis to arrive legally valid financial service benefits applicable to the employees of plaintiff society, since it did not possess with valid service regulations and it is necessary for me to decide upon the audit objections reported in the special report for the financial year 2020-2021. The Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh has been issuing service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies by duly revising the service benefits wherever necessary from time to time. The present audit objections belong to the financial year 2020-2021. Hence, it is necessary to consider the Service Conditions approved by the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh and were in force as on 01.04.2020. The Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad, by exercising the powers vested in him under Rule 4 of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 read with sub-Rule 24 of Rule 5 of A.P.Cooperative Societies Rules, 1964 has approved the service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies in Rc.No.31811/2005/Sec.116-C/2, Dated: 03-10-2005. Hence, such service conditions are legally binding on the part of plaintiff society, since it did not possess with legally valid service conditions. Though the 4 th delinquent has argued that plaintiff society did not avail Vaidyanathan Package, the Bye Law of plaintiff society amended en bloc and registered with all new Bye Law under Revival Package in Rc.No.1157/2008B, Dated: 16.8.2008 of the Deputy Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Proddatur, and thereby the said Bye Law under Revival Package was in operation from 16.8.2008. In the Procs. Rc.No. Affiliation/Develop. Dept/ 2015-2016, Dated: 05-3-2016 of the Chief Executive Officer, DCCB, Kadapa, the plaintiff society was affiliated to the DCCB, Kadapa by renouncing pre-existing financing bank, i.e., Andhra Pragathi Grameena Bank, Kadapa. The said orders were accepted from 18.3.2016, vide Managing Committee Resolution No.8, dated 18.3.2016 of the plaintiff society. As such, the plaintiff society do not fall under ceded society category from 18.3.2016. Hence, it is also absolutely necessary on the part of plaintiff society to comply the orders of the Government or the Registrar, as the case may be, relating to the service benefits sanctioned to its employees during the financial year 2020-2021 as it is treated merely as an Agricultural Credit Cooperative Society in all respects. Thereby, it is necessary to assess compliance in this regard by the plaintiff society.

11 [10]. In his special report, the Auditor has reported that the society has paid gratuity of Rs.1385188.00 on 04.8.2020 to P.Ganganna; the details like date of appointment, sanction of increments, date of retirement, sanction details of gratuity were not recorded in his service register; P.Ganganna was paid retirement gratuity in excess of Rs.1185188.00 as the maximum permissible limit of gratuity is Rs.200000.00. P.Ganganna, Accountant/Clerk was retired from service on 31.07.2020. He worked on daily wages from 01.12.1988 to 28.9.1990, and his services were regularized on 01.10.1990, vide entries in his service register. He was paid gratuity of Rs.972801.00, privilege leave encashment of Rs.226440.00, salary of Rs.185947.00, in total of Rs.1385188.00. Thus, the objection of payment of gratuity shall be considered for Rs.972801.00 for further inquiry. The gratuity comes under allowances of the employees. As aforementioned the staff service regulations, 1985 were not got approved by the Registrar nor it was signed by the then Person-in-Charge Committee, and hence, the same are invalid and not liable for enforcement. As such, the claim of gratuity of P.Ganganna, retired employee of plaintiff society did not possess with legal validity as it was compiled basing on provisions laid down under unlawful service regulations. The Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad has approved the service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies in Rc.No.31811/2005/Sec.116-C/2, Dated: 03-10-2005. As per these approved service conditions, the Managing Committee is empowered to sanction the gratuity @ ‘ for every completed year of service, the employee is eligible to get 15 days of pay towards gratuity as per the Gratuity Act, with a maximum of Rs.1.00 lakh. ’ In its Resolution no:9, dated 31-07-2020, the Person-in-Charge Committee of the plaintiff society has resolved that ‘ the Person-in-Charge committee would bear the responsibility to provide retirement benefits to P.Ganganna ’ . Consequent to deep examination of this resolution, I found that the then Person-in-Charge committee has not sanctioned the compilation/quantum of the gratuity in terms of monetary value before payment of such benefit to the retired employee, since the Managing Committee is the only authority competent to do so. By without getting sanction of gratuity from the then Person-in-Charge

12 committee, the 4 th delinquent being Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has paid the gratuity of Rs.972801.00 to P.Ganganna, retired employee of the plaintiff society. The Chief Executive Officer of the plaintiff society is not competent to sanction the gratuity to the retired employees. Taking note of all these facts and circumstances, I am of the considered view that the gratuity of Rs.982801.00 of which paid to P.Ganganna was compiled basing on the provisions laid down under unlawful Staff Service Regulations, 1985; such gratuity was not got sanctioned by the competent Person-in-Charge committee of plaintiff society; the then Person-in-Charge committee has given up its responsibility of proper sanction and payment of gratuity; and moreover the gratuity paid was exceeded by Rs.882801.00 than to the limit of maximum Rs.1.00 lakh as prescribed under service conditions approved by the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad. The limit of maximum Rs.2.00 lakh for payment of gratuity as reported by the auditor was enforced in Service Regulations approved by the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Guntur on 23-2-2021, and hence could not be fastened to the instant case as the individual was retired on 31-07-2020. In his Lr.Rc.No.34810/2006/PACS/General, Dated: 21-11-2006, the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad has reiterated that the maximum limit of gratuity shall be Rs.1.00 lakh and directed for proper implementation of Service Byelaws for payment of gratuity by the managements of the societies. Therefore, the said excess payment of gratuity of Rs.882801.00 paid to P.Ganganna is considered as unlawful loss to the plaintiff society. P.Ganganna was also paid encashment of privilege leave of 102 days for a monetary value of Rs.226440.00. In the service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies as approved in Rc.No. 31811/ 2005/ Sec.116-C/2, Dated: 03-10-2005 of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad, there was no provision for payment of privilege leave encashment to the employees as a retirement benefit and as such the payment of Rs.226440.00 being the encashment value of privilege leave was unlawful loss to the plaintiff society. Therefore, I felt that the delinquents by breach of trust in relation to the affairs of the plaintiff society have caused unlawful loss of Rs.1109241.00 (Rs.882801.00 + Rs.226440.00) to the plaintiff society and the same shall be

13 recovered from the delinquents-1,2,3,4 jointly severally with a further interest of 15% per annum with effect from 01.04.2021. Accordingly, Point-I is answered. [11]. In his special report, the Auditor has reported that the Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has paid DA/IR to the employees including himself at par with the government employees; paid an amount of Rs.92102.00 being the DA arrears and Rs.405490.00 being the IR, in total Rs.497592.00 paid during 2020- 2021; no approval from the Managing Committee for such payment; such benefits were paid from out of statutory reserve fund of the society; kept Rs.497592.00 under sundry debtors account. The Person-in-Charge committee in its resolution no.05, dated 31.01.2020, has resolved for payment of DA, IR and other benefits payable from 2017-2018. The pay and allowances, their scales and DA paid to that effect could not be ascertained in isolation at this point of time, since the same have been occurring long past and to be dealt separately and comprehensively by other means of action possible to that effect. The Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has furnished a copy of G.O.Ms.No.10 Finance (PC.I) Dept., Dated: 06-01-2014 as basis of their act of payment of IR to its employees as noted below. S.N. Name of Employee IR paid 1. L.Siva Kumar Reddy 131374.00 2. C.Ankalaiah 85990.00 3. P.Ganganna 132070.00 4. K.L.Rangaiah 56055.00 Total 405490.00 The IR sanctioned in this GO is meant for government employees and the same was not at all applicable to the employees of plaintiff society, vide para.2(d) of the said GO. Hence, the payment of IR of Rs.405490.00 during 2020- 2021 was unlawful loss sustained on the part of the plaintiff society.

14 Therefore, I felt that the delinquents by breach of trust in relation to the affairs of the plaintiff society have caused unlawful loss of Rs.405490.00 to the plaintiff society and the same shall be recovered from the delinquents-1,2,3,4 jointly severally with a further interest of 15% per annum with effect from 01.04.2021. Accordingly, Point-II is answered. [12]. The Auditor has reported that the Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has paid Rs.8000.00 on 30.7.2020 as medical reimbursement; no medical bills of expenditure obtained from the employees. The 4 th delinquent has shown that he has paid the said amounts as per service condition.53 of Staff Service Regulations, 1985. Though the said service conditions were unlawful, the plain reading of service condition 53 confirms that there was provision for reimbursement of medical expenses incurred by the employees. Hence, it is not a kind of medical allowance. The 4 th delinquent being the Chief Executive Officer of the plaintiff society has not obtained medical bills from the respective employees in support of medical expenditure incurred by them, but paid an amount of Rs.8000.00, for which he shall be held responsible. Therefore, I felt that the 4 th delinquent by breach of trust in relation to the affairs of the plaintiff society has caused unlawful loss of Rs.8000.00 to the plaintiff society and the same shall be recovered from him with a further interest of 15% per annum with effect from 01.04.2021. Accordingly, Point-III is answered. [13]. The Auditor has reported that the 4 th delinquent being the Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has paid on 12.3.2021 an amount of Rs.182680.00 as ex-gratia to employees including himself. S.N. Name of Employee Ex-Gratia paid 1. L.Siva Kumar Reddy 52509.00 2. C.Ankalaiah 34606.00 3. P.Ganganna 53956.00

15 4. K.L.Rangaiah 22609.00 5. N.Vishwanath 13000.00 6. Y.Manjunath Reddy 3000.00 7. P.Amaranath Reddy 3000.00 Total 182680.00 In the service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies as approved in Rc.No.31811/2005/Sec.116-C/2, Dated: 03-10-2005 of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad, there was no provision for payment of ex-gratia to the employees. In accordance with the provisions laid down under Sec.45(4)(d) of the A.P.Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, certain portion of net profit from the remaining portion of net profit after statutory allocations, be allocated for payment of bonus to employees to the extent and the manner specified in the bye-laws. I found from the Bye Law of the plaintiff society has no such provision for allocation of net profit towards fund for payment of ex-gratia. When there is no provision for allocation of net profit towards ex-gratia fund, it is untenable for the plaintiff society to allocate net profit to ex-gratia fund even if the plaintiff society has had earned net profits. Any payment of bonus without valid allocation of net profit shall be void. Therefore, I felt that the delinquents by breach of trust in relation to the affairs of the plaintiff society has caused unlawful loss of Rs.182680.00 to the plaintiff society and the same shall be recovered from them jointly severally with a further interest of 15% per annum with effect from 01.04.2021. Accordingly, Point-IV is answered. [14]. The Auditor has reported that the 4 th delinquent being the Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has paid encashment of privilege leave of Rs.91432.00 against to the Bye Laws of the society. S.N. Name of Employee PL encashment paid 1. L.Siva Kumar Reddy 43389.00 2. C.Ankalaiah 30564.00

16 3. K.L.Rangaiah 17479.00 Total 91432.00 In the service conditions of Primary Agricultural Cooperative Credit Societies as approved in Rc.No.31811/2005/Sec.116-C/2, Dated: 03-10-2005 of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad, there was no provision for payment of privilege leave encashment to the employees. Therefore, I felt that the delinquents by breach of trust in relation to the affairs of the plaintiff society has caused unlawful loss of Rs.91432.00 to the plaintiff society and the same shall be recovered from them jointly severally with a further interest of 15% per annum with effect from 01.04.2021. Accordingly, Point-V is answered. [15]. The Auditor has reported that the 4 th delinquent being the Chief Executive Officer of plaintiff society has incurred transport and land levelling expenditure to the tune of Rs.410385.00; incurred said expenditure for levelling of site allotted to society by the government; building construction plans, approvals, quotations for transport of soil, permissions not obtained to that effect. The 4 th delinquent has incurred an amount of Rs.410385.00 towards levelling of site of the society with an intention to build office over such site; estimate of work for Rs.450000.00 and work evaluation Certificate for Rs.410385.00 after completion of work were obtained from Licensed Engineer and furnished during inquiry for consideration. The prescribed procedures like quotations, work tenders, etc. were not followed in this regard. The 4 th delinquent has stated that in order to utilize the freely and readily available soil they performed work urgently and incurred transport and levelling expenditures. Taking note of these facts and circumstances the expenditure is allowed. In result, from the above discussions stated supra, I hold that the plaintiff society is entitled for restoration of funds to the tune of Rs.1796843.00 from the responsible delinquents.

FOREWORD It gives me great pleasure to bring out this Hand Book of Cooperative Laws” . At the outset I express my sincere thanks to Sri Pankaj Dwivedi, I.A.S., Principal Secretary to Government (Cooperation & Agricultural Marketing Department) as the Hand Book is his brain child. It all started with his suggestion when we (Additional Registrars and myself) called on him on 11-9-2007 after he had newly joined the post. He suggested that the Commissioner for Cooperation and Registrar of Cooperative Societies may bring out a Hand Book of Cooperative Laws, by reading of which one can know the important provisions of the Cooperative Acts and Rules without actually reading the bulky Act/Rules book. After detailed discussion whether the work should be entrusted to an outsider i.e. a retired senior officer or should be taken up in house , we have decided to go for the in house option. Basically, I wanted to take this opportunity to go through each and every provision of the Law in detail. Sri P.Basanth Kumar, Additional Registrar has volunteered to write the basic draft which was to be amended/improved by the other three Additional Registrars i.e. Sri M.P.Subramaniam, Sri K.Ganpathi, Sri Ramananda Swamy and myself. Accordingly, Sri Basanth Kumar has prepared various chapters which were vetted by us. Thus the main credit for preparation of this Hand Book goes to Sri Basanth Kumar who put many extra hours and worked very sincerely. I express my sincere gratefulness to him. I am also grateful to Sri Subramaniam, Sri Ganapathi and Sri Ramananda Swamy who worked as a team and contributed their mite in this work. Thanks are due to Sri V.Nagesswara Rao, Joint Registrar (Head Office), V.Sree Rama Rao, Dist.Coop.Officer, East Godavari District, Sri T.Sreenivasulu, Dist. Coop. Officer, Ranga Reddy, Sri G.V.Chandramouleswara Rao, Dist.Coop.Officer, Khammam, Sri P.Jagannadha Rao, Dist.Coop.Officer, Guntur, Sri G.Ananth, Assistant Registrar, APCOB and Sri N.Janaki Rama Rao, DR/ OSD, Mahaboobnagar and special thanks to Sri Vasantha Madhava Rao, Additional Registrar(Retd.), Sri D.Chenna Kishan, Joint Registrar(Retd.), Sri P.V.Brahmam, General Manager(Retd.), APCOB, and Sri N.K.D.Prasada Rao, Deputy Registrar (Retd.) who have taken all the pains to go through the entire draft and suggest several improvements to make the Hand Book more informative to the reader. Smt.N.Radhika Madhavi, J.R.(Legal), Sri M.Sreenathachari, SCDR(Legal) Sri M.Sekhar, D.R.(Legal), Sri Satya Prasad and Sri Haneef, Assistant Registrars(Legal) and Sri B.Ramaseshagiri Rao, S.I.(Legal) and Sri V.Venkateswarlu, S.I. have helped in drafting and editing the text and I express my thanks to them. Sri J.Sreekanth, Sri N.Ratnagiri Rao, and Sri D.Srinivasa Raju, S.C.Steno and personal staff of RCS have helped in typing and other related work and we are thankful to them. Sri V.Amarender Rao, Managing Director, A.P.State Coop.Union deserves a special mention for coming forward to take up the publication of the Hank Book in a short time and make it available at an highly affordable price. I hope that the Hand Book will be useful to the officers and staff of the Department as well as to the members/office bearers of the Societies who would be interested in grasping the gist of the Cooperative Laws in a short time. However, this may not be treated as a substitute to the Act/Rules. Date 04 -02-2008 (J.C.SHARMA) J.C. Sharma, IAS Commissioner for Cooperation and Registrar of Cooperative Societies

M E S S A GE It is heartening to note that the Cooperation Department responded to my suggestion and came up with a well organised “Hand Book of Cooperative Laws” which is basically meant for a citizen who is interested in knowing about what cooperative Law is all about in a concise and easily intelligible manner. This Hand Book is definitely not a substitute for various Cooperative Enactments, Commentaries or Manuals already available on the subject but is basically intended to be of help to the common man who intends to form a rudimentary understanding of different topics of the Cooperative Law at a glance. Besides, this volume will be highly useful to all the officials and non officials posted to work in the Department/Cooperative Institutions for the first time. The efforts put in by the Department in compiling the Act, Rules and Regulations at one place on each topic besides organising a comparative status of three different enactments viz., The Andhra Pradesh Cooperative Societies Act, 1964, The A.P. Mutually Aided Cooperative Societies Act, 1995 and The Multi State Cooperative Societies Act, 2002(Central Act) in one volume is really commendable. I heartily appreciate the interest shown by the Commissioner and Registrar in presenting an highly affordable booklet on a technical and complex subject such as cooperative law. I compliment the efforts of the team of officers and staff who are associated with the compilation of the Hand Book. Date: 04-02-2008 (PANKAJ DWIVEDI) Pankaj Dwivedi, I.A.S., Principal Secretary to Government Co-operation & Agricultural Marketing MOHD.FAREEDUDDIN Minister For Co-operation M E S S A GE It is gratifying to note the attempt of the Department of Cooperation in bringing out this Hand Book containing the provisions of the APCS Act 1964, APMACS Act 1995, Multi State Cooperative Societies Act 2002 and the circular instructions issued by the RCS from time to time. I am sure that this Hand Book will be found useful as a ready reckoner by the Cooperators, employees of the Dept., and others connected to or interested in Cooperatives. I appreciate the efforts put in and the contributions made by Sri. J.C. Sharma, IAS, Commissioner for Cooperation & Registrar of Cooperative Societies and his supporting officers in having brought out this useful Hand Book. This will be of immense value to those who need a quick reference to the Laws on Cooperatives in our state. I am very happy that this Hand Book is published when I am the Minister for Cooperation and I feel proud in releasing the same. I take this opportunity in congratulating, individually, all those who contributed their efforts in bringing out this Hand Book. Date: 04-02-2008 (MOHD. FAREEDUDDIN)

CHAPTER 1 INTRODUCTION 1.1. Cooperation – Origin of the concept Cooperation is ‘Self-help’ made effective by organization. It means working together by forming an association voluntarily. The word ‘Cooperation’ is derived from the latin word ‘Cooperari’. The first part of the word ‘Co’ means ‘with’ and the second part ‘operari’ means ‘to work’. Cooperation, therefore, means ‘working together with others’. Cooperative technique in our country is as old as human history. Kautilya’s Arthasastra maintains that during the days of Mauryan empire, a village functioned as a social cooperative unit . Works of Public utility such as temples, public halls, nesting places, dams etc., were constructed by the villagers as a common obligation. Autonomous guilds of workmen and industrial units existed where work was carried on in a cooperative manner. 1.2. Historical Evolution Cooperation as a movement started as a product of industrial revolution in Europe. Robert Owen and Dr. William King of England were its patrons and through the magazine “The Co-operator” in 1828-30 they emphasized that “Cooperation is a subject entirely for the working classes and the rich have nothing to do with it”. The pioneers to start a cooperative store for the working classes were 28 flannel weavers of Rochdale in the year 1844 with a small capital of 28 pounds radiating a spirit of association in an individualist age with capitalist ideology of each for himself. Their business principles of one man-one vote, sale at market price and division of profits among the members (Share holders) in proportion to the extent of their patronage are the foundation stones for the present day Cooperative Principles. Cooperative technique of “all for each and each for all” was adopted in the rural credit system ushered in rural Germany by Raiffeisen, a Postmaster in 1848 at Weyerbusch when he founded a society to distribute potatoes and bread to the poor at cheaper rates while Schulze Mayor of Delitzsch Town started a Loan Society in 1850. In Italy, Signor Luzzatti started a Friendly Society in Lod, an urban town in 1865, while the first Rural Bank was started at Lorrefia, a small village by Dr.Wollemburg. Impetus was thus given to the growth of Cooperation as a movement by public spirited men and unselfish workers in its cause who considered service important above self. The march of the Cooperative movement in several European Countries led to the establishment of a common forum for them namely International Cooperative Alliance in 1895 to address to its policies and problems. A French Co-operator by name Charles Fourier designed a flag for the Cooperative Movement, adopting all the colours of Rainbow and ICA adopted it in 1923. In 1937, ICA formulated Cooperative Principles. They were reformulated by a committee headed by Prof. D.G.Karve in 1966. Again in 1995, Prof Ian Macpherson’s Committee made a reappraisal and a statement of Cooperative identity with reformulated Principles of Cooperation were adopted by ICA in Manchester Congress. 1.3. ICA Statement on the Cooperative Identity The International Cooperative Identity Statement (ICIS) with components of definition of Cooperative Society, Cooperative Values and Principles of Cooperation has been recognized as significant guide post to strengthen the relevance and identity of Cooperatives in the backdrop of market oriented economy characterized by the pressures and pulls of interdependence, competition and free trade. 1.3.1. Definition A Cooperative is an autonomous association of persons united voluntarily to meet their common economic, social and cultural needs and aspirations through a jointly owned and democratically controlled enterprise. 1.3.2. Values Cooperatives are based on the values of self help, self responsibility, democracy, equality, equity and 1 2

solidarity. In the tradition of their founders, Cooperative members believe in the ethical values of honesty, openness, social responsibility and caring for others. 1.3.3. Principles The Seven Cooperative Principles are guidelines by which Cooperatives put their values into practice. 1) Voluntary and Open Membership 2) Democratic Member Control 3) Member Economic Participation 4) Autonomy and Independence 5) Education, Training and Information 6) Cooperation among Cooperatives 7) Concern for community 1.4. Evolution of Cooperative Legislation In India Although Cooperation existed in India since the days of Mauryan Rulers as integral part of Indian Cultural ethos characterized by the instinct and tradition of mutual assistance, joint action, joint possession and joint management of the assets endowed, successive foreign invasions caused the destruction of self reliant rural economy. Because of the usurious practices of the money lenders and illiteracy of the peasants, these debts went on increasing and became a crushing burden on them. Their misery unleashed Deccan Riots in 1874. The Deccan Riots Commission of 1875 and the Famine Commission of 1880 testified to the unjust character of the debt. Justice Ranade and Sir William Wedderburn formulated a scheme in 1883 for the establishment of a bank in Pune to finance agriculturists of Purandar Taluq raising a capital of Rs. 10.00 Lakhs to redeem the outstanding debts of Ryots. Madras Government was the first to depute Sir Federick Nicholson “to study the theory and practice of Agricultural and Land Banks in Europe and to suggest means by which a similar movement may be popularized in India”. His reports issued in 1895 and 1897 and Mr. Dupernex’s book on “Peoples Banks in Northern India” in 1900, stimulated the then Government of India to appoint Sir Edward Law Committee to suggest special legislation for Cooperative Societies. The result was the enactment of Cooperative Credit Societies Act, 1904 on 25.03.1904, which forms the basic structure for Cooperative Legislation in India. It was modelled on the English Friendly Societies Act. Soon Registrars were appointed in Presidencies and major Provinces to organize, register, supervise, audit, inspect and liquidate Societies when not functioning well. In the preamble the scope of Cooperatives was stated to benefit “Agriculturists, Artisans and other persons of limited means”. This law gave no legislative protection to Societies for purposes other than Credit or to the Central Agencies, Banks and Unions which were coming into existence. These deficiencies were remedied in the Cooperative Societies ACT II of 1912. With the authority conferred by Montague-Chelmsford reforms in 1919, Provincial Governments of Bombay, Madras, Bengal enacted their own laws to govern Cooperative Societies. These acts widened the scope of the movement substituting in their preamble “for agriculturists, artisans and persons of limited means” as intended beneficiaries, by the words “agriculturists and other persons with common economic needs” and Better Living, Better Business and Better Methods of Production, which opened the door to advantage being taken even by men of better means. They also conferred enormous powers on the Registrar, such as compulsory amalgamation and division of Societies, Supersession of erring committees, surcharge of persons responsible for malfeasance and misfeasance, adjudication of disputes arising in Societies, execution of awards and decisions or orders given by him. In Bombay Act of 1925, a provision for constitution of Tribunal to entertain appeals against the orders of Registrar was made. After independence, the nation adopted the approach of planned economic development for establishment of a mixed economy consisting of three sectors namely Public, Private and Cooperative Sectors. Cooperatives were visualized to play the role of a balancing factor between public and private sectors. Pandit Nehru considered Cooperatives as one of the three pillars of 3 4

Democracy, the other two being the Panchayat and the School. When Rural Credit Survey Committee in 1954 recommended for state participation in Cooperatives at all levels, S.T.Raja Committee was appointed by the Government of India to suggest amendments to the Cooperative law, the committee prepared a Model Act enabling state participation and appointment of Government nominees on the management of assisted Cooperative Societies. National Development Council in 1958 reviewed Cooperative legislation and advised State Governments to remove restrictive provisions in the Cooperative Law and liberalize it by curtailing certain powers of the Registrar. In Andhra Pradesh, the above pioneering legislative efforts were followed by the Andhra Pradesh (Andhra Area) Cooperative Societies Act (Act VI) of 1932, The A.P. (Andhra Area) Cooperative Land Mortgage Act of 1934, The A.P. (Telangana Area) Cooperative Societies Act (XVI) of 1952, the Hyderabad Land Mortgage Act, 1349 fasli. Amalgamation of these acts resulted in Andhra Pradesh Cooperative Societies Act 7 of 1964. In response to the recommendations of the Ardhanareeswaran Committee (1987) to democratize and professionalize the Cooperatives and to make the role of the Registrar more positive as a development agent and of the Choudhary Brahm Perkash Committee (1991) to change the role of State as a facilitator instead of a regulator and the Model Law set out by the latter, the role of the Government and the Registrar are curtailed in the functioning of the Cooperatives. To facilitate their autonomous and democratic functioning, the A.P. Mutually Aided Cooperative Societies Act 1995 (AP MACS Act) is legislated in Andhra Pradesh on the pattern of the Model Act of Choudhary Brahm Perkash. This is a pioneering step and is a trend setter to other States in the country to liberalize their Cooperative laws. The crisis faced by the Cooperative Credit System in the Country at present demanded its restructuring and capital infusion. Prof. A.Vaidyanathan’s Committee submitted its recommendations on revitalizing the Cooperative Credit System in December 2004. Andhra Pradesh Government was the first to accept the recommendation and to enter into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the Government of India and NABARD on 29.08.2006. APCS Act 1964 was also amended by an Amendment Act 16 of 2007 whereby the Cooperative Credit Societies identified under the Revival Package will be governed by Special Provisions incorporated under Chapter XIII B and Sections 115 C and 115 D. These provisions facilitate autonomous and democratic functioning of Credit Cooperatives to a great extent subject to the guidelines of RBI and NABARD on different aspects of their functioning. With the capital infusion of about Rs. 2,000 Crores expected to be received under the Revival Package of Credit Cooperatives, the Credit Cooperatives will be in a position to cleanse their balance sheets by wiping out losses and improve their financial position. 1.5 Present Status of Cooperatives in India The movement now has 5.03 Lakh Cooperatives, a membership of 210 Million, a 100% rural network, 67% coverage of Rural Households, 46.31% of Agricultural Credit, 23.5% of Fertilizer Production, 51% of Oil Marketed, 55% of Handlooms and 62.5% of storage facility at village level. There are 21 National Level Cooperative Federations, 361 State Level Cooperative Federations and 2572 District Level Cooperative Federations in the Country now. There is no sphere of economic activity which is left untouched by Cooperation. In Andhra Pradesh a total of 73,906 Cooperatives are registered under the A.P.C.S. and the A.P.MACS Act. 1.6 Present Status of Cooperatives in the State of Andhra Pradesh As of 2007, Societies numbering about 56,056 of various types are registered under the Act of which 28, 801 Societies are under the superintendence and control of the Registrar and 27,255 special types of functional societies are under the control of respective heads of 5 6

departments (Functional Registrars). As many as 32,502 Cooperatives are registered under this legislation covering all types of economic activity particularly those fostering Self- help and Thrift Cooperatives. 1.7 Delegation of powers under the Cooperative Acts Powers under the APCS Act and the AP MACS Act have been delegated to various Functional Registrars in the State to deal with the functioning of the Societies coming under their purview to the extent of authority conferred under the Acts. All the powers under the APCS Act have been delegated to the Additional Registrars working in Head Office. Similarly, majority of powers under the APCS Act have been delegated to the District Collectors (Collector, Cooperation) and other functionaries of Cooperation Department viz., Joint Registrars, Special Category Deputy Registrars, Deputy Registrars, Assistant Registrars and Senior Inspectors working under the control of the State Registrar i.e., the Commissioner and Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Andhra Pradesh, Hyderabad. Majority of provisions relating to Primary Societies have been delegated to Deputy Registrars working as Divisional Cooperative Officers and provisions relating to District Level Societies have been delegated to the District Collectors and the Joint Registrars working as District Cooperative Officers. As far as, the statutory powers delegated to the above officers of the Department, those officers will function as Registrars for such class of Societies. However, certain regulatory powers such as conduct of elections, supersession of committees, appointment of PICs etc., in respect of apex level societies, district level societies and central societies appoiment of supervisory staff, sanction of prosecution etc. are retained with the State Registrar. The Act also provides power to the State Registrar to authorise the subordinate officers or any person to discharge certain statutory functions like Surcharge Inquiry, Arbitration References etc. The delegation important powers under the APCS Act, 1964 is tabulated below. The powers of cancellation of registration, restoration of a society wound up, execution of decisions, decrees and orders, attachment of property before decisions or order in respect of primary, district and apex societies whose head quarters is located in the division are vested in the divisional cooperative officer. The powers of appeal are vested in the cooperative tribunal concerned while the revisionary powers are vested in the State Registrar of cooperative societies in respect of orders of the subardinate officers and in the Government in respect of the orders of the State Registrar of coop- societies. The powers of review are vested with the original authority who passed the orders subject to fulfilling corditions like error apparent on a record, surfacing of new facts of evidence etc., In respect of A.P. MACS Act, all powers of the registrar have been delegated to the Additional Registrars in head office. Similarly, all powers under the Act have been delegated to the dist cooperative officers except special audit, inquiry and follow up action after dissolution the tribunal. CHAPTER 2 REGISTRATION [Sections 4, 5, 6, 8, 9 and Rules 2A, 3, 3A, 4 and 5] 2.1 Cooperative Societies which can be registered A Cooperative society, which has promotion of economic interest of its members as its main object can be registered as a primary society (for individuals) or a federal society (for a group of societies). 2.2 Liability Liability means liability of a member to the society in the event of its winding up. A society can be registered with limited or unlimited liability. Liability may be limited (to the extent of share capital paid by him) or unlimited (to the extent of total deficit in the assets of the society). However, the liability of the Government/ financing bank / federal society is limited to the share capital subscribed by it. 2.3. Procedure for Registration The promoters should fulfill the conditions of minimum membership or minimum share capital or pre- conditions or entry point norms prescribed by the Registrar for different classes of Societies separately. They should hold a meeting to i. finalize Objectives ii. prepare a scheme setting out the economic soundness of the society iii. adopt the model bye-laws with necessary modifications etc., However, the provisions of the Act and Rules will prevail if the bye-laws are repugnant to the Act- and rules. 2.4 Application for Registration The application for registration of a Cooperative society as to be made by at least 10 members belonging to 9 10

different families in the prescribed form and shall be accompanied by i. one original and one copy of the proposed bye-laws i i minutes of the promoters’ meeting iii. sworn statement from each applicant etc. In case of a Primary Cooperative Society, the Application for Registration has to be made to the Divisional Cooperative Officer concerned or to the Functional Registrar concerned in case of Societies which are under the administrative control of other Departments. In case of societies formed for raising funds the members shall reside or own immovable property in the area of operation of the society. 2.5 Action on the application for registration The Registrar after satisfying himself about the fulfillment of conditions, may register the society, its byelaws and issue a certificate of registration within 45 days in case of a primary society and within 60 days in case of other societies from the date of receipt of the application. Where the Registrar is not satisfied he shall communicate the order of the refusal within the same time frame. Society becomes a body corporate once it is registered. 2.6 Amendment to bye-laws Bye-laws may be amended either by the society itself as per its Requirements or on the suggestion of the Registrar (voluntary amendment) or by the Registrar (compulsory amendment). To carry out an amendment to any of its bye-laws already registered including change of name, change of liability etc., the same should be approved by the general body by a majority of not less than 2/3rds of the members present and voting and registered by the Registrar concerned within 30 days of the receipt of the amendment proposal. Where the Registrar feels that an amendment of the bye-laws is necessary in the interest of such society or of Cooperative movement, he may issue a notice calling upon the society to convene the general body meeting to adopt such an amendment within a specified time. If the society fails to adopt the amendment within the time stipulated, the Registrar may register such amendment after giving the society an opportunity of making its representation. 2.7 Privileges of Cooperative Society : i) Registration confers the status of a corporate body. ii) Confers first charge on cesation movables and immovable properties of the members while availing a loan iii) Charge and set off in respect of share or contribution or interest of members in respect of any debt due from such member iv) exemption from attachment of share or contribution or interest of members under any decree or order of any court v) exemption from compulsory registration of instruments issued by the society vi) exemption from certain taxes, fees and duties vii) deduction from employees' salary viii) to avail financial assistance from government ix) notice necessary in suits x) bar on jurisdiction of courts xi) application of civil procedure code while discharging quasi-judicial powers by departmental officers xii) admissibility of record maintained by societies as valid evidence etc., 11 12

CHAPTER – 3 MEMBERSHIP [Sections 19 to 29 and Rules 12A, 13, 14, 18, 21B, 24, 30 and 59] 3.1. Introduction Members of a Society constitute the foundation on which the organization is built. The APCS Act contains provisions on eligibility to be a member, associate member, disqualification for membership of society / MC, cessation of membership of MC and reinstatement, rights of members to services, expulsion of members and restriction on holding and transfer of shares, transfer of interest on death of a member and liability of past member etc. 3.2. Eligibility for Membership Individuals competent to contract (i.e., those who are not minors, are of sound mind and not disqualified by any law) can become members of a Society subject to such qualifications as may be specified in the bye-laws of the class of Society concerned. In addition, any other Society, the State or Central Government, any statutory body formed for the purpose of development of a particular industry etc., can become members of Society pertaining to its class. Minors can also be admitted as members of the Society formed for their welfare. 3.3. Procedure for becoming a member Any individual who fulfills the eligibility criteria mentioned above can apply for membership in a society. The MC is competent to admit members and allot shares. Admission to membership cannot be refused without sufficient cause. Refusal, if any, is required to be communicated with reasons within (15) days of decision of the MC or within 60 days of his application whichever is earlier. 3.4. Associate Member Associate member means a member who possesses only limited privileges, rights and liabilities as provided in the Act, Rules and the Bye-laws. The Act provides for admission of individuals or informal groups such as Self- Help Groups (SHGs), Rytu Mitra Groups (RMGs), Non- Government Organizations (NGOs) working for the welfare of farmers as Associate Members. The Associate member is not entitled to vote or to any share in the profits or assets of the society. Members in an Agricultural Credit Society without holding land will be treated as Associate Members. However, in respect of Cooperative Credit Societies identified under Revival Package (Prof. Vaidyanathan Report), an Associate Member fulfilling certain conditions of minimum deposit and minimum period is given full voting rights. 3.5. Disqualification for membership Persons who are insolvent or sentenced for moral turpitude or paid employees of the society or its financing bank or expelled from membership or carrying on conflicting business or failing to transact minimum business prescribed or failing to attend two consecutive general body meetings etc., are disqualified from seeking admission and also for continuing as members of a Society. The GB is competent to remove a disqualified member after giving him an opportunity of making a representation. 3.6. Disqualification for membership of MC A person who is a near relative of a paid employee of the society or financing bank or a defaulter to the society beyond three months or a delegate of a defunct or defaulting society beyond three months or against whom a decree is pending recovery or who acquires any interest in any subsisting contract made with the society or is of an unsound mind or legal practitioner appearing on behalf or against the society or is carrying conflicting business or has been sentenced to imprisonment under Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 or is convicted for moral delinquency or an employee of Government or undertaking or institution 13 14

receiving State Aid or having more than two children is not eligible for being chosen or continuing as member of the Managing Committee (MC). An MLA, MP, Mayor / Chairman of Municipal Corporation / Council, elected member or Chairman of ZP, elected member or President of Mandal Parishad and Sarpanch can retain only one position i.e., either membership of MC or the above mentioned positions. No one can be a member of MC more than two societies classified as Apex or Central. 3.7. Cessation of membership of MC An MC member ceases to hold office forthwith for the residue of his term and also for the subsequent two terms, if he is found guilty of misuse of the property of the society in an Inquiry or found responsible for making any irregular appointment or sanctioning benami loans etc. Both the President and the Secretary cease to hold office if the society incurs loss as a result of misuse / misapplication of share capital. All the members of Committee cease to hold office if they fail to submit annual accounts to the Chief Auditor within six months of the end of the Cooperative Year or fail to place the Inquiry / Inspection / Audit / Special Audit Reports before the next General Body held after the date of receipt of the report or fail to initiate action for prosecution against any member or servant of the society for their criminal liability. 3.8. Rights of members to services, redressal mechanism and expulsion Every member is entitled to the services available to the members under the bye-laws. The services must be made available by the MC on application. If the society fails or refuses to render services, the member can seek redressal through the Registrar. However, members cannot exercise their rights till they remit all dues payable to the Society. Every member shall have one vote. A member is eligible to vote or contest in any election only if he contributes the minimum prescribed share capital, transacts the minimum business or utilize minimum services as provided in the bye-laws, holds his membership for a continuous period of thirty days from the date of admission and is not a defaulter to the Society for the period exceeding three months. If a member acts against the interest of the society, he can be expelled by the General Body after giving an opportunity of making his representation. 3.9. Restriction on holding and transfer of shares No member other than Government / member society, is permitted to hold more than 1/5 th of the total share capital of the society. Further, individual member is prohibited from transferring his share or interest in the society for a period of one Cooperative Year and also to non- members. However, the State Governments’ equity in the Cooperative Credit Societies identified under Revival Package cannot exceed 25% of the Paid-up Share Capital. The State Cooperative Credit Society can reduce the Governments’ equity participation further. 3.10. Transfer of Interest on death of a member and liability of past member and estate of deceased member On the death of a member, the society shall transfer his share or interest to his nominee and in the absence of a valid nomination to the apparent heir or legal representative on his seeking membership of the society. However, the nominee / heir / legal representative can request the society to pay him the cash equivalent of the share or interest of the deceased member. The liability of a past member for the debts of the society continues for two years from the date of his relinquishing membership. In case of deceased member also the said liability continues for a period of two years from the date of his death. However, in case of liquidation, the said liability in both the cases will continue till the completion of the liquidation proceedings but will be limited only to the debts of the society existing as on the date of cessation of membership or death. 15 16

CHAPTER – 4 MANAGEMENT [Sections 32 to 34–A and Rules 21, 21A, 22, 22A, 22B, 22C, 22D, 23, 23A, 23B, 23AA, 24A, 25, 26, 28, 29 and 34] 4.1. Introduction One of the distinguishing features of a Cooperative Society is its adherence to the principle of democratic control. The members of the Society constitute the General Body (GB) and the GB has the ultimate authority in the management of the Society. The GB periodically elects its representatives who are called Managing Committee (MC) members to look after day-to-day functioning of the society. 4.2. Powers and Functions of General Body The main powers of the GB are as follows i. Election and Removal of members of the Committee and members ii. Expulsion of members iii. Amendment to Bye-laws iv. Submission of Annual Report to the Registrar v. Approval of Annual Budget of Income and Expenditure vi. Decision on transfer of assets and liabilities, on division or amalgamation vii. Decision on bad debts considered irrecoverable viii. Approval of staffing pattern, pay and other allowances of the employees of the Society and contingencies. ix. Contribution to Cooperative Education Fund etc. 4.3. General Body Meetings The MC of a Society must call a minimum of two general meetings in a Cooperative Year so as to ensure that at least one meeting is held in each half year. If the MC fails to call the general meeting within the time limit prescribed, the members of the committee cease to hold office from the very next day on which the general meeting is due. On such a default on the part of the MC, the Registrar is competent to convene the general meeting. The Government is also vested with powers to call a general meeting for conducting elections to the MC. There is also a provision for calling GB meeting within 30 days of receipt of a requisition from such number of members as or specified in the Bye-laws or from the financing Bank or federal society or the Registrar. 4.4. Managing Committee (MC) The constitution and composition of MC varies in different types of Societies with specified reservation to various categories. The GB has to constitute a MC as per its Bye-laws and entrust the Management of affairs to such Committee. The term of MC is 5 years from the date of election. 4.5. Powers and Function of Managing Committee The main powers and function of the MC are as follows i) Admission of members, allotment and transfer of shares ii) Recommended removal of disqualified members and committee members iii) Raise funds in the form of loans / deposits and invest them iv) Conduct GB meeting and elections to the MC in time v) Carryon day-to-day management of the Society vi) Place annual report, annual plan and budget, reports of Inquiry / Inspection / Audit / Special Audit before the GB and ensure rectification of defects. 17 18

vii) Prepare and place before the GB information on disposal of properties, deficits, write off bad debts, removal or expulsion of members, appropriation of net profits, election of delegates to financing bank or other societies etc. viii) Initiate action for prosecution against persons incurring criminal liability ix) Review outstanding loans and ensure legal action etc. 4.6 Government Nominees The Government is empowered to nominate up to three persons or 1/3 rd of the total number of MC members whichever is less as members of the MC of societies receiving State Aid. However, as per amendment carried out as a part of implementation of Vaidyanathan Committee Recommendations, the DCCBs and APCOB can have one Government Nominee and there will be no Government nominee on the MC of PACS. 4.7 Election to Managing Committee Elections are to be conducted to the MC by the Registrar before the expiry of its term in respect of societies in receipt of State Aid. In respect of societies not in receipt of State Aid, the concerned committee itself shall hold elections or request the Registrar for the same. 4.8 MC Meetings The MC meetings should be called by the President as frequently as possible, at least once in 3 months. If he fails to convene the meetings he ceases to hold office from the next day on expiry of one more month thereafter. 4.9 Person-in-Charge (PIC) Committee In case there is no committee or it is not possible to call a GB to hold elections to the MC of the Society, Person or Persons may be appointed to manage the affairs of the Society by Registrar initially for a period not exceeding six months. The term can be extended by Government up to three years and in special circumstances upto six years in aggregate. The PIC can exercise all or any of the functions of the committee subject to the control and directions / instructions of the Government / Registrar as the case may be issued from time to time. The Registrar will conduct elections of a new Committee before expiry of the tenure of the PIC. 4.10 Supersession of the Committee The Registrar is empowered to supersede the Committee and appoint a Special Officer / MC for a period not exceeding two years, extendable to three years, if the Committee is not functioning properly or wilfully disobeys or fails to comply any lawful order or direction given by the Registrar under the Act or the Rules after giving an opportunity of making its representation. The financing bank has to be consulted by the Registrar in case the Society is indebted to it. In respect MCs of APCOB and DCCB, supersession can be done only in consultation with the RBI. The MC of a PACS can be superseded only on the grounds of i) incurring losses for three consecutive years or (ii) detection of serious financial irregularities or frauds or (iii) based on judicial directives or (iv) lack of quorum for three consecutive meetings or v) failure in getting audit conducted or vi) failure in conducting GB meetings or vii) wilful disobedience or failure to comply with lawful directions / guidelines issued by the Registrar. As in the case of PIC, the Special Officer or Managing Committee can exercise only the functions of the Committee subject to the control and instructions / directions of the Government / Registrar as the case may be. The Registrar will conduct elections of a new committee before the expiry of the term of the Special Officer / MC. 19 20

4.11 Devolution of the powers and functions of the President or Vice-President The powers and functions of the President will devolve on the Vice-President if he ceases to hold office or continuously absent from the Head Quarters for more than fifteen days or is incapacitated for more than fifteen days. 4.12 No-confidence Motion against the President and Vice President The President / Vice-President will be removed from office by the Registrar if a motion of no-confidence signed by not less than half of the MC members is passed with the support of not less than 2/3 rd of the total MC members in the meeting of the MC convened for the purpose by the Registrar. No-confidence motions are not allowed within one year of the assumption of office by the President / Vice- President elected by the MC. CHAPTER - 5 ELECTIONS [Section 31 and Rules 18, 22, 22(A), 22-AA, 22-AAA, 22-B, 22- C and 22-D] 5.1. Introduction The Cooperatives function on democratic principles. The Managing Committees (Boards) are elected on the basis of ‘one man – one vote’. Proxy voting is not permitted. 5.2 Authority to conduct Election The elections in a Cooperative Society are conducted by the Registrar in respect of Societies receiving state-aid. In respect of other (unaided) Societies, the existing MC itself is responsible for conduct of elections. However, the MC of such Societies has the option to request the Registrar to conduct elections. In both the cases the election process has to be initiated well before the expiry of term of the existing MC. The elections to the Societies managed by Person-in-Charge (PIC) Committees or Special Officers (SO) / MC are held by the Registrar at the expiration of the term of such PICs / SOs. The Registrar is the Election Authority in respect of Apex and Central (like DCCBs, DCMSs) societies. The District Collector is the Election Authority in respect of all other societies in the district. 5.3 Cost of Conducting Elections The total expenses for conducting elections must be borne by the Society. Where the Registrar conducts the election, the expenses will be prescribed by him. 5.4.1 Disqualifications to vote in elections and to contest Elections Eligibility to vote in elections A member of the Society can vote in elections only if he ; i. transacts the minimum value of business or utilize the minimum value of services or facilities as prescribed in the byelaws during a Cooperative Year 21 22

ii. subscribes the minimum share capital prescribed (at present it is Rs. 300/-), i i i continues to be a member for a period of 30 days from the date of admission till the date of election notification, iv is not a defaulter for more than three months, v not a delegate of a Society under liquidation or marked for deregistration. 5.4.2 Disqualifications for contesting in elections or for continuing as a member of the Managing Committee A member is not eligible to contest in elections unless he transacts the minimum value of business or utilize the minimum value of services or facilities as prescribed in the byelaws during a Cooperative Year. A member shall not be eligible for contesting in elections or to continue as a member of the Managing Committee if he - i. is a near relative of a paid employees of Society or its financing bank, ii. is in default in the payment of any amount due to the society or any other society, iii. is a delegate of defunct or a defaulting society iv. is a person against whom a decree is pending recovery v. acquires any interest in any subsisting contract with the society vi. is of unsound mind, a deaf-mute or a leper vii. is appearing as a legal practitioner for or against the society viii.is carrying on a conflicting business interest ix. is sentenced to imprisonment under Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 x. is convicted by a Criminal Court for moral delinquency, xi. is having more than two children as on 02.01.1996 etc. 5.5 Composition of MC, Reservations and Division of Constituencies The Composition of Managing Committees of important societies is as follows Sl. No Type of society Strength of the committee Elected Nominated Total 1. PACS 13 5 18 2. FSCS 13 6 19 3. DCCB 21 4 25 4. DCMS 10 3 13 Reservation for SC, ST, BC and Woman has been provided in the election to the above Managing Committees For other Societies where the composition has not been fixed, the byelaws of the societies indicate the composition of the MC. For conducting elections, the area of the society is divided in as many constituencies as the number of members to be elected. Where there is no division of constituencies, the members of MC will be elected by the General Body (GB) in accordance with Bye-laws. 5.6 Mode of Election The elections are held by secret ballot. However, in respect of elections to Weaker Section Societies or Societies having small number of members election is held by show of hands at the General Body meeting convened for the purpose. Even in such cases, if the election authority is of the view that polling by show of hands is not possible, he may order for conduct of elections by secret ballot. 5.7 Conduct of Elections The main steps involved in the conduct of elections are as follows 23 24

i. Election Authority appoints an Election Officer. The Election Officer shall be an officer shall be not below the rank of Deputy Registrar in respect of Apex, District and Central Society and any employee of Government in respect of other societies ii. The Election Authority shall issue a notification fixing a Election Schedule and date of poll separately. iii. The Election Officer shall send a communication of Election within 3 (three) days of his appointment and requisition the records and voters list of the society. iv. The society publish the voters list within 7 days from the date of requisition of the Election Officer and invite the claims or objections within 7 days thereafter. In respect of PACS including FSCS the voters list shall be prepared revenue village wise and territorial constituency wise. v. The society after deciding on the objections or claims shall prepare the voters list and communicate the same to the Election Officer. vi.
