On receipt of the voters list the Election Officer shall verify the same and satisfy himself that the list conforms to the criteria for eligibility to vote as per APCS Act and Rules. If he notices any omissions or commissions he shall refer the same to the society for rectification. After rectification he shall publish the final list of voters along with the Election Schedule in Form I. vii. The Election Officer shall receive nominations in Form II at the specified place, date and time. Every nomination shall be signed by one proposer and one seconder finding place in the voters list of the constituency in respect of which the nomination is filed. The nomination paper shall be presented by the candidate him self and by his proposer or seconder to the Election Officer or to the person authorized by the Election Officer. Nomination fee is also to be paid to the Election Officer. Members claiming to be SC/ST/ BC shall produce the caste certificate issued by the competent authority. The nominations shall be received on a day, which is not a public holiday. The Election Officer shall publish the list of nominations received by him in Form III at the close of the time fixed. viii. Copy of the voters list can be obtained by the contesting candidates on payment of the cost fixed by the society. ix. The Election Officer shall scrutinize the nominations on the next day and decide all objections if any candidate wise and accept and reject the nominations and publish the list of valid nominations in Form IV and the list of rejected nomination in Form V. x. A candidate may withdraw his nomination by notice in writing in Form VI by delivering it in person to the Election Officer before 5.00 PM of the following day on which the list of valid nominations was published. xi. The Election Officer shall publish the final list in Form VII duly allotting symbols to each candidate in case of contest. If the number of candidates whose nominations are valid does not exceed the number of vacancies to be filled by election, the Election Officer shall declare them to have been duly elected. xii. In case of contest the ballot papers shall be prepared in Form VIII and hold the poll within a period of 7(seven) days from the date of withdrawals. xiii. Counting shall take place immediately after the close of poll and results shall be declared immediately on completion of counting xiv. The Election Officer shall call for nominations for the post of office bearers like President, Vice President, Secretary within 3 (Three) days thereafter. xv Scrutiny of the nominations shall be made in the morning of the day notified for the purpose and the valid nominations shall be published by 12.00 Noon on the same day. 25 26

xvi. Withdrawals shall be allowed upto 2.00 PM of the same day and final list of nominations shall be published by 2.30 PM on the same day xvii. Voting for the office bearers from 3.00 PM to 5.00 PM and the results declared after counting is over on the same day. 5.8 Election of Office Bearers There is indirect election of the President and other Office Bearers of the Society. They are elected by the elected and co-opted members of the MC. The Election Officer will convene the meeting of the newly constituted MC for the purpose of election of Office Bearers including President, Vice-President, Secretary within 3 days of the date of election of the MC. In case of any vacancy in the MC unfilled through election, the Election Officer will ensure the co-option of members to fill up the unfilled vacancies before conducting elections to the Office Bearers. 5.9 Postponement of Elections The Government or the Election Authority may direct the postponement of elections under one or more of the following circumstances i. Breakdown of Law and Order ii. On account of natural calamity that prevents the conduct of elections iii. When there is apprehension that elections cannot be conducted freely and frankly When elections are postponed, the Election Authority will order for recommencement of the election from the stage at which it was obstructed or interrupted. 5.10 Nominations by Registrar Two women members from among the Self-Help Groups / Rytu Mitra Groups / NGOs and one member of the minority community are nominated by the Registrar to the PACS including LSCS and Rural Banks within 60 days of the declaration of election results. These nominated members will not have any voting rights 5.11 Custody and Preservation of Records After completion of the process of elections, the election officer has to handover all the election material and records to the Divisional Cooperative Officer in respect of Primary Societies and to the District Cooperative Officer in case of other Societies. These records are to be preserved for a period of six months from the date of election or the till the election dispute, if any, is disposed of, which ever is later. 5.12 Comprehensive Report Diary on Election Proceedings The proceedings of the elections to the MC and Office Bearers are to be recorded in the Minute Book by the Election Officer and a comprehensive report diary along with the specimen signature of the President has to be furnished to the Election Authority. 27 28

CHAPTER – 6 REGULATION (AUDIT, INQUIRY, INSPECTION AND SURCHARGE) [Sections 50 to 60 and Rules 45, 46, 47, 48, 50, 58, 59 and 60] 6.1 Audit 6.1.1 Introduction Audit in respect of a cooperative society refers to official examination and verification of accounts pertaining to the society carried out for a particular period. The responsibility of conducting the statutory audit of Societies which received State-Aid cooperative societies conducted once in a Cooperative Year is cast on the Cooperation Department. There is a separate Audit wing in the Cooperation Department headed by the Additional Registrar / Chief Auditor who works under the general superintendence of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies. The Chief Auditor shall audit or cause to be audited by a person authorized by him, the accounts of a cooperative society at least once in every year and issue audit certificate before the end of the succeeding cooperative year. There is no provision in the Act for Re-Audit. The District Cooperative Audit Officer is responsible for audit of cooperative societies and issue of Audit Certificates within his jurisdiction other than the apex societies and DCCBs. The Additional Registrar / Chief Auditor will issue the Audit Certificates of apex societies and Dist. Coop. Central Banks. 6.1.2 Audit of Unaided Societies In respect of a cooperative society not in receipt of state aid the Managing Committee of such societies shall cause the audit of accounts of the society every year as per the Audit Manual prescribed by the Registrar, either through the Chief Auditor or a Chartered Accountant. Thus the responsibility for conduct of audit of un-aided societies rests with the management of the societies. The audit will primarily cover an examination of the debts, over due, verification of cash balance and securities and valuations of the assets and liabilities including prudent management of the affairs of the Society. 6.1.3 Audit of APCOB & CCBs covered under the Revival Package CHAPTER XIII-B has been added to the APCS Act, 1964 duly incorporating special provisions applicable to Cooperative Credit Societies identified under Revival Package through an amendment issued vide Act No. 16 of 2007. As per Section 115 – D (21) (of Chapter XIII-B), the APCOB/DCCB shall cause audit of its accounts by a Chartered Accountant selected from a panel approved by NABARD. 6.1.4 Time Limit The audit shall be completed within a period of six months from the close of the cooperative year. Responsibility is also placed on the Managing Committee (MC) of the societies under section 50 (3) of APCS Act 1964 to present the accounts within three months failing which the MC will attract the penal provision of cessation under section 21 AA of the Act. 6.1.5 Audit Fee The cooperative societies whose audit is conducted by the departmental auditors, have to pay audit fee as per Rule 46 of APCS Rules. 6.2 Inquiry 6.2.1 Authority to hold an Inquiry The Registrar is vested with the powers to hold an Inquiry against a Cooperative Society either himself personally or through some person authorized by him. 6.2.2 Basis for Inquiry The Inquiry can be ordered by Registrar in two situations. i. He may Inquire on his own motion (suo-moto). 29 30

ii. He is bound to order an Inquiry if application is filed by a federal society or by not less than 1/3 rd of MC members or by not less than 1/5 th of the total members of the Society. 6.2.3 Aspects (areas to be covered) for holding an Inquiry The Registrar or person authorized by him can hold an Inquiry into the i. Constitution of the Society ii. Working of the Society iii. Financial condition of the Society and related matters. 6.2.4 Essential Ingredients of an order of Inquiry The order instituting an Inquiry should contain, among others, i. The name of the Society ii. The name of the Inquiry Officer iii. The terms of Inquiry iv. Apportionment of costs of Inquiry and any other specific matter pertaining to the Inquiry. 6.2.5 Time limit for completion of Inquiry The Inquiry shall be completed within a period of 4 months from the date of commencement of Inquiry. The Registrar is empowered to extend the time period by another two months for reasons to be recorded in writing. However, the time stipulations will not vitiate the legality of the statutory Inquiry Report. 6.2.6 Powers of the Inquiry Officer An Inquiry Officer is vested with the following powers- i. Free access to books, accounts, documents, records etc., belonging to the Society ii. Summon person/s in possession of the above mentioned documents or persons having knowledge about the affairs of the Society at any place at the headquarters of the society or any branch or any other place and examine on oath. iii. Seize the books, accounts or documents and retain upto a maximum period of 60 days. Retention beyond 60 days requires permission of the next higher authority. iv. Powers of the Civil Court in a limited measure. 6.2.7 Communication of the Inquiry Report along with findings and follow up action by MC i. The Inquiry report is required to be communicated to the MC of the Society by the Registrar along with his findings. ii. The MC or Ad hoc Committee or PIC Committee or Special Officer as the case may be, is required to place the same before the General Body or the Special General Body convened for the purpose for its information before the next GB meeting held after the date of receipt of the Inquiry Report by the Society. iii. The Registrar is empowered to ask the society to take corrective action on the findings communicated and to take action against the MC if it fails to take such action. The Registrar is also empowered to take follow-up action on the findings of the Inquiry Report and such action cannot be nullified even if the General Body negatives the findings of the Inquiry. iv. The Registrar can apportion costs of Inquiry among the Society, members or creditors demanding Inquiry and the said costs are recovered as arrears of land revenue. v. The findings of the Inquiry report forms the basis for the issue of direction by the Registrar for placing an employee of a society under suspension who has committed misappropriation or found responsible for breach of trust or other offences in relation to the Society. vi. The findings also forms basis for Surcharge and for winding up of Societies. 31 32

6.3 Inspection 6.3.1 Authority to hold an Inspection The Registrar is vested with the powers to institute an Inspection against a Cooperative Society. The Inspections can be conducted by the Registrar himself or by any person authorized by him by a general or special order directing such person to inspect the books of the Society. 6.3.2 Basis for Inspection The Inspection can be ordered by Registrar in two situations i. He may order an Inspection either based on the knowledge he has about the Society ii. He may order on the basis of an application made by a creditor of a society. However, the Registrar can take cognizance of the application by the creditor only if he satisfied that i. Certain amount from the Society is due to the creditor, ii. The creditor demanded the payment due to him from the Society but, iii. The creditor has not received satisfaction of his claim within a reasonable time. In addition the creditor must deposit a sum of money as security towards the cost of Inspection instituted at the instance of the creditor. 6.3.3 Time limit for completion of Inspection The Inspection shall be completed within a period of 3 months from the date of order of Inspection. The Registrar is empowered to extend the time period by another two months for reasons to be recorded in writing. However, the time stipulations will not vitiate the legality of the statutory Inspection Report. 6.3.4 Powers of the Inspection Officer An Inspection Officer is empowered to summon and examine documents and persons on par with an Inquiry Officer under the Act i.e., he can have i. Free access to books, accounts, documents, records etc., belonging to the Society i i Summon person/s in possession of the above mentioned documents or persons having knowledge about the affairs of the Society at any place at the headquarters of the society or any branch or any other place and examine on oath. iii. Seize the books, accounts or documents and retain upto a maximum period of 60 days. Retention beyond 60 days requires permission of the next higher authority. 6.3.5 Communication of the Inspection Report along with findings and follow up action by MC The communication of the Inspection Report and follow-up action by MC is similar to follow-up action on Inquiry Report. 6.3.6 Inspection by Financing Bank or Federal Society The financing bank or a federal Society is empowered to inspect the books of the Society indebted to it. Such an Inspection can be conducted by an Officer of the financing bank or federal society authorized by the MC or its paid employee certified by the Registrar as competent to undertake such Inspections. Such Officer will have free access to the books of the Society, to call for such information, statements and returns as may be necessary to examine the financial condition of the Society and safety of sums lent to it by the Bank. The follow up action on the Inspection Report submitted by the authorized officer or member is similar to the Inspection instituted by the Registrar. 33 34

6.4 Surcharge 6.4.1 Introduction Surcharge is the civil liability fixed on any person responsible for misappropriation of funds or property of the society. 6.4.2 Basis for Surcharge Proceedings The Registrar may initiate Surcharge proceedings against any of the officials and employees of Cooperative Society, both present and past based on the information or reports received during the conduct of Audit, Inquiry, Inspection and Winding-up Proceedings of a Society. 6.4.3 Persons Liable for Surcharge Surcharge Proceedings can be initiated for misappropriation or fraudulent retention of money or breach of trust or causing any deficiency to the assets of the society or making payment contrary to the bye-laws against i. Person who is / was entrusted with the organization, affairs or management of the Society ii. Any past or present officer or servant of the Society. 6.4.4 Authority to initiate Surcharge Surcharge proceedings have to be initiated by the Registrar himself or by any person specially authorized by him to carryout this task. 6.4.5 Procedure to be followed The Surcharge Proceedings contemplates an independent Inquiry by the Registrar and any prior report forming basis for Surcharge constitutes only a preliminary report. The Surcharge Inquiry into the conduct of the person may be done by a personal examination of the records connected with Audit, Inquiry or Inspection once again even though such examination was conducted by other statutory officers earlier. The conduct referred to in the provision pertains to the involvement of the person to be surcharged and the role played by him directly or indirectly in the alleged Act of omission or commission. The person proposed to be surcharged is required to be given an opportunity of making his representation invariably by giving him a detailed Surcharge Notice setting out the circumstances and full details of the case which led to the initiation of Surcharge. The Surcharge order must specify the money or property to be repaid by the delinquent along with reasonable rate of interest by way of compensation in respect of the misappropriation, misapplication of funds, fraudulent retention, breach of trust or willful negligence committed by him. Surcharge will not absolve the delinquent from the criminal liability incurred by him. 6.4.6 Execution of Surcharge Orders Any sum ordered for recovery under Surcharge may be recovered i. on a requisition being made in this behalf by the Registrar to the Collector in the same manner as arrears of land revenue or ii. Through a Civil Court or iii. Through Officers of Cooperation Department deeming as if an Execution Petition has been filed. 35 36

CHAPTER – 7 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES [Sections 61 to 63 and Rules 48 to 50] 7.1. Introduction Method of Arbitration is provided in order to ensure early settlement of disputes, both monetary and non– monetary, without having to approach a Civil Court. However, the procedure similar to Civil Court proceedings is to be followed. 7.2. Disputes which may be taken up for Arbitration All disputes touching the constitution, management or any claim regarding the business of the society except a dispute regarding disciplinary action taken by the society against a paid employee has to be referred to the Registrar for Arbitration. Such a reference is popularly known as Arbitration Reference Case (ARC). However, disputes relating to Election to MC have to be referred to Cooperative Tribunal. The financing bank may proceed against the society or its members for recovery of moneys due to it when the Society fails to take action for recovery. Civil disputes such as disputes relating to transfer of property, consumer disputes, summary recovery proceedings for which there is separate provision in the act do not come under the purview of Arbitration. 7.3. Parties to the disputes The parties to the dispute can be as tabulated below S.No. Parties on one side Parties on the other side 7.4 Action to be taken by Registrar on receipt of Arbitration Reference i) To examine whether the Plaint copy with supporting documents and fee remittance challan is filed or not ii) To examine whether the dispute referred is within the scope of the Act iii) To decide whether to take up the case himself or transfer to for disposal to any person authorized by Government 7.5 Procedure of hearing and deciding the disputes The arbitrator shall record a brief note of the evidence of the parties and witnesses who attended and after consideration of any documentary evidence produced by the parties shall pass a decision in accordance with justice, equity and good conscience in writing. Pending final decision on the subject, the Arbitrator may pass interlocutory orders in the interest of justice. 7.6 Limitation The limitations prescribed under the Indian Limitation Act 1963 are applicable to the disputes under the Act. However, the limitation for the disputes between the Society and its committee and any past committee, any officer, agent or employee shall be six years from the occurrence and for election disputes it is one month from the date declaration of results. 7.7. Time Limit - for completion : The ARC is expected to be completed with in Sir marths of reference. Members, past members and persons claiming through members, past members and deceased members i) Members, past members and persons claiming through members, past members and deceased members Society, its committee or any officer, agent or employee of the Society Any past committee, any officer, agent or employee or his legal representative Any other society ii) Members, past members or persons claiming through a member, past member or deceased member iii) The society or its committee iv) Society 37 38

CHAPTER – 8 EXECUTION [Sections 70 TO 74 and Rules 52, 54, 55 & 55A] 8. Introduction Decisions or Orders issued by the Registrar under various provisions of the Act are executable. The decisions or orders can be of two types, monetary and non-monetary. 8.1. Execution of Monetary Decisions or Orders (Popularly known as Decrees) Important monetary decisions or orders are as follows a) Amount ordered to be recovered from a member of a MC or an employee of the Society responsible for misappropriation or loss to the Society under Surcharge Orders under Section 60 b) Amount ordered to be recovered for recovery of debts to a Society under a Certificate under Section 71 or under an Award under Section 62 The manner of execution of these decisions or orders is as follows i. The Decree holder has to apply for Execution to the concerned Divisional Cooperative Officer (DlCO) or the Office on Special Duty (OSD), District Cooperative Central Bank (DCCB) in respect of Societies financed by DCCB along with necessary fee ii. The DlCO / OSD shall prepare a demand notice against the defaulter and forward it to a Sale Officer to serve on the defaulter iii. If the defaulter does not pay as required in the demand notice the Sale Office shall proceed first against the movable and then immovable property of the defaulter iv. If a defaulter dies before the decree has been fully satisfied the proceedings will be initiated against the legal representative v. The Registrar or authorized person for recovery of any amount has been vested with the powers of a Civil Court 8.2. Execution of Non-monetary Decisions or Orders Important non-monetary decisions or orders are decisions on matters like membership, allotment of plots etc., passed under an Award under Section 62, an Appeal by Cooperative Tribunal under Section 76, a Revision by Registrar or Government under Section 77 and a Review by Cooperative Tribunal, Registrar or Government. All non-monetary decisions are executed by the Civil Courts having jurisdiction as if such order or decision is a decree of that Court on a certificate issued by the Registrar or any person authorized by him in his behalf. 39 40

CHAPTER - 9 WINDING UP [Sections 64 to 69-B and Rules 50, 50A & 51] 9.1. Introduction Winding up refers to the act of brining an end to the affairs of a Cooperative Society. The process of winding up is called Liquidation. The liquidation process is to be resorted to only after exhausting all other possible steps to revive the society. 9.2. Circumstances warranting Winding up The Registrar can order winding up of a Society either suo-moto or on the basis of application of not less than two – thirds of the members. The suo-moto order can be given in the following circumstances i. On the basis of Inquiry held under Section 51 or an Inspection conducted under Section 52 ii. Where the membership falls below the statutory minimum of ten iii. Where the Society has not commenced working within a period of two years in case of Urban Bank or Housing Society or within a period of one year in case of other societies or ceased to work for the last three years and was classified as “defunct” as per Audit. iv. Where the Society conducts its affairs in a manner detrimental to the interests of its members or the promotion of the object for which it has been registered. 9.3. Opportunity before Winding up Order The Order for winding up is to be passed only after giving the Society an opportunity of making representation by issuing a Show Cause Notice. Generally the Financing Bank is also consulted before passing the Order. The Notice can be issued in any one of the following manners i. By publication in A.P. Gazette or ii By Publication in local news papers or iii. By tom-tom in the village and affixture to the notice board of the society and in case no office exists for the society, in the panchayat office. 9.4. Appointment of Liquidator A Liquidator is appointed after passing the winding up order. On taking charge as Liquidator, the properties and assets of the Society vest in the Liquidator. Similarly, the liabilities devolve on him. 9.5. Functions and powers of the Liquidator The Liquidator has to take measures for winding up of the Society under the general control of the Registrar. He has power to carry on the business of the society and to do all acts and execute all documents necessary for beneficial winding up. He can exercise the following powers, among others i. to institute and defend suits on behalf of the Society ii. to realize the assets of the Society iii. to determine the contribution to be made by the members / past members etc., iv. to investigate all claims against the Society and decide priorities v. to pay claims against the Society including interests upto the date of winding up vi. to determine the costs of liquidation to be borne by various persons vii. to compromise with creditors or persons to whom society is liable to pay viii.to raise any money required on the security of the assets of the society. ix. to take action for recovery of dues and dispose off the surpluses etc., 41 42

The Liquidator is a public officer and hence no suit can be filed or continued against him without prior permission of the Registrar. The actions of the Liquidator cannot be called in question in Civil Courts as the APCS Act itself provides for remedies by way of Appeal, Revision and Review. 9.6. Discharge of Liabilities & Disposal of Surplus Assets The assets realized by the Liquidator should be utilized for payment to creditors and such payment should be on a pro-rata basis. The priority should be fixed in the order of Government dues, Secured Loans, Unsecured Loans, Share Capital, Dividends etc. Interest on deposits of outsiders is payable only upto the date of winding up. If all the liabilities has been met, the Surplus assets should be distributed in the following order i. to meet the objects of the Society as contained in the bye-laws ii. where there are no bye-laws, the Registrar will hold the surplus amount in trust and transfer it to the reserve fund of a new Society to be formed with similar objects within a period of 3 years iii. If no new society is registered within 3 years, the Registrar may distribute the surplus to (1) The Federal Society with similar objects or (2) A.P. State Cooperative Union or (3) any charitable purpose stated in the Charitable Endowments Act 1890 9.7. Final Closure Report After all the assets are realized and liabilities are discharged, the liquidator has to call a General Body Meeting and place a summary of action taken by him. The Liquidator has to submit a final closure report to the Registrar containing statements of receipts and payments, final audit report, resolution passed by the GB, statement of assets and liabilities etc. 9.8. Cancellation of Registration The Registrar will pass an order of cancellation of the Registration of the Society if he is satisfied with the liquidation proceedings as contained in the final closure report. The Society ceases to exist as a body corporate from the date of cancellation of Registration. 9.9. Restoration of a Society wound up Before the affairs of the society are completely wound up, the Registrar is competent to withdraw the winding up orders and restore it to a committee. The assets, properties, claims or liabilities as on the date of such restoration will devolve on the Society. 9.10. Termination of Liquidation Proceedings The winding up proceedings are required to be closed within a period of 3 years from the date of winding up order. After the expiry of 3 years, the liquidation proceedings will be deemed to have been terminated and the Registrar shall pass orders terminating the liquidation proceedings. 43 44

CHAPTER - 10 APPEAL, REVISION AND REVIEW [Sections 75 to 78 and Rules 49-A and 56] 10.1. Introduction Separate Courts, called Cooperative Tribunals have been established to deal with cases / appeals under the Act. Cooperative Tribunals, Appeal, Revision and Review have been made in the Act. 10.2. Cooperative Tribunals The Government have constituted four Cooperative Tribunals at Hyderabad, Warangal, Vijayawada and Visakhapatnam for speedy disposal of appeals arising under the provisions of the Act. The Tribunals consist of a Chairman who is judicial officer not below the rank of a District Judge and not more than two members of the rank of Additional Registrars of Cooperative Societies. The working of the Tribunals is governed by the Andhra Pradesh Cooperative Tribunal (Procedure) Rules, 1994. 10.3. Appeal 10.3.1. An appeal lies to Tribunal against the orders passed in the following matters- i. Registration of a Society (Section 6) ii. Restriction and creation of any charge, De- registration of a Society and appointment of official assignee (section 9A, 9B, 9C) iii. Transfer of assets and liabilities, division, amalgamation and conversion of certain Societies (Section 12A) iv. Exercise of option by members and creditors (Section 13) v. Amendment of byelaws of a Society (Section) vi. Partnership of Society (Section 17) vii. Eligibility for membership (Section 19) viii. Disqualification for membership of society (Section 21) ix. Disqualification for membership of Committee (Section 21A) x. Cessation of membership of Committee (Section 21 AA) xi. Expulsion of members (Section 23) xii. Cessation of President on failure to convene committee meetings [Section 32 (3)] xiii. Supersession of the Committee (Section 34) xiv. Motion of no-confidence in the President and Vice – President of the Committee (Section 34A) xv. Surcharge (Section 60) xvi. Arbitration reference (Section 62) xvii. Winding up of Societies (Section 64) xviii. Power of the Liquidator (Section 66) xix. Power of the Registrar to recover amounts by attachment and sale of property and execution of orders (Section 70) xx. Recovery of debts (Section 71) xxi. Attachment of property before decision or order (Section 73) and xxii. Delivery of possession of records and properties of a society (Section 117) xxiii. Election original petitions as per section 61(3) or rule 49-B. 10.3.2. The jurisdiction of the Tribunal is barred in respect of any order of withdrawal or transfer of a dispute under Section 62(3) . The Registrar is also empowered to refer any matter to Tribunal in respect of cases where appeal lies to it to examine legality or propriety of any decision or order passed by the Lower Authority. 45 46

10.3.3 . Appeals shall lie within sixty days from the date of communication to the appellant of the decision/order complained against. However, the Tribunal is empowered to admit an appeal after the expiry of sixty days by condoning the delay if it is satisfied that the appellant had sufficient cause for not preferring the appeal within the sixty days. 10.4. Revision Revision, in general sense, refers to re- examination of matters to ensure correctness of action of sub-ordinate officers. Revision under the Act refers to i. The power vested in the Registrar of the State (not Functional Registrar or persons delegated powers of Registrar) against the orders of the sub-ordinate officers ii. The power vested in Government against the orders of the Registrar of the State. The power of Registrar is either on application or suo-moto. The power of revision can be exercised in respect of all proceedings of sub-ordinate officers except in cases where appeal is provided to the Tribunal. A Revision petition should be presented within 90 days from the date on which the proceedings/decision or order to which the petition relates was communicated. The revisional authority can modify, annul, reverse or remit for re-consideration and pass appropriate orders after giving an opportunity to both the parties to represent their cases. It may allow costs to be paid either out of the funds of the society or by any party to the revision petition. The revisional authority is also empowered to suspend the order of the sub-ordinate officer pending disposal of the revision petition. 10.5. Review Review means re-viewing a matter or subject afresh by the same authority based on i. Discovery of new and important facts which were not there within the knowledge of the party or could not be produced when the order was made or ii. On the basis of some mistake or error apparent on the face of the record or iii. For any other sufficient reason. The powers of review shall be exercised only by the same authority which passed the order in appeal or revision. Review, therefore, does not lie in cases of other orders passed by the authorities under the provisions of the Act. The application for review shall be presented only once within 90 days of the communication of the order. The reviewing authority can pass orders only after affording an opportunity of representation to all the parties concerned and it is also empowered to pass interlocutory orders and to award costs to be paid out of the funds of the society or any party to the review application. 47 48

CHAPTER –11 OFFENCES AND PENALTIES [Sections 79 to 83 – C] 11.1. Introduction The offences coming within the purview of Cooperative Law cover various types of actions or omissions committed by the Managing Committee, an officer, employee or any member of the society. For misappropriation of funds prosecutions are launched after sanction of prosecution by Registrar and for other offences, action is taken under this Act. 11.2. Offences and Corrupt practices 11.2.1.The following are the major offences and punishments prescribed in the Act. 11.2.2. The actions treated as corrupt practices with the punishments are as follows Offences Furnishing false return or false information on a lawful order or direction issued under the Act or to gain admission or to continue as member of a society or to get elected to the M.C. or to continue as member of the society or an officer of the society. Disobedience of any summons, requisition or order or direction under the Act wilfully an without reasonable cause. Failure to furnish any information lawfully required from him by an authorised person under the Act. Penalties Punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months and with a fine up to Rs.500/- Contravention of the instructions on creating first charge of a society on certain movable assets of a member for the amount due by him or on the immovable property borrowed by him or on conducting transactions with non-members by a Primary Weavers Cooperative Society. Wilful failure to produce Books of Accounts, documents, records, cash etc., belonging to or in the custody of the society. Misappropriation of the property of the society entrusted to his care. Possession of the stolen property of the society. Abetment of any offence punishable under the Act. Punishabl with imprison ment for a minimum of one year to seven years. Punishable with imprison ment up to 5 years Punishable with imprison ment for a minimum of six months extendable up to five years Corrupt practices Irregularity in sanction or receipt of loans or in purchases are causing deficit in stocks. Accepting illegal gratification in the discharge of official duties. Irregular use of funds of the society. Punishments Punishable with imprisonment upto 1 year or with fine upto Rs.5,000/- or both. 49 50

11.2.3. The use of the word ‘Cooperative’ or its equivalent in any Indian language by any person or firm other than a Cooperative Society is prohibited without the sanction of the Government. Any violation is punishable with fine upto Rs.200/- in the first instance and with Rs. 50/- for each day in case of continuing offence. 11.2.4. The Committee or an Officer or an employee of a Society who fails to implement a non-monetary Award given by an Arbitrator or order passed Cooperative Tribunal is punishable with fine upto Rs. 500/- 11.2.5. Any Society or any Officer or member or any other person contravening the provisions of Act for which no specific punishment is provided under the relevant provision is punishable with fine upto Rs. 50/-. 11.3. Cognizance of Offences The offences cannot be tried by any Court inferior to that of Magistrate of the first class or the Metropolitan Magistrate. Every offence is to be treated as cognizable offence under Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. The previous sanction of the Registrar is also made mandatory for launching prosecutions. 11.4. Recovery presumption as to commission of offences The following presumptions are made in respect of commission of Offences and Statements recorded under Inquiry and Inspection 11.5. Loss to be recovered in addition to Criminal Liability The Act also stipulates that any Member, Officer or Employee of a Society committing offences discussed above is responsible to make good the loss caused to the Society besides incurring criminal liability. The Registrar or Authorized person is empowered to recover the said loss as arrears of land revenue from the responsible person or his legal heirs or any person who acquired the properties from such a person. Signing minutes book of the meetings of the MC without actually attending Passing a meeting without proper Quorum as valid Offence / Statements Drawl of amounts and failure to disburse or account for it. Failure to produce the books and properties under his custody / his possession on requisition by Registrar or Authorized person Making false entries or manipulates or alters the books of accounts Misuse of the properties of Society in contravention of Law Execution of documents or entering into agreement for sale or purchase of land in contravention of bye-laws Issue of any receipt by a member Officer or Employee of the Society Statements recorded under statutory Inquiry or Inspection by the Registrar of authorized person Presumption as to Misappropriation of said amount Wilful withholding of the books and properties Committing the same wilfully to cause loss or damage to Society Misuse for his personal benefit Done for personal benefit to cause loss to the Society Issue in the name of the Society and responsible to account for the same. Statement within the meaning of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 51 52

PART II Andhra Pradesh Mutually Aided Cooperative Societies Act, 1995. CHAPTER-12 INTRODUCTION In early 1960s, co-operative legislation all over the country underwent major changes to ensure State participation in promotion of cooperatives and to bring subsidized credit, production inputs at the door step of the disadvantaged sections of people in rural areas. In Andhra Pradesh too, at this time, the Andhra Pradesh Cooperative Societies Act,1964 was enacted. Over the years, however, increased State participation in the financing and management of Cooperative has led to an unfortunate situation where cooperatives themselves have started to perceive themselves not as member-controlled, member-sensitive businesses guided by the universally accepted principles of cooperation, but as channels for government subsidies and largesse. At a time when deregulation and liberalization enabled other sectors of economy to break new ground, it was considered necessary to allow cooperatives also to have the same degree of freedom to conduct their affairs to enable them compete successfully with other sectors. Cooperative financing organizations, such as the NABARD, NCDC, NDDB etc., have also desired to deal directly with cooperative societies without involvement of Government. On the other hand, the Government also realised that cooperatives not dependent on Government funds need to be given greater autonomy to voluntarily promote and effectively develop services for themselves through their own cooperative societies. In the backdrop of the above changed perception, several work shops have been held during the year 1994 and the State Legislature has enacted Andhra Pradesh Mutually Aided Cooperative Societies Act, 1995 (AP MACS Act) in order to promote cooperative societies as accountable, competitive, self-reliant and autonomous business enterprises based on thrift, self-help and mutually aid, owned, managed and controlled by the members for their economic and social betterment and to make the Co-operative movement more vibrant in the State. In this Act, no Rule making power is vested to the Government. 53

CHAPTER 13 REGISTRATION [Sections 4, 5, 6 and 9] 13.1. Societies which can be registered and conditions for registration A minimum of 10 individuals each being a member of a different family or two or more Societies registered under the MACS Act wish to form a federation, may apply for registration of a Society to the Registrar concerned. A Society registered under APCS Act 1964 may also apply to the Registrar for conversion into a MAC Society. Application for registration is to be submitted by hand or by registered post accompanied by the following particulars. i. Original copy of the bye-laws of the proposed Society as adopted by the individuals of a newly proposed Society /General Body of the existing Society under APCS Act. ii. Names of individuals (promoters) with their addresses, occupations and their financial commitment i i i True copy of the minutes adopting the byelaws signed by majority of members and minutes intending conversion as the case may be. iv. Registration fee amounting to 1% of the authorized share capital subject to a minimum of Rs.100/- and a maximum of Rs.10, 000/- v. In case of a Society registered under APCS Act and wishing to convert itself into MACS, evidence has to be shown that it is not in receipt of any Government share capital, loans or guarantees at the time of applying for registration or that it has entered into an Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the Government for any such outstanding loans or guarantees. 13.2. Scope of Bye-laws The bye-laws of a Society should cover the following matters specifically: i. the objects of the Society and the services to be provided to the members ii. eligibility and ineligibility of members and eligibility to vote in elections iii. minimum performance expected from members and the consequences in the event of default iv. the powers and functions of General Body, Board and the Chair Person v. the manner and frequency of convening general meetings and board meetings and the quorum required there for vi. the term of office of directors and size, composition, eligibility and ineligibility of the Board of Directors vii. the manner of conducting elections and of filling casual vacancies viii.penalties for acting against the interests of the Society and for non-fulfilment of duties and the nature and extent of liability of office bearers/directors for debts contracted ix. appointment of authorized signatory, manner of choosing delegates x. the nature and amount of capital, maximum capital a single member can hold, maximum interest payable to members on the share capital xi. raising of funds/resources and their utilization, constitution and disposal of funds, appointment of auditors/internal auditors xii. manner of dissolution etc. 54 55

13.3. Procedure for registration and action on the application for registration If the application fulfils the above conditions and if the name of the proposed Society is not the same as that of a Society already registered under the APCS Act or the APMACS Act, the Registrar has to register the Society along with the bye-laws, issue a registration certificate and communicate to the Chief Promoter within a period of 60 days from the date of application. In case of non fulfillment of the above conditions, the Registrar has to send a refusal order together with the reasons there for, within 60 days from the date of application. A Society may be registered with limited liability or un limited liability. The Society once registered acquires the status of a body corporate. CHAPTER 14 MEMBERSHIP [Sections 19, 35 and Bye-Laws] 14.1 Eligibility for Membership: Any person with contractual capacity and desirous of utilizing the services of the Society and willing to accept the responsibilities of membership and fulfils the conditions specified in the Bye-laws can apply for membership of the Society. The Society can admit if it is in a position to extend its services to him. Admission and removal of membership has to be done by an elected Board or by the General Body when there is no elected Board. 14.2 Rights and liabilities of Members: The members enjoy the rights as provided in the bye-laws. The members of the society are liable for the losses of the society over and above the Deficit Cover Fund in proportion to the services availed or expected to avail. 56 57

CHAPTER 15 MANAGEMENT AND ELECTIONS [Sections 20, 22, 23, 24 & Bye-laws] 15.1 Composition of Board and its term A Society is managed by a Board of Directors elected by the members eligible to vote . The size of the Board will be a multiple of the term of office of its directors ex: if the term of office is 5 years, the size of the Board can be 5, 10, 15 and so on. The Board of Directors will have staggered term. At the first election each director’s term is to be decided by lots. At any given time, vacancies on account of retirement of directors are to be less than half of the strength of the Board. The Chairperson will be elected by the Board of Directors. However, the term of a director should not be more than five years . 15.2 Eligibility to become a Director A member intending to contest for the post of a director should be a voting member of the Society for at least two years immediately preceding the year of elections and he must have attended two General Body meetings of the Society held immediately preceding the elections if the society is in existence for more than two years. A person who has, at any time, lost the right to vote as a member or to continue as a director or incurring any disqualification as per the bye-laws, is not eligible to contest in the election. 15.3 Disqualifications A person ceases to be a director if he absents himself from three consecutive Board Meetings without leave of absence or absents himself from the General Body meeting without leave of absence or is penalized under the Act. The Directors will not be eligible to continue as such and will not be eligible for being chosen as directors for a period of three years if they did not conduct elections before the expiry of the terms, did not conduct their annual general body meeting in time and the requisitioned meeting of the general body within the specified time or they did not place the audited accounts and the report of the auditor before the annual general meeting. 15.4 Eligibility Criteria of a member to vote A member can exercise his rights of membership including right to vote on fulfilment of the stipulations in the bye-laws and if he had been a member for at least one year before exercising his vote. However, this stipulation is not applicable to the promoter members in the first year of registration. 15.5 Conduct of elections The responsibility for conducting elections before the expiry of the term of the directors lies with the board in office. Where the Board does not take steps for conducting elections before the expiry of their term or where there are no directors remaining on the Board to conduct elections, the Registrar may at the request of not less then 25 members or 5 percent of the total members of the Society, whichever is less or suo-moto may convene a General Body meeting within 30 days for appointing an ad- hoc board for conducting elections. The term of the ad-hoc board can be for a maximum period of one month plus the minimum period required to conduct elections. When the regular Board is put in place, the ad-hoc board ceases to exist. In the first instance, election should be held for the total strength of the Board. Fresh elections are to be conducted to fill up the vacancies at the respective intervals. A Director can contest any number of times subject to the provisions of the bye-laws and if he is not otherwise disqualified. The elections are to be conducted as per bye-laws. 15.6 Recording of Election Proceedings The proceedings of the election are to be recorded in the minutes book of the Society by the Election officer. 58 59

Fresh elections are to be conducted to fill the vacancies at the respective intervals. A retiring director is eligible for re-election if the byelaws so provide 15.7 Important Powers & Functions of the Board of Directors i. Admit and terminate membership and review of its membership in other Cooperatives ii. Election and removal of Chairperson and other office bearers iii. Framing the following policies a. Recruitment and removal of chief executive and fix service conditions of staff such as recruitment, salaries etc. b. Organization and provision of services to members c. Custody, mobilization, utilization and investment of funds and manner of keeping accounts d. Monitoring and management information systems and filing of returns e. Placing of Annual Report, Financial Statements, Annual Plan and budget for the approval of the General Body and undertaking functionaries delegated by the General Body. 15.8 General Body The General Body of a Society is the ultimate authority subject to the provisions of the Act and its bye- laws. The Society may constitute a representative General Body, if required, for effective decision making. 15.9 Board Meetings & General Meetings The Board of a Society has to meet at least once in every 3 months. The General Body shall meet at least once a year. On requisition received from at least one tenth (1/10 th ) of the members, the Board has to convene the General Meeting. When the Board fails, the Registrar can convene the General Body. In accordance with the Byelaws, the Chairperson will preside at the meeting of the Board and the General Body. Every Society has to record all proceedings of every General Body and Board of Directors meeting in the minutes book and communicate such minutes to all the invitees with in thirty days of the conclusion of the meeting. The person who chaired the meetings has to sign the minutes recorded. The Chairperson enjoys powers delegated by the Board. 15.10 Supersession of the Board The Registrar has no power to supersede the Board of a Society, unless it is a Society/Bank coming under Banking Regulation Act, 1949 in which case he has to abide by the directives of the RBI. 60 61

CHAPTER – 16 REGULATION (Audit, Special Audit, Inquiry and Recovery) (Sections 27, 28, 29, 30, 31 & 33) 16.1 Audit A Society may get its accounts audited by a Chartered Accountant or by any auditor of the Department of Cooperation. The General Body is competent to appoint an Auditor and fix remuneration by a resolution and such resolution is valid only until the close of the next succeeding annual General Body meeting. 16.2 Special Audit A Society dealing with funds from the Government or other external individual or institutions may be subjected to a Special Audit on the request of a creditor and the Registrar may appoint a Special Auditor. Special Audit has to be completed within 120 days of its commencement and report submitted to the Registrar. 16.3 Inquiry The Registrar may of his own motion and shall on the application of a federation to which the Cooperative Society concerned is affiliated, or of a Creditor of the Society, or of 1/3 directors or of 1/10 th of the members hold or cause an inquiry to be made into the specific matters relating to any gross violation of the Act by the Society. The Inquiry has to be completed within 120 days of the order failing which the order lapses. The Inquiry report is to be communicated to the Society concerned and the applicant concerned or any person on payment of fees and to the Cooperative Tribunal where necessary, within 30 days of completion of Inquiry. The Inquiry Officer has to specifically state the amount of deficiency, waste or loss appeared to have been caused by the gross negligence or misconduct of any person in the performance of his duties. 16.4 Action on Special Audit or Inquiry Report Registrar has to communicate copies of the Special Audit Report to the applicant-creditor, the Society concerned and the Cooperative Tribunal if necessary, for taking action within 30 days from the date of receipt. The Cost of Special Audit is to be borne by the Creditor applying for it. If the Special Audit reveals serious mismanagement in the Society, the cost of Special Audit may be recovered from the Society or the persons responsible for the mismanagement. The Registrar, without prejudice to civil or criminal proceedings, may direct the Board of the Society to convene a General Body meeting within a reasonable time to enable him to bring to its notice the findings of the Special Audit report for necessary action. If the Special audit reveals serious lapses such as payments contrary to law, deficiency of amounts, waste, loss or gross negligence, misconduct etc., such loss amounts may be ordered to be recovered from the Society or the persons responsible for mismanagement by the Tribunal on the application of a Member, Director, Chairperson or the Registrar. 16.5 Powers of the Tribunal to order recovery Tribunal can order for recovery of the amounts from the person responsible for the deficiency or loss or unprofitable outlay occasioned by his negligence on an application from a member, director, Chairperson or Registrar on filing a copy of the Special Audit Report or the Inquiry report. The Tribunal disallows irregular expenditure and orders for recovery from the person responsible for such deficiency / loss / unprofitable outlay due to his negligence / misconduct. The Tribunal is expected to issue a reasoned order for its decision after giving an opportunity of making representation and serves a copy on the delinquent as per the procedure laid down in Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The orders of Tribunal are appealable in the High Court within sixty days of receipt of such order. The orders of Tribunal / High Court are executed in the same manner as a decree of a Civil Court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 and under the provision contained in Chapter X of A.P.C.S. Act, 1964. 62 63

CHAPTER – 17 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES AND EXECUTION [Sections 36 and 37] 17.1 Introduction A Society may set up its own system in the Bye- laws for settlement of disputes touching its constitution management or business. It can approach Society/ Tribunal only after all such remedies available in the Bye- laws are exhausted. 17.2 Parties to the Disputes i. Disputes among members, past members or persons claiming through members, past members and deceased members. ii. Disputes between a member, past member or a person claiming through member, past member or deceased member and co-operative Society, its board, director, office bearer or liquidator , past or present iii. Disputes between the Co-operative Society or its Board and any past Board, Director or the nominee, heirs or legal representatives of any eceased Director, deceased officer of the Society iv. Disputes between the Co-operative Societies v. Disputes between the promoters of a Society and the Registrar. vi. Disputes between the Society and Registrar vii. Disputes between the liquidators of two or more societies. The disputes relating to elections of a Society are to be referred to the Co-operative Tribunal for decision. 17.3 Execution of Decisions, Decrees and Orders The procedure for execution of Decisions, Decrees and Orders is the same as applicable in the case of a Society registered under A.P.C.S. Act 1964. However, all references to the Registrar are to be construed as references to the Tribunal. CHAPTER – 18 DISSOLUTION [Section 39 to 44] 18.1 Introduction Dissolution of a Society can be authorized by its members or by a Cooperative Tribunal 18.2 Dissolution by members A notice of the General Body meeting for dissolution of the Society has to be sent to the Registrar, federations if any, creditors and partnership organizations who will have a right to make a representation to the General Body against dissolution. A special resolution is to be passed authorizing its own dissolution and within 150 days a copy of it is to be sent to Registrar by Registered Post to dissolve the Society. On receipt of the special resolution the Registrar shall cause publication of the same in a newspaper once a wek for two weeks or distribute the resolution in the district where the registered office of the Society is located at the expense of the Society. 18.3 Appointment of Liquidator The General Body or the Tribunal, as the case may be, may appoint the liquidator. Where a Liquidator is not appointed the Registrar may appoint a Liquidator to wind up the affairs of the society or where he is satisfied that the society has no assets and liabilities, issue a certificate or dissolution. 18.4 Dissolution by Tribunal The Registrar or an interested person may apply to the Tribunal for dissolution of a Society after giving ninety days notice to the Society and to the Registrar, if the society i. Obtained registration by fraud or mistake ii. is serving illegal purposes iii. has wilfully violated the Act, Bye-laws, even after notice by Registrar 64 65

iv. is not running in accordance with the principles of Cooperation v. has not commenced business within two years of the date of Registration vi. has not carried on business for the past two consecutive years After receipt of application for dissolution, the Tribunal after giving an opportunity to the Society may order that the society be dissolved or liquidated and dissolved under the supervision of the Registrar. CHAPTER –19 OFFENCES AND PENALTIES [Section 38] 19.1 Offences The following will be treated as offences under the Act i. failure to give notice, send a return or document violating the provisions of Act and bye-laws ii. wilful negligence or refusal to furnish the information required for the purposes of the Act or bye- laws iii. filing a false or insufficient return / information violating the provisions of Act and Bye-laws. An offence by a Cooperative Society will be deemed to be an offence committed by each office bearer bound by the bye-laws unless the office bearer or director proves to have attempted to prevent the commission of the offence. Any person contravening the provisions of the Act and bye-laws will also be deemed to have committed an offence under the Act. 19.2 Penalties Offences are punishable with imprisonment upto one year or with a fine up to Rs.10,000/- or with both. 66 67

Admission disqualification and expulsion of members are the exclusive prerogative of the cooperative 5. Membership In matters of admission, disqualification and expulsion of members, the Registrar has final say Size, term, composition of board left to bye-laws; staggered terms; elections by incumbent board failing which by ad-hoc committee; disqualification of all directors for not conducting elections in time, for not conducting general body meetings in time, for not placing audited accounts before annual general meeting 6. Management Size of board fixed; term of board fixed; composition of board fixed; elections by Registrar; reservations on board All staff fully accountable to cooperative; deputationists from government and other organisations possible if a cooperative so deserves 7. Share capital Government and other non-members may contribute share capital Cooperatives may mobilise funds within the limits fixed by bye-laws 9. Mobilisation of funds Cooperatives may mobilise funds within the limits fixed by Registrar No restriction in investment in own business, but other investments to be in any non-speculative manner specified by bye-laws 10. Investment of funds Investment of funds even in own business restricted, lending limits are fixed by Registrar Deficit is required to be analysed and dealt with on an annual basis; members have to meet the deficit in proportion to their actual use or commitment to the use of the services of the cooperative during the year, if the deficit cannot be set off against reasons 11. Deficit The issue of deficit not addressed; accumulated deficits are dealt with at the time of liquidation Audit is the responsibility of the board; auditor to be chartered accountant or from Registrar’s office at cooperative’s discretion; non presentation of audit report to general body in stipulated time results in disqualification of all directors 12. Audit Audit is the responsibility of audit wing of the department, choice of auditors not available to cooperatives; no penalty for non-conduct of audit 70 71

Subsidiary organisations may be set up by a cooperative 13. Subsidiaries No subsidiary organisation can be set up by a cooperative Same as available in 1964 Act 14.
