PART III MULTI - STATE COOPERATIVE SOCITIES ACT, 2002. CHAPTER 21 INTRODUCTION The Central Government enacted the Multi-Unit Cooperative Societies Act (MUCS Act) 1942 to facilitate the organization, administration and functioning of Cooperative Societies with area of operation extended to more than one State. A number of National Level Cooperative Societies and Federations have come up with the assistance of the Central and State Governments. The provisions in the Cooperative laws in different States governing these multi state cooperative societies varied from one another. The lack of uniformity and inadequacies in the then existing arrangements rendered it necessary to replace the Multi Unit Cooperative Societies Act, 1942 by a common comprehensive central legislation namely the Multi State Cooperative Societies Act, 1984(MSCS Act) providing for Central Authority to be responsible for their registration, promotion and supervision. With the increasing demand for more autonomy for democratic management with lesser control from the Central or State Governments, a Model Cooperative Law was suggested by the Choudhary Brahm Perkash Committee. The Multi State Cooperative Societies Act, 2002 has been enacted based on this Model Cooperative Law. 74

CHAPTER 22 REGISTRAION [Sections 5, 6, 7, 8 and 16, Rules 3, 4, 5, and 12] 22.1 Society which may be registered A society, with the main object to serve the interests of the members in more than one State and bye- laws of which provide for social and economic betterment of its members through self-help and mutual aid in accordance with the cooperative principles, can be registered as a Multi-State Cooperative Society. 22.2 Procedure for Registration The application for registration of a Society shall be made to the Central Registrar( Government of India) and such application shall be signed by, i. at least by fifty persons from each of the State concerned in case the members of the proposed society are individuals, ii. by the authorised representatives on behalf of at least five societies which are not registered in the same State in case members of the proposed society are cooperative societies, iii. authorised representatives of the each of the society, in case the members of the proposed society are another Multi-State Cooperative Society and other cooperative societies and not less than two of the cooperative societies shall have not been registered in the same Sate, iv. fifty persons being individuals from each of the two States or more and one Cooperative Society each from two states or more, or one Multi-State Cooperative Society in case the members of the proposed Multi- State Cooperative Society are cooperative societies or Multi-State Cooperative Societies and individuals Such application shall be accompanied by four copies of the proposed bye-laws of the proposed society and such other information as may be required by the Central Registrar 22.3 Action on the application for registration of the Society The Central Registrar after satisfying himself about the fulfilment of the conditions, may Register the Multi- State Cooperative Society and its bye-laws, and Federal Cooperative Societies, and issue a certificate of registration within four months from the date of receipt of application, unless he refuses to register the said society. The order of refusal to register a Multi-State Cooperative Society shall be communicated by registered post to the concerned, within a period of four months from the date of receipt of application for registration and the manner of communication of orders refusing the registration is the conclusive proof of refusal of the registration of the proposed society. Any inaction in registering the society or refusal within the stipulated time frame, the deemed clauses of registration are attracted. While refusing the registration the pre -condition of giving a reasonable opportunity of being heard is to be, necessarily, observed. Once the society is registered, it acquires the status of the body corporate. 22.4 Liability The name of every Multi-State Cooperative Society registered under this act is to be suffixed with the word “limited”, which denotes that the liability of the members is limited to the extent of share capital paid by them. No Multi- State Cooperative Society can be registered with unlimited liability after commencement of this Act. However, a Multi-State Cooperative Society with unlimited liability before commencement of this Act has to exercise the option within a period of one year from such commencement either to continue to function as such or to convert itself into a Multi-State Coop Society with limited liability. A Multi-State Cooperative Society may, by an amendment of its bye-laws, can change the extent of its liability. An amendment of the bye-law in this regard 75 76

shall not be registered or shall not take effect unless the conditions laid down in the Act are fulfilled. 22.5 Amendment of Bye-laws A Multi State Cooperative Society can amend its bye-laws by adopting a resolution at the meeting of its Executive Committee, pass the said resolution in its general meeting by a 2/3 rd majority of the members present and voting and apply to the Central Registrar for registration of the said amendment within 60 days of passing the resolution by the general body. The Central Registrar, if he is satisfied, may register the amendments and issue a Certified Copy of the amended bye-laws. He may also refuse the registration of the amendments and such order of refusal is appealable within 60 days from the date of order. CHAPTER – 23 MEMBERSHIP [Sections 25,26,27,28,29,30, 31,33,35,36,37 and Rule 11] 23.1 Eligibility for Membership and procedure for becoming a member Any individual competent to contract under Indian Contract Act , any Multi-State Cooperative Society or any Cooperative Society, the Central Government, a State Government, National Cooperative Development Corporation, any other Corporation owned or controlled by the Government, any Government company as defined in Section 617 of the Companies Act 1956, such class or classes of persons or association of persons as may be permitted by the Central Registrar having regard to the nature and activities of a Multi-State Cooperative Society, on application be admitted as a member of such Multi-State Cooperative Society. Every application for admission as a member of Multi-State Cooperative Society has to be disposed of by such society within four months from the date of receipt of such application. And decision of the society on such application is to be communicated within fifteen days from the date of such decision. If the application is not disposed of or decision not communicated with in the above stipulated period, it is deemed that the society has refused admission of the applicant. The society shall not admit members within 30 days prior to the date of general body meeting. 23.2 Nominal or Associate member of a society If it is provided in the bye-laws, a Multi-State Cooperative Society can admit a person as a nominal or associate member. However, no such member is entitled to subscribe to the shares of the society or have any interest in the management of the society including right to vote, get elected as director of the society or to participate in the general body meetings. 77 78

23.3 Disqualification for membership Persons carrying on conflicting business or competitive with the business of the society, persons who failed to use the services of the society below the minimum level specified in the bye-laws for two consecutive years, members who failed to attend the general meetings of the society for three consecutive general meetings without condonation and members who defaulted in payment of amount due by them to the society under the bye-laws of the society. 23.4. Expulsion of members The General Body is competent to expel any member by passing a resolution with not less than two thirds majority of the members present and voting, for acts of such members which are detrimental to the proper working of the society after giving him an opportunity for making representation. An expelled member acquires eligibility for readmission after lapse of one year from the date of such expulsion. 23.5 Eligibility to Vote Every member of the society including a member who is an employee of such society will have one vote . However, a member can exercise his voting right only on payment of his membership dues or has acquired such interest in the society as may be specified in the bye-laws of the society. 23.6. Restriction on holding of shares No member except the Central/State Government, Government owned/controlled Corporations or Companies can hold shares more than the maximum limit of one- fifth prescribed in the bye-laws of the society. 23.7. Transfer of interest on death of members On the death of a member, the society may transfer the share or interest of the deceased member to the person nominated in accordance with the bye-laws of the society. If no person is nominated, then the board may transfer to the person who appears to be the legal heir or legal representative of the deceased member, or pay to the nominee or legal heir or legal representative as the case may be, the sum equal to the value of the share or interest of the deceased member. The board must pay all the moneys due to the deceased member, to the nominee or legal heir or legal representative, as the case may be, within a period of six months of the death of the member, unless it is stopped by an order of the competent court. 23.8. Liabilities of past member and estate of deceased member The past member of the society is liable for the debts of the society as they existed on the date on which he ceased to be a member. Likewise, the liability of the estate of the deceased member exists for the debts due by him to the society as on the date of his death and shall continue for a period of two years from such date. Liability of a past member who ceased to be a member or of the estate of the deceased member who died within two years immediately preceding to the date of order of winding up shall continue until the entire liquidation proceedings are completed. 79 80

CHAPTER – 24 MANAGEMENT [Sections 38,39,40,41,43,44,45,46,49,50 and Rules 13,15 and 16] 24.1 General Body Subject to the provisions of the Act, the rules, and the bye-laws, the ultimate authority of a society vests in the General Body which consists of all the members of such society. 24.2 Annual General Meeting The board of a Society has to call the annual general meeting within the period as may be prescribed, but not later than six months after the close of the corresponding year by giving not less than fourteen days notice in writing. All the general body meetings are to be called at the principal place of the society. If the board fails to call such general meeting within the prescribed period, the Central Registrar or the person authorised by him in this regard, is competent to convene such general meeting within a period of ninety days from the date of expiry of the period specified for calling general meeting by the board and the expenditure incurred on such meeting is to be borne by the society. A Society having membership exceeding one thousand may provide in its bye-laws for constitution of a smaller body. The smaller body so constituted will exercise all powers of the general body as may be specified in its bye-laws. The quorum for the annual general meeting shall be 1/5 th of the total number of members or as prescribed in its bye-laws 24.3 Special General Meeting of General Body At any time, on the direction of the board, the Chief Executive of the society may call a special general meeting. Further, he has to call a special general meeting within one month from the date of requisition in writing from the Central Registrar for convening such meeting or on receipt of such request from such number of members or proportion of the total number of members as prescribed in the bye-laws of the society. Special general meeting of the society may be called by giving not less than seven days notice in writing. 24.4 Board of Directors Every Society must have a board of directors consisting of such number of members as may be specified in its bye-laws, the maximum being twenty one excluding two directors co-opted by the board and the functional directors of national cooperatives. The term of the elected board of the society is as specified in its bye-laws, but not exceeding five years from the date of elections. 24.5 Board Meeting The total number of meetings of the board in a year and the venue of meetings as may be specified in the bye- laws. However, the board shall meet at least once in every quarter. 24.6 Disqualification for being a member of board No one is eligible to be a member of the society if, he has been adjudged by a competent court as insolvent or of unsound mind, is concerned or participates in the profits of any contract with the society, holds any office or place of profit under the society, has been a member in the society for less than twelve months immediately preceding the date of such election or appointment, has interest in any business of the kind carried on by the society of which he is a member, defaulter in repayment of amounts/value of goods taken on credit due by him to the society, for such period and has been convicted for an offence involving moral turpitude. 24.7 Powers and functions of the board The board may exercise all such powers as may be necessary or expedient for the purpose of carrying out its functions under the Act including the following. i. Admission of members and review of membership. ii. Appointment, removal and regulating the services of the Chief Executive and other employees iii. To place annual report, financial statement, annual plan, budget, audit report and compliance report before the general body. iv. To raise funds and to approve annual and supplementary budget v. To sanction loans to the members etc. 81 82

CHAPTER - 25 ELECTIONS [Sections 41, 44, 45 and 46] 25.1 Election of Board of directors It is the responsibility of the existing board to conduct elections. Election of members of the board is held by secret ballot in the general meeting of the members. Where the board fails to conduct elections, the Central Registrar has to hold elections within a period of ninety days from the date when such elections became due. The expenses for holding elections have to be borne by the said society. The elected members are eligible for re-election if the Bye-laws permit. The elected members will in turn elect their Chairman / Vice-Chairman / Other Office Bearers in the meeting convened by the Election Officer. The Central Government prescribed the procedure for the conduct of election to the board of societies in the schedule appended to the Rules. 25.2 Prohibition to hold office of the Chairperson or President or Vice- Chairperson or Vice-President i. A Minister in Central Government or State Government is not eligible to be elected as Chairperson or President or Vice- Chairperson or Vice-President of a Society. ii. No member is eligible to be elected as Chairperson/ President of the society after he has held such office for two consecutive terms, full or part. iii. No person is eligible to hold the office of President or Chairperson or Vice President or Vice -Chairperson on board for more than two Societies at the same time. CHAPTER –26 REGULATION (Audit, Inquiry, Inspection and Repayment) (Sections 70 to 77, 78, 81, 82 ,83, 79, 80 81,83) 26.1 Audit Every Society has to be audited by an auditor appointed in its general meeting and he will hold such office from the conclusion of such general meeting until the conclusion of next general meeting. A person is not qualified to be appointed as an auditor of the society unless he is a charted accountant within the meaning of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949. The appointment of Auditor may be from the approved panel by the Central Registrar or from the panel prepared by the Society. Every auditor appointed by the society has to inform within thirty days of the receipt of intimation of his appointment to the Central Registrar in writing that he has accepted or refused to accept the appointment. Where no auditor is appointed at an annual general meeting, the Central Registrar may appoint a person as an auditor of the society. If any auditor resigns then the vacancy is to be filled by the general meeting of the society. An auditor can be removed by the society before expiry of his term in the general meeting. The remuneration of the auditors will be fixed by the society in its general meeting or the Central Registrar as the case may be. The report of the auditor will be laid before the society in its general meeting. Where the Central Government is of the opinion that the affairs of the society are not being managed properly, may order for special audit by appointing chartered accountant or the auditor of the society. However, Central Government cannot order for special audit, unless the Central Government or State Government by itself or both hold more than fifty one percent of the paid up share capital. 83 84

26.2 Inquiry At the request of the Federal Society to which the Society is affiliated, or a creditor or not less than one third of the members of the board or one fifth of the total number of members of the society, the Central Registrar may hold an inquiry or direct some person authorized by him by order in writing to hold an inquiry into the constitution, working and financial condition of the Society. No Inquiry can be held unless a notice of not less than fifteen days is given to the society. An enquiry officer can have free access to the books, accounts, documents etc at all reasonable times. He can require the officials of the society to call the general meeting at not less than 7 days notice and if they fail to convene such meeting, he himself can convene the general meeting. On receipt of the inquiry report, the Central Registrar has to communicate the said report within three months to the society, to the financial institutions, if any, to which the Society is affiliated and to the person or authority at whose instance the inquiry is made. 26.3 Inspection At the request of the Federal Society to which the Society is affiliated, or a creditor or not less than one third of the members of the board or not less than one fifth of the total number of members of the society, the Central Registrar may hold an Inspection or direct some person authorized by him by order in writing to make an Inspection into the constitution, working and financial condition of a Society. An Inspecting Officer can have free access to the books, accounts, and documents etc at all reasonable times. He may convene board meeting and if necessary the general meeting subject to the general or special order from the Central Registrar. No Inspection shall be made unless a notice of not less than fifteen days given to the society. Copy of the Inspection report is to be communicated to the society within a period of three months from the date of completion of such Inspection. 26.4 Apportionment of cost of Inquiry and Inspection Where Inquiry and Inspection are held, the Central Registrar may apportion the costs or such part of the costs as he think fit between the Society, the members or creditors demanding an Inquiry or Inspection, and the officers or former officers and the members and former members. Such costs may be recovered by filing an application before a Magistrate having jurisdiction. 26.5 Repayment If in the course of an Audit, Inquiry, Inspection or winding up of a society, it is found any person who is entrusted with the organization or management of such society or who is or has at any time been an officer or an employee of the society, had made an irregular payment, or caused any deficiency to the assets of the society by breach of trust or willful negligence, or misappropriated the funds of the society, the Central Registrar may of his own motion or on the application of the board, liquidator or any creditor inquire himself or direct any person authorised by him, by order in writing to inquire in to the conduct of such person within a period of two years from the date of receipt of audit, Inquiry, Inspection reports or the date of the order of winding up as the case may be. However if the Central Registrar is satisfied that such inquiry could not be commenced during the period of two years and the period can be extended to the maximum period of six years. After the inquiry is made the Central Registrar may after giving a reasonable opportunity of being heard to the concerned, make an order requiring him to repay or restore the money or property or any part thereof with interest or pay contribution and costs or compensation to such extent as the Central Registrar may consider just and equitable. 85 86

CHAPTER – 27 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES [Sections 84 and 85 and Rule 30] 27.1 Scope The Central Registrar concerned or the person appointed by him in this behalf is the Arbitrator for deciding any dispute (other than a dispute regarding disciplinary action taken by the society against its paid employee or industrial dispute defined in Industrial Dispute Act, 1947) touching the constitution management or business of a Society. If not otherwise provided in the Act, the provisions of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 will apply to all arbitrations as if the proceedings for arbitration were referred for settlement or decision under the provisions of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. 27.2 Parties to dispute Any dispute arising among the following parties shall be referred to arbitration. i. among members, past members and persons claiming through members, past members and deceased members, or ii. between a member, past member and persons claiming through past member and deceased member and the Society , its board or any officer, agent or employee of Society or liquidator, past or present, or iii. between the Society or its board and any past board, any officer, agent or employee, or any past officer, past agent or past employee, heirs or legal representatives of any deceased officer, deceased agent, or deceased employee of the Society, or iv. between the Society and any other Society, between a Society and liquidator of another Society or between a liquidator of one Society and liquidator of another Society Powers of the Central Registrar in this regard have been delegated to the State Registrars/Union Territories for appointment of Arbitrators. The Central Registrar concerned fixes the fee as per the provisions of Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. 27.3 Limitation The period of limitation in case of any dispute which are required to be referred to arbitration shall be regulated under provisions of Limitation Act, 1963 except in the following cases. i. When disputes relates to recovery of any sum including interest thereon due to a Society by a member be commuted from the date on which such member dies or ceases to be a member of the society. ii. When a dispute relates to any act or omission on the part of any of the parties to the Arbitration mentioned in para (ii), (iii),(iv) of para 27.2 above, the limitation will be six years from the date on which the act or omission, with reference to which the dispute arose took place. iii. When the dispute is in respect of an election of a society, the limitation will be one month from the date of the declaration of the result of the election. The Arbitrator may admit a dispute after the expiration of limitation, if the applicant satisfies the Arbitrator that he had sufficient cause for not referring the dispute within such period. 87 88

CHAPTER – 28 EXECUTION [Sections 94 to 98] 28.1 Execution of decisions etc., Every decision or order passed against any violations in convening of annual general meetings, special general meetings; orders passed for repayment of amounts, Orders issued under appeals and reviews duly certified by the Central Registrar or any person authorized by him in writing will be deemed to be a decree of Civil Court and shall be executed in the same manner as if it were a decree of such court and such decrees shall be executed by the Central Registrar or any person authorized by him in writing in this behalf by attachment and sale or sale without attachment of any property of the persons or a Society against whom the decision or orders have been made. Where the decision or order provides for recovery of money and every order made by the Liquidator, will be recovered as arrears of land revenue. 28.2 Attachment before award If the Arbitrator is satisfied that a party to the Arbitration is about to dispose of the whole or any part of the property or remove the whole or any part of the property from its existing precincts, he may direct conditional attachment of the said property or such part thereof as he deems necessary unless adequate security is furnished. 28.3 Recovery of sums due to Government All the sums due from the Society or from an officer or from a member or past member of the Society to the Central Government or State Government including any cost awarded to the Central Government or to the State Government under any provisions of this Act may on a Certificate issued by the Central Registrar, be recovered in the same manner as arrears of land revenue as first charge on the assets of such society or officer or member or as the case may be. Sums due from a society to the Central Government or to a State Government and recoverable as stated in the above para may be recovered firstly from the property of the society and secondly from the members, past members or estates of the deceased members subject to the limit of their liability. 89 90

CHAPTER - 29 WINDING UP [Sections 86 to 93, Rule 28] 29.1 Introduction If the Central Registrar on the basis of Audit, Special Audit, Inquiry and Inspection is of the opinion that the society is to be wound up, he may, after giving an opportunity of making its representations, direct it, to be wound up. The Central Registrar may also order for winding up of the society if minimum number of members falls below the statutory number i.e. 50 after giving an opportunity of being heard or where the society has not commenced its work within the period of six months of the date of its registration or such extended period as Central Registrar may allow or if the society by a resolution passed by two thirds majority of members present and voting in the general body decides for winding of the society. No Cooperative bank can be wound up except with the previous sanction of the Reserve Bank in writing. The Central Registrar may cancel an order for winding up of a society at any time in any case where in his opinion the society should continue to exist 29.2 Appointment of Liquidator Where the Central Registrar has made an order for the winding up of a society, he may appoint a liquidator for the purpose and fixes his remuneration. On appointment, the Liquidator has to take into his custody or under his control all the properties, effects and actionable claims to which the society is or appears to be entitled. He has to take necessary steps to prevent loss or deterioration to such property, effects and claims. He may carry on the business of the society so far as may be necessary with the previous approval of the Central Registrar. Where an order for the winding up of the society is set aside in appeal, the properties, effects and actionable claims of the society will re-vest in the society. 29.3 Powers of liquidator All the assets of a Society ordered for winding up will vest in the Liquidator from the date on which the order takes effect and the Liquidator will have power to realize such assets by sale or otherwise. He will exercise his powers subject to the control of the Central Registrar. When the affairs of the society have been wound up, the Liquidator will make a report to the Central Registrar and deposit the records of the society in a place decided by the Central Registrar . 29.4 Cancellation of Registration of multi-State Co-operative Society The Central Registrar may after considering the report of the Liquidator, order the registration of the society to be cancelled. On such cancellation, the society will stand dissolved. Such order has to be communicated by registered post to the President or the Chairperson as the case may be, of the society and to the financial institutions, if any, of which the society was a member. All the books and records of a society, whose registration has been cancelled, may be destroyed by the Central Registrar after the expiry of three years from the date of the order cancelling the registration of the society. 91 92

CHAPTER - 30 APPEAL AND REVIEW [Sections 99 to 101 and Rule 32] 30.1 Appeal An appeal will lie against (a) an order made by the Central Registrar refusing to register a Society, (b) refusing to register an amendment to the bye-laws, (c) refusal by the society to admit any person as a member of the society, (d) apportioning the costs of an inquiry and inspection,(e) order of repayment (f) orders of winding up of the society (g) order of the liquidator. An appeal against any of the above decisions or orders has to be made within 60 days from the date of such decisions or orders before the authority mentioned below. However, the Appellate Authority if satisfied of the reasons for delay may admit the appeal within such further period as it may deem fit. An Appeal is to be made in the form of a memorandum and to be accompanied by the original or certified copies of the orders appealed against. No appeal lies on winding up proceedings of a cooperative bank, a scheme of amalgamation or reorganization is given effect to with the previous sanction of R.B.I. and the sanction or requisition of the R.B.I. in this regard. 30.2 Review The Appellate Authority on the application of any party may review its own order and pass such order as it thinks fit. An application in this regard has to be made within 30 days from the date of communication of the orders of the Appellate Authority sought to be reviewed. However, no application in this regard can be entertained unless the Appellate Authority is satisfied that there has been a discovery of new and important matter or evidence which after exercise of due diligence was not within the knowledge of the applicant or could not be produced by him at the time the order was made or that there has been some mistake or error apparent on the face of the record or for any other sufficient reason. No order on the review can be made unless notice has been given to all the interested parties and they have been afforded a reasonable opportunity of being heard. Sl.No. Order Appellate Authority 1. Where the order of decision was made by the Central Registrar. The Secretary to the Government of India in the Dept. of Agriculture and Co- operation. 2. Where the decision or order is made by any officer of the Central Government or of the State Government of the rank of Registrar on whom powers of the Central Registrar have been conferred. The Additional Secretary to the Government of India in the Dept. of Agriculture and Co-operation. 3. Where the order or decision has been made by any other officer of the State Govt. on whom powers of Central Registrar have been conferred. Chief Director (Co-operation) in the Ministry or Agriculture and Co-operation. 93 94

CHAPTER –31 OFFENCES AND PENALTIES [Sections 104 and 105] 31.1 A Society or an officer or a member wilfully making a false return or furnishing false information or any person wilfully or without any reasonable excuse disobeying any summons, requisition or lawful written order issued under the provisions of this Act or wilfully not furnishes any information required from him by a person authorized in this behalf under the provisions of this Act shall be punishable with a fine which shall not be less than Rs.2000/- and which may extend to Rs.10,000/- . 31.2 Any employer who without sufficient cause fails to pay to a Society the amount deducted by him from the salary of an employee of the society within a period of fourteen days from the date on which such deduction is made, is punishable with fine which may extend to Rs.5,000/- without prejudice to any action that may be taken against him under any other law. 31.3 Any officer or custodian who wilfully fails to hand over custody of books, accounts, documents, records, cash, security and other property belonging to a society of which he is an officer or custodian, to a person entitled under the provisions of this Act shall be punishable with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees and in the case of a continuing breach, with a further fine which may extend to five thousand rupees for every day during which the breach is continued after conviction for the first such breach. 31.4 Whoever, before, during or after the election of delegates or election of members of the board commits any fraudulent acts detailed in the Act are punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months or with fine or with both. No Court inferior to that of a Metropolitan Magistrate or the first class can try any of the above offences. No prosecution for the said offences can be instituted except on a complaint filed in writing by a member of a society or by a Central Registrar in the competent Court.
